<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL508 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** ALP Programming for Data Transfer (Block Data Movement, Exchanging Data, Sorting, Finding Largest Element in an Array) and Arithmetic Operations (Addition, Subtraction, Multiplication, Division, and Comparing Square and Cube of 16-bit Numbers)

# ALP Programming for Data Transfer & Arithmetic Operations (8086)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)
**Assembly Language Programming (ALP)** is a low-level, mnemonic-based representation of machine instructions for a specific microprocessor (in this lab, the **Intel 8086**), where each statement corresponds to exactly one machine code instruction. The **Module Experiment 2** specifically tests the student's ability to write, assemble (using MASM/TASM), and execute ALP code for two broad categories of operations:

1. **Data Transfer Operations** — Instructions that move, copy, exchange, sort, or search data within memory and CPU registers without altering the data's intrinsic value (with the exception of flags, which may be affected by certain instructions like `MOVS`).
2. **Arithmetic Operations** — Instructions that perform mathematical computations (Addition, Subtraction, Multiplication, Division) and execute conditional logic based on numerical comparison.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **The "Pigeon-Hole" Analogy for 8086 Registers**
> Think of the **8086 CPU** as a busy post-office clerk's desk. The desk has a small set of **pigeon holes (registers AX, BX, CX, DX, SI, DI, BP, SP)** that the clerk can instantly reach into. The **storage room (RAM memory)** is far away — every time the clerk wants something, he has to walk over, fetch a parcel, and bring it back to his desk. 
> 
> - **Data Transfer Instructions** (`MOV`, `LODS`, `STOS`, `MOVS`) are the clerk *physically carrying* parcels between the desk and the storage room.
> - **Arithmetic Instructions** (`ADD`, `SUB`, `MUL`, `DIV`) are the clerk *opening the parcels on his desk, performing some math, and either repacking or creating a new parcel*.
> 
> This is *literally* what ALP does — it tells the CPU **where to fetch**, **what to do with it**, and **where to put the result**.

### Key Hardware Context for This Lab
- **Processor:** Intel 8086 / 8088 (16-bit data bus, 20-bit address bus, **1 MB** addressable memory).
- **Assembler:** Microsoft **MASM 6.15** or Borland **TASM** running under DOSBox.
- **Debugger:** Turbo Debugger (TD) — used for single-stepping and register inspection.
- **Data Size:** **16-bit (WORD)** is the default operand size; 8-bit (BYTE) operations are also supported.

### Syllabus Highlight Callouts
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Expected Outcomes (CO Mapped)**
> After completing this experiment, the student must be able to:
> - **CO2:** Write and execute ALP programs for block data transfer and arithmetic operations on the 8086 kit / emulator.
> - Apply the concept of **looping with counters (`CX`)**, **indexed addressing using `SI` and `DI`**, and the **direction flag (`DF`)** for string operations.
> - Debug programs using single-stepping and breakpoint features of the debugger.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 8086 Register Set (Quick Recall)

| Register | Full Name | Special Purpose in This Experiment |
|----------|-----------|------------------------------------|
| **AX** | Accumulator | Primary register for `MUL`, `DIV` operands |
| **BX** | Base Register | Used in indexed addressing `[BX+SI]` |
| **CX** | Counter Register | Default loop counter (`LOOP` instruction) |
| **DX** | Data Register | Holds high word of `MUL`/`DIV` result |
| **SI** | Source Index | Points to source in string operations |
| **DI** | Destination Index | Points to destination in string operations |
| **DS** | Data Segment | Base for `SI` addressing |
| **ES** | Extra Segment | Base for `DI` addressing |
| **CS** | Code Segment | Holds the program code |
| **SS** | Stack Segment | Holds the stack |
| **IP** | Instruction Pointer | Points to next instruction |
| **FLAGS** | Flag Register | Holds status flags (CF, ZF, SF, OF, PF, AF) |

## 2.2 Addressing Modes Used in This Experiment

| Mode | Syntax Example | Used For |
|------|----------------|----------|
| **Register direct** | `MOV AX, BX` | Register-to-register copy |
| **Immediate** | `MOV CX, 0005H` | Loading constants |
| **Direct (memory)** | `MOV AL, [2000H]` | Accessing fixed memory location |
| **Register indirect** | `MOV AL, [SI]` | Array traversal |
| **Based indexed** | `MOV AL, [BX+SI]` | Offset addressing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The `[ ]` brackets in 8086 ALP mean "contents of the memory address", NOT an array index.** This is one of the **top student errors** in KTU lab exams.

## 2.3 Data Transfer Instructions — Complete Cheat Sheet

| Mnemonic | Operation | Affected Flags | Example |
|----------|-----------|----------------|---------|
| `MOV dest, src` | Copy `src` to `dest` | None | `MOV AX, BX` |
| `XCHG op1, op2` | Swap `op1` and `op2` | None | `XCHG AX, BX` |
| `LODS` / `STOS` | Load / Store string element | None | `LODSB` |
| `MOVS` / `MOVSB` / `MOVSW` | Move string element | None | `MOVSB` |
| `LEA reg, mem` | Load effective address | None | `LEA SI, ARR` |
| `PUSH src` | Push to stack | None | `PUSH AX` |
| `POP dest` | Pop from stack | None | `POP BX` |
| `IN/OUT` | I/O port read/write | None | `IN AL, 60H` |

## 2.4 Arithmetic Instructions — Complete Cheat Sheet

| Mnemonic | Operation | Result Size | Example |
|----------|-----------|-------------|---------|
| `ADD dest, src` | Addition | Same as operands | `ADD AX, BX` |
| `ADC dest, src` | Add with carry | Same as operands | `ADC DX, 0` |
| `SUB dest, src` | Subtraction | Same as operands | `SUB AX, BX` |
| `SBB dest, src` | Subtract with borrow | Same as operands | `SBB DX, 0` |
| `MUL src` | Unsigned multiply | `AX × src` → `DX:AX` | `MUL BX` |
| `IMUL src` | Signed multiply | `AX × src` → `DX:AX` | `IMUL BX` |
| `DIV src` | Unsigned divide | `DX:AX ÷ src` → `AX` (quot), `DX` (rem) | `DIV BX` |
| `IDIV src` | Signed divide | Same as `DIV` | `IDIV BX` |
| `CMP op1, op2` | Compare (subtract without storing) | Sets flags | `CMP AX, BX` |
| `INC dest` | Increment by 1 | All except CF | `INC CX` |
| `DEC dest` | Decrement by 1 | All except CF | `DEC CX` |
| `NEG dest` | Two's complement negate | All flags | `NEG AX` |

## 2.5 Conditional Jump Instructions (Used After `CMP`)

| Instruction | Jump Condition | Typical Use |
|-------------|----------------|-------------|
| `JE / JZ` | Jump if Equal / Zero | Equality check |
| `JNE / JNZ` | Jump if Not Equal | Inequality |
| `JG / JNLE` | Greater (signed) | Compare Square vs Cube |
| `JL / JNGE` | Less (signed) | Compare Square vs Cube |
| `JA / JNBE` | Above (unsigned) | Array sorting |
| `JB / JNAE` | Below (unsigned) | Array sorting |
| `JC` | Jump if Carry | Multi-word subtraction |
| `JNC` | Jump if No Carry | Multi-word addition |
| `LOOP label` | Decrement CX, jump if CX≠0 | Array traversal |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Data movement** routines form the core of **DMA controllers**, **memory copy engines**, and **buffer management** in OS kernels (e.g., `memcpy()` in C is essentially a 32/64-bit extension of `MOVS`).
- **Sorting algorithms** in ALP are the bedrock of **embedded signal processing** where resources are limited and no high-level language is available.
- **Multi-word arithmetic** is essential in **cryptographic processors**, **DSP chips**, and **financial calculation engines** where 64-bit or 128-bit numbers exceed native CPU word size.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step ALP Programs & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Program 1 — Block Data Movement (Memory Block Copy)

**Problem Statement:** Copy a block of **N bytes** from source memory block `SRC` to destination block `DST` using 8086 ALP.

### Algorithm Steps
1. Initialize the data segment registers (`DS`, `ES`).
2. Load the count `N` into `CX`.
3. Point `SI` to source array and `DI` to destination array.
4. Use the `MOVSB` instruction inside a `LOOP` to transfer one byte per iteration.
5. Stop when `CX = 0`.

### Complete ALP Code

```asm
; PROGRAM 1: BLOCK DATA MOVEMENT (BYTE COPY)
ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA, ES:EXTRA

DATA    SEGMENT
SRC     DB 10H, 20H, 30H, 40H, 50H      ; Source block
N       DW 05H                          ; Number of bytes
DATA    ENDS

EXTRA   SEGMENT
DST     DB 5 DUP(?)                     ; Destination (uninitialized)
EXTRA   ENDS

CODE    SEGMENT
START:  MOV AX, DATA                    ; Initialize DS
        MOV DS, AX
        MOV AX, EXTRA                   ; Initialize ES
        MOV ES, AX

        LEA SI, SRC                     ; SI -> source
        LEA DI, DST                     ; DI -> destination
        MOV CX, N                       ; Counter = N

        CLD                             ; Clear direction flag (auto-increment)
        REP MOVSB                       ; Repeat MOVSB CX times

        MOV AH, 4CH                     ; Terminate program
        INT 21H
CODE    ENDS
END     START
```

### Step-by-Step Execution Trace

| Iteration | CX (before) | SI (hex) | DI (hex) | Byte Copied |
|-----------|-------------|----------|----------|-------------|
| 1 | 0005H | 0000H | 0000H | 10H → DST[0] |
| 2 | 0004H | 0001H | 0001H | 20H → DST[1] |
| 3 | 0003H | 0002H | 0002H | 30H → DST[2] |
| 4 | 0002H | 0003H | 0003H | 40H → DST[3] |
| 5 | 0001H | 0004H | 0004H | 50H → DST[4] |
| Exit | 0000H | 0005H | 0005H | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why `CLD`?** `CLD` clears the **Direction Flag (DF)** so that `SI` and `DI` *auto-increment* after every `MOVSB`. The opposite (`STD`) would auto-decrement, used for overlapping memory copies.

---

## 3.2 Program 2 — Exchanging Data (Swap Two Memory Blocks)

**Problem Statement:** Exchange the contents of two equal-sized memory blocks `BLK1` and `BLK2` using 8086 ALP.

### Algorithm Steps
1. Load count `N` into `CX`.
2. Point `SI` to `BLK1` and `DI` to `BLK2`.
3. In each iteration, use `XCHG [SI], AL` after `LODSB` and `STOSB` — or simpler, swap using a register buffer.
4. Loop until `CX = 0`.

### Complete ALP Code

```asm
; PROGRAM 2: EXCHANGING TWO DATA BLOCKS
ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA

DATA    SEGMENT
BLK1    DB 11H, 22H, 33H, 44H, 55H
BLK2    DB 99H, 88H, 77H, 66H, 55H
N       DW 0005H
DATA    ENDS

CODE    SEGMENT
START:  MOV AX, DATA
        MOV DS, AX

        LEA SI, BLK1
        LEA DI, BLK2
        MOV CX, N

NEXT:   MOV AL, [SI]                    ; AL <- BLK1[i]
        MOV BL, [DI]                    ; BL <- BLK2[i]
        MOV [SI], BL                    ; BLK1[i] <- BL2[i]
        MOV [DI], AL                    ; BLK2[i] <- BL1[i]
        INC SI
        INC DI
        LOOP NEXT

        MOV AH, 4CH
        INT 21H
CODE    ENDS
END     START
```

### Trace Table

| Iteration | BLK1 (SI) | BLK2 (DI) | AL | BL | After swap BLK1 | After swap BLK2 |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|----|----|-----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | 11H | 99H | 11H | 99H | 99H | 11H |
| 2 | 22H | 88H | 22H | 88H | 88H | 22H |
| 3 | 33H | 77H | 33H | 77H | 77H | 33H |
| 4 | 44H | 66H | 44H | 66H | 66H | 44H |
| 5 | 55H | 55H | 55H | 55H | 55H | 55H |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not `XCHG [SI], [DI]` directly?** The 8086 `XCHG` instruction **does not support memory-to-memory operands**. A register buffer (`AL`, `BL`) is mandatory — a common KTU exam question.

---

## 3.3 Program 3 — Sorting an Array in Ascending Order (Bubble Sort)

**Problem Statement:** Sort an array of **N unsigned bytes** in ascending order using 8086 ALP.

### Algorithm Steps (Bubble Sort)
1. Use **two nested loops**: outer pass counter and inner comparison counter.
2. Compare adjacent elements `ARR[J]` and `ARR[J+1]`.
3. If `ARR[J] > ARR[J+1]`, swap them.
4. Repeat passes until no swaps occur (or simply N−1 passes).

### Complete ALP Code

```asm
; PROGRAM 3: ASCENDING SORT (BUBBLE SORT)
ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA

DATA    SEGMENT
ARR     DB 05H, 02H, 08H, 01H, 04H    ; Unsorted array
N       DW 0005H
DATA    ENDS

CODE    SEGMENT
START:  MOV AX, DATA
        MOV DS, AX

        MOV DX, N                       ; DX = total passes = N-1
        DEC DX
        OUTER: MOV CX, DX               ; Inner loop count
               LEA SI, ARR              ; Reset pointer to start
               INNER: MOV AL, [SI]
                      MOV BL, [SI+1]
                      CMP AL, BL
                      JBE NOSWAP        ; If AL <= BL, no swap
                      MOV [SI], BL      ; else swap
                      MOV [SI+1], AL
                      NOSWAP: INC SI
               LOOP INNER
               DEC DX
               JNZ OUTER

        MOV AH, 4CH
        INT 21H
CODE    ENDS
END     START
```

### Trace of Pass 1 on Sample Data

| Step | SI | AL (ARR[J]) | BL (ARR[J+1]) | CMP Result | Action | Array State |
|------|----|--------|-----|------------|--------|-------------|
| 1 | 0 | 05H | 02H | 05H > 02H | Swap | 02, 05, 08, 01, 04 |
| 2 | 1 | 05H | 08H | 05H < 08H | No swap | 02, 05, 08, 01, 04 |
| 3 | 2 | 08H | 01H | 08H > 01H | Swap | 02, 05, 01, 08, 04 |
| 4 | 3 | 08H | 04H | 08H > 04H | Swap | 02, 05, 01, 04, 08 |

**Final Sorted Array:** `02H, 05H, 01H, 04H, 08H` → after all passes → `01H, 02H, 04H, 05H, 08H` ✓

---

## 3.4 Program 4 — Finding the Largest Element in an Array

**Problem Statement:** Find the **largest unsigned byte** in an array of N elements using 8086 ALP.

### Algorithm Steps
1. Load count `N` into `CX`, point `SI` to the first element.
2. Load the first element as the *current maximum* into `AL`.
3. For each subsequent element, compare it with `AL`. If greater, update `AL`.
4. After the loop, `AL` holds the maximum.

### Complete ALP Code

```asm
; PROGRAM 4: LARGEST ELEMENT IN AN ARRAY
ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA

DATA    SEGMENT
ARR     DB 30H, 80H, 10H, 70H, 50H, FFH, 20H
N       DW 0007H
RESULT  DB ?                            ; To store the maximum
DATA    ENDS

CODE    SEGMENT
START:  MOV AX, DATA
        MOV DS, AX

        LEA SI, ARR
        MOV CX, N
        DEC CX                          ; Compare N-1 remaining elements
        MOV AL, [SI]                    ; AL = ARR[0] (initial max)
        INC SI

NEXT:   CMP AL, [SI]
        JAE SKIP                        ; If AL >= ARR[i], skip
        MOV AL, [SI]                    ; Else update max
        SKIP: INC SI
        LOOP NEXT

        MOV RESULT, AL                  ; Store final maximum

        MOV AH, 4CH
        INT 21H
CODE    ENDS
END     START
```

### Trace Table

| Iteration | SI (offset) | ARR[SI] | AL (current max) | Action |
|-----------|-------------|---------|------------------|--------|
| Init | 0 | 30H | 30H | AL = 30H (first) |
| 1 | 1 | 80H | 80H | 80H > 30H → update |
| 2 | 2 | 10H | 80H | 10H < 80H → skip |
| 3 | 3 | 70H | 80H | 70H < 80H → skip |
| 4 | 4 | 50H | 80H | 50H < 80H → skip |
| 5 | 5 | FFH | FFH | FFH > 80H → update |
| 6 | 6 | 20H | FFH | 20H < FFH → skip |

**Result:** `AL = FFH` stored at `RESULT` ✓

---

## 3.5 Program 5 — 16-bit Addition (Multi-Word / Multi-Operand)

**Problem Statement:** Add two 16-bit numbers and store the 16-bit sum (ignoring any carry for simplicity, with an extension shown for multi-word addition).

### Complete ALP Code

```asm
; PROGRAM 5: 16-BIT ADDITION
ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA

DATA    SEGMENT
NUM1    DW 1234H
NUM2    DW 0ABCDH
SUM     DW ?
CARRY   DB ?
DATA    ENDS

CODE    SEGMENT
START:  MOV AX, DATA
        MOV DS, AX

        MOV AX, NUM1                    ; AX = 1234H
        ADD AX, NUM2                    ; AX = 1234H + ABCDH = BE01H
        JNC STORE                       ; If no carry, skip
        INC CARRY                       ; Record carry
        STORE: MOV SUM, AX

        MOV AH, 4CH
        INT 21H
CODE    ENDS
END     START
```

### Binary-Level Verification

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{1234H} &= 0001\,0010\,0011\,0100_2 \\
\text{0ABCDH} &= 1010\,1011\,1100\,1101_2 \\
\text{1234H + ABCDH} &= 1011\,1110\,0000\,0001_2 = \text{BE01H}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.6 Program 6 — 16-bit Subtraction

**Problem Statement:** Subtract two 16-bit numbers and store the result and the borrow flag.

### Complete ALP Code

```asm
; PROGRAM 6: 16-BIT SUBTRACTION
ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA

DATA    SEGMENT
NUM1    DW 8000H
NUM2    DW 1234H
DIFF    DW ?
BORROW  DB ?
DATA    ENDS

CODE    SEGMENT
START:  MOV AX, DATA
        MOV DS, AX

        MOV AX, NUM1                    ; AX = 8000H
        SUB AX, NUM2                    ; AX = 8000H - 1234H = 6DCCH
        JNC STORE                       ; If no borrow, CF = 0
        INC BORROW                      ; CF = 1, record borrow
        STORE: MOV DIFF, AX

        MOV AH, 4CH
        INT 21H
CODE    ENDS
END     START
```

$$
\text{8000H} - \text{1234H} = \text{6DCCH} \quad \text{(no borrow, CF} = 0\text{)}
$$

---

## 3.7 Program 7 — 16-bit Unsigned Multiplication

**Problem Statement:** Multiply two 16-bit unsigned numbers. The result is **32 bits** stored in `DX:AX`.

### Complete ALP Code

```asm
; PROGRAM 7: 16-BIT UNSIGNED MULTIPLICATION
ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA

DATA    SEGMENT
MUL1    DW 0FFFFH                       ; 65535
MUL2    DW 0002H                        ; 2
PROD_LO DW ?                            ; AX (low word of result)
PROD_HI DW ?                            ; DX (high word of result)
DATA    ENDS

CODE    SEGMENT
START:  MOV AX, DATA
        MOV DS, AX

        MOV AX, MUL1                    ; AX = 0FFFFH
        MUL MUL2                        ; Unsigned multiply
                                        ; DX:AX = 0FFFFH * 2 = 0001FFFEH
        MOV PROD_LO, AX
        MOV PROD_HI, DX

        MOV AH, 4CH
        INT 21H
CODE    ENDS
END     START
```

### Analytical Calculation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Result} &= \text{AX} \times \text{MUL2} \\
&= \text{FFFFH} \times 0002\text{H} \\
&= 65535 \times 2 \\
&= 131070_{10} \\
&= 0\text{0001FFFEH} \\
\Rightarrow \text{DX:AX} &= \text{0001H : FFFEH}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The `MUL` instruction **always** uses `AX` (for 16-bit operands) as the implicit multiplicand, and stores the 32-bit result across `DX` (high) and `AX` (low).

---

## 3.8 Program 8 — 16-bit Unsigned Division

**Problem Statement:** Divide a 32-bit dividend by a 16-bit divisor. The **16-bit quotient** goes to `AX` and the **16-bit remainder** goes to `DX`.

### Complete ALP Code

```asm
; PROGRAM 8: 16-BIT UNSIGNED DIVISION
ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA

DATA    SEGMENT
DIVD_HI DW 0001H                        ; High word of dividend (DX)
DIVD_LO DW 0FFFEH                       ; Low word of dividend (AX)
DIVSOR  DW 0002H                        ; Divisor
QUOT    DW ?
REM     DW ?
DATA    ENDS

CODE    SEGMENT
START:  MOV AX, DATA
        MOV DS, AX

        MOV DX, DIVD_HI                 ; DX = high word
        MOV AX, DIVD_LO                 ; AX = low word
        DIV DIVSOR                      ; DX:AX / 0002H
                                        ; Quotient in AX, Remainder in DX
        MOV QUOT, AX
        MOV REM, DX

        MOV AH, 4CH
        INT 21H
CODE    ENDS
END     START
```

### Analytical Calculation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Dividend (DX:AX)} &= 0\text{0001FFFEH} = 131070_{10} \\
\text{Divisor} &= 0002\text{H} = 2_{10} \\
\text{Quotient} &= 131070 \div 2 = 65535_{10} = \text{FFFFH} \rightarrow \text{AX} \\
\text{Remainder} &= 131070 \bmod 2 = 0 \rightarrow \text{DX}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Divide Error Exception (INT 0):** If the divisor is **0** OR the quotient does not fit in `AX` (i.e., divide overflow), the 8086 automatically generates a **Type 0 interrupt**, halting the program. Always validate the divisor before `DIV`.

---

## 3.9 Program 9 — Compare Square and Cube of a 16-bit Number

**Problem Statement:** Read a 16-bit number `N`. Compute `N²` and `N³`. Compare them: if `N² > N³` print "Square greater", else print "Cube greater" (for N ≥ 2, cube is always greater, so the test is more meaningful for `N = 0, 1` or in modular arithmetic — the program framework is what's being tested).

### Complete ALP Code

```asm
; PROGRAM 9: COMPARE SQUARE AND CUBE OF A 16-BIT NUMBER
ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA

DATA    SEGMENT
N       DW 0004H                        ; Input number
SQ_LO   DW ?                            ; N^2 low word  (AX)
SQ_HI   DW ?                            ; N^2 high word (DX)
CB_LO   DW ?                            ; N^3 low word
CB_HI   DW ?                            ; N^3 high word
MSG1    DB 'CUBE IS GREATER OR EQUAL$'
MSG2    DB 'SQUARE IS GREATER$'
DATA    ENDS

CODE    SEGMENT
START:  MOV AX, DATA
        MOV DS, AX

        ; ---- Compute N^2 ----
        MOV AX, N
        MUL N                           ; DX:AX = N * N
        MOV SQ_LO, AX
        MOV SQ_HI, DX

        ; ---- Compute N^3 = (N^2) * N ----
        ; Multiply the 32-bit square by 16-bit N
        ; Approach: take N^2 (32-bit) and split multiply
        ;   (N^2_high * 2^16 + N^2_low) * N
        ; We use the trick: load DX:AX with N^2 and do MUL
        ; But MUL is 16-bit; we need 32x16 = 48-bit
        ; For simplicity here, assume N^2 fits in 16 bits
        MOV AX, SQ_LO
        MUL N                           ; AX = N^2 * N (low 32 bits of N^3)
        MOV CB_LO, AX
        MOV CB_HI, DX

        ; ---- Compare: N^3 vs N^2 ----
        ; Compare high words first, then low words
        MOV AX, CB_HI
        CMP AX, SQ_HI
        JA  CUBE_BIG                    ; If cube high > square high
        JB  SQ_BIG                      ; If cube high < square high
        ; High words equal, compare lows
        MOV AX, CB_LO
        CMP AX, SQ_LO
        JAE CUBE_BIG                    ; Cube >= Square
        JA  CUBE_BIG
        JMP SQ_BIG

        CUBE_BIG: LEA DX, MSG1
                  MOV AH, 09H
                  INT 21H
                  JMP END_PRG

        SQ_BIG:  LEA DX, MSG2
                 MOV AH, 09H
                 INT 21H

        END_PRG: MOV AH, 4CH
                 INT 21H
CODE    ENDS
END     START
```

### Verification with N = 4

$$
\begin{aligned}
N^2 &= 4 \times 4 = 16 = 10\text{H} \\
N^3 &= 4 \times 4 \times 4 = 64 = 40\text{H} \\
\text{Result} &= \text{N}^3 > \text{N}^2 \Rightarrow \text{"CUBE IS GREATER OR EQUAL"}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Multi-Word Multiplication Strategy (For Larger N)

$$
(N^2)_{\text{32-bit}} \times N_{\text{16-bit}} = (N^2_{\text{hi}} \times 2^{16} + N^2_{\text{lo}}) \times N
$$

$$
= (N^2_{\text{hi}} \times N) \times 2^{16} + (N^2_{\text{lo}} \times N)
$$

This is implemented using two `MUL` instructions and combining the 32-bit partial results — **a classic KTU 14-mark question**.

---

## 3.10 Master Comparative Table (For Quick Lab Viva)

| Program | Key Instructions | Critical Register | Result Location |
|---------|------------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| Block Move | `MOVSB`, `REP`, `CLD` | `CX` (counter) | Memory block `DST` |
| Block Exchange | `MOV`, `XCHG` (reg-only) | `AL`, `BL` (buffer) | Swapped in-place |
| Bubble Sort | `CMP`, `JBE`, nested `LOOP` | `SI`, `CX` | In-place sorted array |
| Largest Element | `CMP`, `JAE`, `LOOP` | `AL` (running max) | `RESULT` variable |
| Addition | `ADD`, `JC/JNC` | `AX` | `SUM` + `CARRY` byte |
| Subtraction | `SUB`, `JC/JNC` | `AX` | `DIFF` + `BORROW` byte |
| Multiplication | `MUL` | `AX` (input), `DX:AX` (output) | `PROD_HI:PROD_LO` |
| Division | `DIV` | `DX:AX` (input), `AX` (quot), `DX` (rem) | `QUOT`, `REM` |
| Square vs Cube | `MUL` (twice) + `CMP` + `JA/JB` | `DX:AX` for both | Comparison + display |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master ALP Program Flow (Generic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Initialize DS, ES] --> B[Load Count N into CX]
    B --> C[Load Source Offset into SI]
    C --> D[Load Destination Offset into DI]
    D --> E{CX = 0?}
    E -->|No| F[Process One Element MOV / XCHG / CMP / ADD etc.]
    F --> G[Update SI, DI Increment or Decrement]
    G --> H[Decrement CX: LOOP or DEC]
    H --> E
    E -->|Yes| I[Store Result in Memory]
    I --> J[INT 21H: Terminate Program]
    J --> K[END]
```

## 4.2 Block Data Movement Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC1[Source Memory Block SRC] -->|SI points here| LOAD1[Load byte into AL: LODSB]
    LOAD1 --> STORE1[Store byte via DI: STOSB]
    STORE1 --> DEST1[Destination Memory Block DST]
    STORE1 --> DEC1[DEC CX]
    DEC1 -->|CX not zero| LOAD1
    DEC1 -->|CX is zero| DONE1[Transfer Complete]
```

## 4.3 Bubble Sort Inner Loop (Comparison & Swap)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Start Inner Loop] --> Q[AL = ARR J]
    Q --> R[BL = ARR J+1]
    R --> S{AL greater than BL?}
    S -->|Yes| T1[ARR J = BL]
    T1 --> T2[ARR J+1 = AL]
    T2 --> U[Increment J: SI++]
    S -->|No| U
    U --> V[CX = CX - 1]
    V --> W{CX not zero?}
    W -->|Yes| Q
    W -->|No| X[Inner Loop Done]
```

## 4.4 Largest Element Search

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start] --> B[AL = ARR 0]
    B --> C[SI = Address of ARR 1]
    C --> D{CX = 0?}
    D -->|Yes| H[Store AL in RESULT: Stop]
    D -->|No| E{AL greater than or equal to ARR SI?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Skip: SI++]
    E -->|No| G[AL = ARR SI: Update max]
    F --> K[LOOP back]
    G --> K
    K --> D
```

## 4.5 Multi-Word Multiplication (Square and Cube) — Architectural Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    NREG[16-bit Register: N] --> MUL1[MUL Instruction]
    MUL1 -->|N squared 32-bit| DXAX1[DX high : AX low]
    DXAX1 -->|AX low part| MUL2[MUL by N again]
    NREG --> MUL2
    MUL2 --> CUBE[DX:AX = N cubed 32-bit]
    DXAX1 --> SQ[N squared: DX:AX]
    CUBE --> CMP[CMP high then low words]
    SQ --> CMP
    CMP -->|Cube greater| MSGA[Display MSG1]
    CMP -->|Square greater| MSGB[Display MSG2]
```

## 4.6 8086 Memory Map (For Block Operations)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MEMORY["8086 Memory Map 1 MB"]
        direction LR
        SEG1[00000H: Code Segment CS] --> SEG2[0FFF0H: Boundary]
        SEG2 --> SEG3[10000H: Data Segment DS]
        SEG3 --> SEG4[20000H: Extra Segment ES]
        SEG4 --> SEG5[30000H: Stack Segment SS]
    end

    subgraph SRCBLK["Data Segment DS: SRC Block"]
        direction LR
        S0[S0] --> S1[S1] --> S2[S2] --> S3[S3] --> S4[S4]
    end

    subgraph DSTBLK["Extra Segment ES: DST Block"]
        direction LR
        D0[D0] --> D1[D1] --> D2[D2] --> D3[D3] --> D4[D4]
    end
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer / Conceptual)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Explain the role of the `CLD` instruction in 8086 block data transfer. What happens if `STD` is used instead?**

**Model Answer:**
The `CLD` (Clear Direction Flag) instruction **resets the DF to 0**, causing `SI` and `DI` registers to **auto-increment** after every string operation like `MOVSB`, `LODSB`, or `STOSB`. This is essential when copying a block from a lower memory address to a higher one, as it ensures forward progression through both source and destination. If `STD` (Set Direction Flag) is used, `DF = 1` and the pointers **auto-decrement**, which is required when copying blocks in reverse order or handling overlapping memory regions where copying forward would overwrite source data. (3 marks)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Why does the 8086 `DIV` instruction require the dividend to be loaded into `DX:AX` rather than just `AX`?**

**Model Answer:**
The 8086 `DIV` instruction performs **16-bit ÷ 16-bit = 16-bit quotient + 16-bit remainder**. To support division of numbers larger than 16 bits, the dividend is **extended to 32 bits** by loading the high word into `DX` and the low word into `AX`. This 32-bit dividend (in `DX:AX`) is then divided by the 16-bit divisor, producing a 16-bit quotient in `AX` and a 16-bit remainder in `DX`. This scheme allows the 8086 to divide 32-bit numbers without requiring a separate instruction, maximizing the use of existing registers. (3 marks)

---

## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, divided into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. You must attempt **ONE full question** out of the two alternatives provided. Both alternatives cover equivalent cognitive levels per Bloom's Taxonomy.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU Model – May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Write an 8086 ALP to sort an array of 10 unsigned bytes stored in memory in **descending order** using the bubble sort algorithm. Show the trace for the first pass.

**Model Solution:**

```asm
ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA
DATA  SEGMENT
ARR   DB 12H, 05H, 33H, 08H, 1AH, 09H, 44H, 02H, 0FH, 1CH
N     DW 000AH
DATA  ENDS
CODE  SEGMENT
START: MOV AX, DATA
       MOV DS, AX
       MOV DX, N
       DEC DX                          ; Pass counter = N-1
OUTER: MOV CX, DX
       LEA SI, ARR
INNER: MOV AL, [SI]
       MOV BL, [SI+1]
       CMP AL, BL
       JAE NOSWAP                      ; For DESCENDING: swap if AL < BL
       MOV [SI], BL
       MOV [SI+1], AL
       NOSWAP: INC SI
       LOOP INNER
       DEC DX
       JNZ OUTER
       MOV AH, 4CH
       INT 21H
CODE ENDS
END START
```

**Trace of Pass 1 (first 5 swaps on sample data):**

| Step | AL (ARR[J]) | BL (ARR[J+1]) | Condition (AL < BL) | Action | Array After |
|------|-------------|---------------|---------------------|--------|-------------|
| 1 | 12H | 05H | 12H ≥ 05H | No swap | 12,05,33,08,1A,09,44,02,0F,1C |
| 2 | 05H | 33H | 05H < 33H | Swap | 12,33,05,08,1A,09,44,02,0F,1C |
| 3 | 05H | 08H | 05H < 08H | Swap | 12,33,08,05,1A,09,44,02,0F,1C |
| 4 | 05H | 1AH | 05H < 1AH | Swap | 12,33,08,1A,05,09,44,02,0F,1C |
| 5 | 05H | 09H | 05H < 09H | Swap | 12,33,08,1A,09,05,44,02,0F,1C |

(7 marks: 4 for code, 3 for trace)

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write an 8086 ALP to perform **16-bit unsigned division** of a 32-bit dividend by a 16-bit divisor, and store the **quotient and remainder** in memory. Explain the role of the `DX` register during the operation.

**Model Solution:**

```asm
ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA
DATA    SEGMENT
DVND_HI DW 0002H                       ; High 16 bits of dividend
DVND_LO DW 8000H                       ; Low 16 bits  of dividend
DVSR    DW 0003H                       ; Divisor
QUOT    DW ?
REM     DW ?
DATA    ENDS
CODE    SEGMENT
START:  MOV AX, DATA
        MOV DS, AX
        MOV DX, DVND_HI                ; High word → DX
        MOV AX, DVND_LO                ; Low word  → AX
        DIV DVSR                       ; (DX:AX) ÷ DVSR
        MOV QUOT, AX                   ; Quotient in AX
        MOV REM,  DX                   ; Remainder in DX
        MOV AH, 4CH
        INT 21H
CODE    ENDS
        END     START
```

**Role of `DX`:** The 8086 `DIV` instruction accepts a 32-bit dividend held in the **DX:AX register pair**. `DX` holds the **upper 16 bits (most significant word)** of the dividend, while `AX` holds the **lower 16 bits**. The 32-bit value `(DX × 65536) + AX` is divided by the operand (16-bit divisor). After execution, `AX` receives the 16-bit quotient and `DX` receives the 16-bit remainder. If the quotient overflows 16 bits (i.e., the actual quotient > 65535), the 8086 triggers **Type 0 Divide Error interrupt**, halting execution.

**Numerical verification:** 0x00028000 ÷ 0x0003 = 163840 ÷ 3 = 54613 (0xD555) remainder 1 (0x0001). (7 marks: 4 for code, 3 for explanation)

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU Model – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Write an 8086 ALP to find the **smallest element** in an array of N unsigned words. Show the trace.

**Model Solution:**

```asm
ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA
DATA    SEGMENT
ARR     DW 3500H, 1200H, 8800H, 4500H, 2100H
N       DW 0005H
SMALL   DW ?
DATA    ENDS
CODE    SEGMENT
START:  MOV AX, DATA
        MOV DS, AX
        LEA SI, ARR
        MOV CX, N
        DEC CX
        MOV AX, [SI]                   ; AX = ARR[0]
        ADD SI, 2                      ; Words are 2 bytes each
NEXT:   CMP AX, [SI]
        JBE SKIP                       ; If AX <= ARR[i], skip
        MOV AX, [SI]                   ; Update smaller
        SKIP: ADD SI, 2
        LOOP NEXT
        MOV SMALL, AX
        MOV AH, 4CH
        INT 21H
CODE    ENDS
END     START
```

**Trace (sample):**

| Iteration | SI offset | ARR[SI] | AX (current min) | Action |
|-----------|-----------|---------|------------------|--------|
| Init | 0000H | 3500H | 3500H | Set initial |
| 1 | 0002H | 1200H | 1200H | 1200H < 3500H → update |
| 2 | 0004H | 8800H | 1200H | No update |
| 3 | 0006H | 4500H | 1200H | No update |
| 4 | 0008H | 2100H | 1200H | No update |

**Result:** `SMALL = 1200H` ✓ (7 marks: 4 for code, 3 for trace)

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write an 8086 ALP to compute the **square and cube of a 16-bit number** stored in memory and display which is greater using DOS `INT 21H` function `09H`.

**Model Solution:**

```asm
ASSUME CS:CODE, DS:DATA
DATA    SEGMENT
N       DW 0005H
SQUARE  DW ?
CUBE    DW ?
MSG1    DB 'CUBE IS LARGER $'
MSG2    DB 'SQUARE IS LARGER $'
DATA    ENDS
CODE    SEGMENT
START:  MOV AX, DATA
        MOV DS, AX

        ; Compute N^2
        MOV AX, N
        MUL N                          ; DX:AX = N*N
        MOV SQUARE, AX

        ; Compute N^3
        MUL N                          ; DX:AX = N*N*N (low 32 bits)
        MOV CUBE, AX

        ; Compare
        MOV AX, CUBE
        CMP AX, SQUARE
        JAE CUBE_LARGER
        LEA DX, MSG2
        JMP DISPLAY
        CUBE_LARGER: LEA DX, MSG1
        DISPLAY: MOV AH, 09H
                 INT 21H
                 MOV AH, 4CH
                 INT 21H
CODE    ENDS
END     START
```

**Verification with N = 5:** N² = 25 (19H), N³ = 125 (7DH) → "CUBE IS LARGER" is displayed. (7 marks: 5 for code, 2 for sample output)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting `CLD` before `REP MOVSB`**: Marks deducted for unpredictable memory behavior. Always reset DF.
> 2. **Using `MUL` with memory operand incorrectly**: The syntax is `MUL src` where `src` is a register or memory, **not** an immediate. Use `MOV AX, N` first, then `MUL N`.
> 3. **Neglecting the `DX` register before `DIV`**: The dividend is **assumed** to be in `DX:AX`. Forgetting to load `DX` (especially for small dividends) gives garbage results.
> 4. **Off-by-one in `LOOP`**: `LOOP` decrements `CX` first, then jumps. If you initialize `CX` with `N` (instead of `N-1` or use the natural decrement), the loop runs `N+1` times. Trace carefully.
> 5. **Bubble sort comparison direction**: For **ascending**, use `JBE` (no swap if `AL ≤ BL`); for **descending**, use `JAE` (no swap if `AL ≥ BL`). Many students mix these up.
> 6. **Not initializing `ES`**: For string operations targeting the Extra Segment (e.g., `MOVSB` with `DI`), `ES` must be explicitly loaded with the destination segment address.
> 7. **Square vs Cube for N > 181**: For 16-bit input N > ~181, N³ exceeds 32 bits and the simple program fails. The examiner may award partial credit for identifying this limitation.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Quick-Recall Conceptual Map
- **Data Transfer** = `MOV`, `MOVSB/MOVSW`, `LODS`, `STOS`, `XCHG` (reg-only), `LEA`, `PUSH/POP`. **No flags affected** (except `MOVS`).
- **Arithmetic** = `ADD`, `SUB`, `ADC`, `SBB`, `MUL` (unsigned), `IMUL` (signed), `DIV` (unsigned), `IDIV` (signed), `CMP`, `INC`, `DEC`, `NEG`.
- **Logical & Program Control** = `LOOP`, conditional jumps (`JE`, `JNE`, `JA`, `JB`, `JG`, `JL`, `JC`, `JNC`).
- **String Operations** = Always require `SI` (source) and `DI` (destination), `CX` (counter), and `DF` (direction).

### 🔑 Five Must-Know Exam Formulas
1. `MUL` result location → **DX:AX** (32-bit result of 16 × 16)
2. `DIV` dividend location → **DX:AX** (32-bit dividend ÷ 16-bit divisor)
3. `DIV` result location → **AX** (quotient) and **DX** (remainder)
4. `LOOP` semantic → **CX = CX − 1, jump if CX ≠ 0**
5. `CLD`/`STD` semantic → `CLD`: DF = 0 (auto-increment), `STD`: DF = 1 (auto-decrement)

### ⚠️ Common Lab Errors
- **MASM/TASM case sensitivity:** MASM is **case-insensitive** for instructions but **case-sensitive** for labels. Be consistent.
- **Segment override missing:** Use `DS:` for `SI` and `ES:` for `DI` if segments are different.
- **Stack not balanced:** Every `PUSH` must have a matching `POP`.
- **Endless loops:** Forgetting to decrement `CX` or using wrong branch condition creates infinite loops — use **Ctrl+Break** in DOSBox.

### 🎯 KTU 2024 Bloom's Taxonomy Distribution for This Module
| Level | Coverage in Module 2 |
|-------|----------------------|
| **Remember (L1)** | Recall instruction mnemonics, register names |
| **Understand (L2)** | Explain data flow, addressing modes |
| **Apply (L3)** | Write and execute ALP programs |
| **Analyze (L4)** | Trace execution, identify flag changes |
| **Evaluate (L5)** | Compare algorithms (e.g., bubble sort vs selection sort in ALP) |
| **Create (L6)** | Design multi-word arithmetic routines |

### 📋 Lab Record Checklist (Viva Voce Favorites)
- ✅ Why are `SI` and `DI` 16-bit registers? — Because 8086 is a 16-bit processor; both segment and offset are 16 bits each.
- ✅ What is the difference between `MUL` and `IMUL`? — `MUL` is unsigned; `IMUL` is signed (preserves sign in 2's complement).
- ✅ Can `MUL` take an immediate operand? — **No.** Must first move to a register.
- ✅ What is the size of the largest block `REP MOVSB` can transfer? — Up to **65,536 bytes** (CX = 0 means 65,536 iterations in `REP`).

---

> **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — PCEOL508 (KTU 2024 Scheme)**

topics.title: ALP Programming for the Implementation of Counters (Hex Up/Down Counters and BCD Up/Down Counters)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEOL508 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** ALP Programming for the Implementation of Counters (Hex Up/Down Counters and BCD Up/Down Counters) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of the **Intel 8051 Microcontroller**, a **Counter** is a hardware peripheral subsystem built around a chain of flip-flops that increments or decrements its internal binary value in response to an external stimulus (a transition on a designated I/O pin) or an internal machine cycle pulse. When programmed using **Assembly Language Programming (ALP)**, the counter is configured via Special Function Registers (SFRs) — primarily the **Timer Mode Register (TMOD)** and the **Timer Control Register (TCON)** — to either count external events on pins **T0 (P3.4)** and **T1 (P3.5)**, or to time internal operations. In **Module Experiment 2**, the objective is to write four distinct ALP programs: 1. **Hexadecimal Up Counter** — counts $00_{H}$ to $\text{FF}_{H}$ ($0$ to $255$ decimal) and rolls over. 2. **Hexadecimal Down Counter** — counts $\text{FF}_{H}$ to $00_{H}$ ($255$ to $0$ decimal). 3. **BCD Up Counter** — counts $00$ to $99$ (Binary Coded Decimal, two digits). 4. **BCD Down Counter** — counts $99$ to $00$ (Binary Coded Decimal, two digits). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The exam explicitly tests the ability to write, trace, and debug 8051 ALP code for counter-based applications. Memorization of SFR bit names is **non-negotiable**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a **counter** as a **digital tally counter** — the kind a bouncer clicks at a nightclub door to keep track of the number of patrons entering. - Each click represents a **pulse** arriving at the counter's input pin. - An **Up Counter** increments the tally: $1, 2, 3, 4 \dots$ - A **Down Counter** decrements from a preset value: $99, 98, 97 \dots$ - A **Hex Counter** uses the full $0$–$\text{F}$ digit range per position (like a car's odometer with hexadecimal digits). - A **BCD Counter** is restricted to decimal digits $0$–$9$ per position, mimicking how a digital clock displays time. The 8051's built-in counters are exactly such tally counters, but they are implemented in silicon as flip-flops that toggle state on each external pulse. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction:** *Timer Mode* uses the internal oscillator frequency divided by 12 as the counting source. *Counter Mode* uses an external pin (T0/T1) as the counting source. For Module Experiment 2, we operate in **Counter Mode**. ## 1.3 Critical Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **8051 Machine Cycle Frequency:** $\dfrac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12}$ - **Standard 8051 Crystal Frequency:** $f_{\text{osc}} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$ (for serial baud rate generation) - **Counter Resolution (Mode 1, 16-bit):** $2^{16} = \mathbf{65{,}536}$ counts - **Counter Resolution (Mode 2, 8-bit Auto-Reload):** $2^{8} = \mathbf{256}$ counts - **Hex Counting Range:** $\mathbf{00_{H}}$ to $\mathbf{FF_{H}}$ ($0$ to $255$ decimal) - **BCD Counting Range (per digit):** $\mathbf{0000}$ to $\mathbf{1001}$ ($0$ to $9$ decimal) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The 8051 Counter Hardware Architecture The 8051 contains **two 16-bit counters**, named **Timer/Counter 0** and **Timer/Counter 1**. Each can be split into two 8-bit registers: | Counter | High Byte Register | Low Byte Register | External Pin | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | Timer/Counter 0 | **TH0** | **TL0** | **P3.4 (T0)** | | Timer/Counter 1 | **TH1** | **TL1** | **P1.0 (T1)** — *correction:* **P3.5 (T1)** | The low byte overflows into the high byte, forming a 16-bit counter when configured in Mode 1. ## 2.2 The TMOD Register — Mode Configuration The **TMOD** register is an **8-bit, non-bit-addressable** SFR located at address $\text{89}_{H}$. It configures the operating mode of both counters simultaneously. | Bit 7 | Bit 6 | Bit 5 | Bit 4 | Bit 3 | Bit 2 | Bit 1 | Bit 0 | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | **GATE** | **C/T̄** | **M1** | **M0** | **GATE** | **C/T̄** | **M1** | **M0** | | \multicolumn{4}{c}{TIMER 1} | \multicolumn{4}{c}{TIMER 0} | ### Bit-Field Definitions - **GATE** ($=1$): Hardware control enabled. Counter runs only when $\overline{\text{INTx}}$ pin is HIGH and TRx is set. - **C/T̄** ($=1$): **Counter Mode** (count external pulses on Tx pin). - **C/T̄** ($=0$): **Timer Mode** (count internal oscillator cycles). - **M1 M0**: Mode select bits. ### Mode Selection Truth Table | M1 | M0 | Mode | Description | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---| | 0 | 0 | **Mode 0** | 13-bit Timer/Counter (legacy 8048 mode) | | 0 | 1 | **Mode 1** | 16-bit Timer/Counter | | 1 | 0 | **Mode 2** | 8-bit Auto-Reload Timer/Counter | | 1 | 1 | **Mode 3** | Split Timer 0 (TL0 + TH0 as two 8-bit counters) | > [!IMPORTANT] > For Module Experiment 2 (Counters), the standard configuration is **Mode 1** with **C/T̄ = 1** (Counter Mode). The TMOD value for Counter 0 is therefore: **GATE=0, C/T̄=1, M1=0, M0=1** $\Rightarrow$ **TMOD = 0001 0001$_b$ = 11$_H$** for Timer 0 + Timer 1 unused. ## 2.3 The TCON Register — Run Control & Flags The **TCON** register at address $\text{88}_{H}$ is **bit-addressable** and controls the start/stop of counters and latches overflow flags. | Bit | Name | Address (bit) | Function | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---| | 7 | **TF1** | $\text{8F}_{H}$ | Timer 1 Overflow Flag (set by hardware, cleared by software) | | 6 | **TR1** | $\text{8E}_{H}$ | Timer 1 Run Control Bit ($1$ = start) | | 5 | **TF0** | $\text{8D}_{H}$ | Timer 0 Overflow Flag | | 4 | **TR0** | $\text{8C}_{H}$ | Timer 0 Run Control Bit | | 3 | IE1 | $\text{8B}_{H}$ | External Interrupt 1 Edge Flag | | 2 | IT1 | $\text{8A}_{H}$ | External Interrupt 1 Type (Edge/Level) | | 1 | IE0 | $\text{89}_{H}$ | External Interrupt 0 Edge Flag | | 0 | IT0 | $\text{88}_{H}$ | External Interrupt 0 Type | ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Concept | Formula / Value | Description | |:---|:---|:---| | Machine Cycle Period | $T_{mc} = \dfrac{12}{f_{\text{osc}}}$ | Time per increment in Timer Mode | | Max Count (Mode 1) | $\text{FFFF}_{H} - \text{Initial Value} + 1$ | Counts before overflow | | Max Count (Mode 2) | $\text{FF}_{H} - \text{Reload Value} + 1$ | Counts before auto-reload overflow | | Hex Up Roll-over | $\text{FF}_{H} + 1 \rightarrow \mathbf{00}_{H}$ | 8-bit overflow behavior | | BCD Tens Carry | $\text{0A}_{H} + 1 \rightarrow \mathbf{10}_{H}$ | BCD increment requires hex addition, then correction if $>9$ | | DA A (Decimal Adjust) | Conditional correction | Adds $06_{H}$ to lower nibble if $>9$ or AC=1; adds $60_{H}$ to upper nibble if $>9$ or CY=1 | | Counter Input Pulse | 1-to-0 transition on Tx | Hardware trigger for increment | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Counters implemented via 8051 ALP form the foundation of: - **Event counting systems** (object/person counters at industrial gates) - **Frequency measurement instruments** (counting pulses over a known time window) - **Digital tachometers** (counting shaft rotations per minute) - **Production line conveyor controllers** (counting items packaged) - **Vending machines** (deducting product counts from inventory) - **Traffic light controllers** (timing phase durations) # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix This section provides the **exhaustive, line-by-line ALP solutions** for all four counter variants. Every instruction is explained with its engineering rationale. ## 3.1 Program 1: Hexadecimal Up Counter (00 to FF) ### Algorithm Logic 1. Load TMOD to configure Counter 0 in **Mode 1 (16-bit), Counter Mode**. 2. Clear TH0 and TL0 (start count at $0000_{H}$). 3. Set TR0 to start counting external pulses on **P3.4**. 4. Poll the TF0 flag in a loop until overflow occurs. 5. Increment the displayed value on Port P1 or P2. 6. Repeat indefinitely. ### Complete ALP Listing ```assembly ORG 0000H ; Origin: Program starts at code address 0000H LJMP MAIN ; Long jump to main routine (skipping ISR vector area) ORG 000BH ; Reserve space for Timer 0 ISR (not used here) RETI ;----------------------------------------------------------- MAIN: MOV TMOD, #01H ; TMOD = 0000 0001 -> Timer0, Mode1 (16-bit), Timer Mode ; (We use Mode 1 structure, but TR0 only runs with pulses) MOV TH0, #00H ; Initialize HIGH byte of counter 0 to 00H MOV TL0, #00H ; Initialize LOW byte of counter 0 to 00H MOV A, #00H ; Accumulator holds the displayed count value MOV P1, A ; Display initial value 00H on Port 1 LOOP: SETB TR0 ; Start Timer/Counter 0 (begin counting) WAIT: JNB TF0, WAIT ; Loop here while TF0 = 0 (no overflow yet) CLR TR0 ; Stop the counter CLR TF0 ; Clear overflow flag manually (HW sets it, SW clears it) INC A ; Increment the displayed value (A <- A + 1) MOV P1, A ; Output new value to Port 1 LEDs ACALL DELAY ; Call a small software delay for visibility MOV TH0, #00H ; Reload TH0 for next counting cycle MOV TL0, #00H ; Reload TL0 for next counting cycle SJMP LOOP ; Repeat the counting cycle forever ;----------------------------------------------------------- DELAY: MOV R0, #0FFH ; Simple software delay subroutine D1: DJNZ R0, D1 ; Inner loop: 255 iterations of decrement-jump RET ; Return from subroutine END ; End of assembly source ``` ### Step-by-Step Rationale Table | Line | Instruction | Engineering Logic | |:---|:---|:---| | `ORG 0000H` | Origin declaration | Forces assembler to place next instruction at reset vector | | `MOV TMOD, #01H` | Mode select | Sets Timer 0 to Mode 1 with software start (`GATE=0, C/T̄=0`) | | `MOV TH0, #00H` | High byte reset | Prepares counter to overflow after exactly $2^{16}$ pulses | | `MOV TL0, #00H` | Low byte reset | Combined with TH0 = $00_{H}$, ensures initial count state | | `MOV P1, A` | Port output | Drives LEDs / 7-segment display for visual feedback | | `SETB TR0` | Start counter | Hardware begins incrementing TL0 on each T0 pin transition | | `JNB TF0, WAIT` | Polling check | Continuously polls overflow flag until counter wraps from $\text{FFFF}_H$ to $0000_H$ | | `CLR TF0` | Flag clear | Mandatory — overflow flag is NOT auto-cleared in polling mode | | `INC A` | Increment display | Each overflow represents one full count cycle complete | ## 3.2 Program 2: Hexadecimal Down Counter (FF to 00) ### Algorithm Logic 1. Preload TL0 = TH0 = $\text{FF}_H$ (counter starts near maximum). 2. Start the counter. 3. Wait for TF0 to set (overflow occurs when $\text{FFFF}_H + 1 \rightarrow 0000_H$). 4. On each overflow, **decrement** the displayed value. 5. Reload counter for the next cycle. ### Complete ALP Listing ```assembly ORG 0000H LJMP MAIN ORG 000BH RETI MAIN: MOV TMOD, #01H ; Counter 0 in Mode 1 (16-bit) MOV TH0, #0FFH ; Preload HIGH byte = FFH MOV TL0, #0FFH ; Preload LOW byte = FFH MOV A, #0FFH ; Display register starts at FFH MOV P1, A ; Output FFH to Port 1 LOOP: SETB TR0 ; Start counter WAIT: JNB TF0, WAIT ; Wait for overflow flag CLR TR0 ; Stop counter CLR TF0 ; Clear overflow flag DEC A ; A <- A - 1 (down count) MOV P1, A ; Display new value ACALL DELAY ; Visibility delay MOV TH0, #0FFH ; Reload counter MOV TL0, #0FFH SJMP LOOP ; Repeat DELAY: MOV R0, #0FFH D1: DJNZ R0, D1 RET END ``` ### Analytical Trace Matrix | Pulse # on T0 pin | TL0 Internal | TF0 Flag | A (Display) | P1 Output | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | $\text{FF}_H + 1 = 00_H$ (TH unchanged) | 0 | $\text{FF}_H$ | $\text{FF}_H$ | | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | | 255 | $\text{FF}_H$ | 0 | $\text{81}_H$ | $\text{81}_H$ | | 256 | $\text{00}_H$ (with carry to TH) | 1 | $\text{80}_H$ | $\mathbf{80}_H$ ← overflow detected | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Insight:** For the *Down Counter*, the decrement happens on the *display register*, not the hardware counter. The hardware counter simply toggles an overflow event; the application logic decides whether to count up or down. ## 3.3 Program 3: BCD Up Counter (00 to 99) ### Algorithm Logic — The BCD Constraint A pure 8051 counter is fundamentally a **hex counter**. To produce a **BCD** count ($00 \rightarrow 99$), we must: 1. Increment a display register. 2. Use the **`DA A`** (Decimal Adjust Accumulator) instruction to convert hex addition into BCD. 3. Detect when the count exceeds $99_{BCD}$ and reset. ### Complete ALP Listing ```assembly ORG 0000H LJMP MAIN ORG 000BH RETI MAIN: MOV TMOD, #01H ; Counter 0 in Mode 1 MOV TH0, #00H MOV TL0, #00H MOV R1, #00H ; R1 holds the BCD count value (00 to 99) MOV P1, R1 ; Display initial value 00 LOOP: SETB TR0 ; Start counter WAIT: JNB TF0, WAIT ; Wait for overflow CLR TR0 CLR TF0 MOV A, R1 ; Move current count to accumulator ADD A, #01H ; A <- A + 1 (binary addition) DA A ; Decimal Adjust: corrects to BCD format MOV R1, A ; Store corrected BCD value back CJNE A, #99H, SKIP ; Compare A with 99H (BCD 99); if not equal, skip reset MOV R1, #00H ; If equal to 99, reset to 00 SKIP: MOV P1, R1 ; Display current BCD value ACALL DELAY ; Visibility delay MOV TH0, #00H ; Reload counter for next iteration MOV TL0, #00H SJMP LOOP ; Repeat DELAY: MOV R0, #0FFH D1: DJNZ R0, D1 RET END ``` ### `DA A` Instruction — Detailed Mathematical Operation The Decimal Adjust instruction performs the following corrections automatically: $$ A_{\text{BCD}} = \begin{cases} A_{\text{lower nibble}} + 06_{H} & \text{if } (A_{\text{lower nibble}} > 9) \text{ OR } (AC = 1) \\ A_{\text{upper nibble}} + 60_{H} & \text{if } (A_{\text{upper nibble}} > 9) \text{ OR } (CY = 1) \end{cases} $$ **Example Trace:** $$ A_{\text{before}} = 09_{H}, \quad \text{ADD } A, \#01_{H} \Rightarrow A = 0A_{H} $$ Since lower nibble $A > 9$, `DA A` adds $06_{H}$: $$ 0A_{H} + 06_{H} = 10_{H} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{BCD display} = 10 $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The `DA A` instruction **only works on the Accumulator**. It cannot decimal-adjust any other register directly. ## 3.4 Program 4: BCD Down Counter (99 to 00) ### Algorithm Logic 1. Initialize the display register to $99_{BCD}$. 2. Use a subtraction with `SUBB` and then decimal-adjust by adding $06_{H}/60_{H}$ conditionally — **or** more practically, decrement and then add $9A_{H}$ if the result needs BCD correction. ### Complete ALP Listing ```assembly ORG 0000H LJMP MAIN ORG 000BH RETI MAIN: MOV TMOD, #01H ; Counter 0 in Mode 1 MOV TH0, #00H MOV TL0, #00H MOV R1, #99H ; Initialize BCD count to 99 MOV P1, R1 LOOP: SETB TR0 WAIT: JNB TF0, WAIT CLR TR0 CLR TF0 MOV A, R1 ; Get current BCD value ADD A, #99H ; Equivalent to A - 1 in BCD (using 9's complement) DA A ; Decimal adjust to BCD MOV R1, A ; Store back CJNE R1, #00H, SKIP ; If not zero, continue MOV R1, #99H ; If zero, reset to 99 (rollover) SKIP: MOV P1, R1 ; Display value ACALL DELAY MOV TH0, #00H MOV TL0, #00H SJMP LOOP DELAY: MOV R0, #0FFH D1: DJNZ R0, D1 RET END ``` ### Mathematical Justification of BCD Down Count Trick The expression $A + 99_{H}$ is equivalent to $A - 1_{H}$ in BCD when followed by `DA A`: $$ A_{\text{BCD}} + 99_{H} = A_{\text{BCD}} + 100_{H} - 1_{H} $$ For example: $$ 08_{H} + 99_{H} = A1_{H} \quad \xrightarrow{\text{DA A}} \quad \mathbf{07}_{H} + \text{CY=1} $$ The carry flag is set, and the accumulator holds the decremented BCD value. ## 3.5 Comparison Matrix — All Four Counter Variants | Parameter | Hex Up | Hex Down | BCD Up | BCD Down | |:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | TMOD Value | $01_{H}$ | $01_{H}$ | $01_{H}$ | $01_{H}$ | | Initial TH0/TL0 | $00_{H}/00_{H}$ | $\text{FF}_H/\text{FF}_H$ | $00_{H}/00_{H}$ | $00_{H}/00_{H}$ | | Initial Display | $00_{H}$ | $\text{FF}_H$ | $00_{H}$ | $99_{H}$ | | Update Instruction | `INC A` | `DEC A` | `ADD A,#01H` + `DA A` | `ADD A,#99H` + `DA A` | | Roll-over Condition | $\text{FF}_H \rightarrow 00_{H}$ | $00_{H} \rightarrow \text{FF}_H$ | $99_{H} \rightarrow 00_{H}$ | $00_{H} \rightarrow 99_{H}$ | | Range | $0$–$255$ | $255$–$0$ | $0$–$99$ | $99$–$0$ | | Flag Used | TF0 | TF0 | TF0 | TF0 | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Program Flow Diagram — Generic Counter ALP Structure ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Power ON Reset] --> B[Initialize TMOD = 01H] B --> C[Load TH0 and TL0] C --> D[Initialize Display Register] D --> E[Output to Port P1] E --> F[SETB TR0 - Start Counter] F --> G{TF0 = 0?} G -- Yes --> G G -- No, TF0 = 1 --> H[CLR TR0 - Stop Counter] H --> I[CLR TF0 - Clear Flag] I --> J[Update Display Value] J --> K[Reload TH0 and TL0] K --> L[Call DELAY subroutine] L --> F ``` ## 4.2 TMOD Register Configuration Map ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph T1[Timer 1 Control Bits] A1[GATE1 = 0] --> B1[C/T_bar1 = 0] B1 --> C1[M11 = 0] C1 --> D1[M01 = 1] end subgraph T0[Timer 0 Control Bits] A2[GATE0 = 0] --> B2[C/T_bar0 = 1] B2 --> C2[M10 = 0] C2 --> D2[M00 = 1] end T1 --> E[TMOD = 0001 0001 binary = 11H] T0 --> E ``` ## 4.3 BCD Up Counter State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> S00 S00 --> S01 : Pulse 1: ADD 01H, DA A S01 --> S02 : Pulse 2 S02 --> S03 : Pulse 3 S03 --> S04 : Pulse 4 S04 --> S05 : Pulse 5 S05 --> S06 : Pulse 6 S06 --> S07 : Pulse 7 S07 --> S08 : Pulse 8 S08 --> S09 : Pulse 9 S09 --> S10 : Pulse 10 - BCD carry S10 --> S11 : Pulse 11 S98 --> S99 : Pulse 98 S99 --> S00 : Pulse 99 - ROLLOVER detected ``` ## 4.4 Counter Hardware Block Diagram (8051 Internal) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SF[Special Function Registers] TCON[TCON Register
TR0, TF0 Control] TMOD[TMOD Register
Mode Configuration] end subgraph CR[Counter 0 Hardware] TL[TL0 - Low Byte 8-bit] TH[TH0 - High Byte 8-bit] TL --> TH end EXT[External Pin P3.4 T0] -->|negative edge| TL OSC[Internal Oscillator
f_osc divided by 12] -.->|Timer Mode only| TL TMOD -->|selects source| TL TCON -->|TR0 starts/stops| CR CR -->|overflow| TCON TCON -->|sets TF0| CPU[CPU Polling JNB TF0] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Legend:** Solid arrows represent signal flow during **Counter Mode** (external pin). Dotted arrow represents **Timer Mode** signal flow (internal oscillator), which is *not* used in Module Experiment 2. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Explain the function of the TMOD register in the 8051 microcontroller. What value must be loaded into TMOD to configure Timer 0 as a 16-bit counter in software-started mode?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **TMOD (Timer Mode) register** is an 8-bit non-bit-addressable SFR at address $89_{H}$ that configures the operating mode of the two 8051 timers/counters. Its upper nibble controls Timer 1 and the lower nibble controls Timer 0. Each nibble contains four bits: **GATE** (hardware control), **C/T̄** (Counter/Timer select), **M1** and **M0** (mode select). For Timer 0 as a 16-bit software-started counter: - GATE = $0$ (software control only) - C/T̄ = $1$ (Counter Mode) - M1 = $0$, M0 = $1$ (Mode 1 = 16-bit) The required value is $\text{TMOD} = \mathbf{01_{H}}$. ### Question A2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is the role of the `DA A` instruction in 8051 ALP? Write a short note on its operation.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **`DA A`** (**Decimal Adjust Accumulator**) instruction converts the result of a binary addition operation on the accumulator into proper **Binary Coded Decimal (BCD)** format. It is used after `ADD` or `ADDC` instructions when the operands contain valid BCD values. Operation: - If the lower nibble of A is $> 9$ **OR** the auxiliary carry (AC) flag is $1$, then $06_{H}$ is added to the lower nibble. - If the upper nibble of A is $> 9$ **OR** the carry (CY) flag is $1$, then $60_{H}$ is added to the upper nibble. This ensures that BCD counting (e.g., $09 + 1 = 10$) produces the correct display representation on 7-segment displays or LCDs. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question B1 (Question A Choice): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Write an 8051 Assembly Language Program (ALP) to implement a Hexadecimal Up Counter using Timer 0 in Mode 1. The counter should display its value on Port P1 and increment on each overflow of Timer 0. Explain the role of TMOD and TCON registers in your program. $[14 \text{ marks}]$** #### Part (a): ALP Code $[7 \text{ marks}]$ ```assembly ORG 0000H LJMP MAIN ORG 000BH RETI MAIN: MOV TMOD, #01H ; Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit), software start MOV TH0, #00H ; Load high byte = 00H MOV TL0, #00H ; Load low byte = 00H MOV A, #00H ; Display register initial value MOV P1, A ; Output 00H on Port 1 LOOP: SETB TR0 ; Start Timer 0 WAIT: JNB TF0, WAIT ; Wait for overflow flag CLR TR0 ; Stop Timer 0 CLR TF0 ; Clear overflow flag INC A ; Increment display MOV P1, A ; Display on Port 1 ACALL DELAY MOV TH0, #00H ; Reload counter MOV TL0, #00H SJMP LOOP DELAY: MOV R0, #0FFH D1: DJNZ R0, D1 RET END ``` #### Part (b): TMOD and TCON Explanation $[7 \text{ marks}]$ **TMOD Register ($89_{H}$):** - It selects the **mode of operation** of the timers/counters. - Lower nibble ($01_{H}$): Configures Timer 0 in **Mode 1 (16-bit)** with software start (`GATE = 0, C/T̄ = 0$ — Timer Mode$). - Upper nibble ($00_{H}$): Timer 1 is unused and held in Mode 0 with software start. **TCON Register ($88_{H}$):** - **TR0 (bit 4)**: Set to $1$ by `SETB TR0` to start counting; cleared by `CLR TR0` to stop. - **TF0 (bit 5)**: Set to $1$ by hardware when the counter overflows from $\text{FFFF}_H$ to $0000_H$; polled by `JNB TF0, WAIT` instruction and cleared by `CLR TF0`. - The other bits (TF1, TR1, IE0/1, IT0/1) are not used in this program. The combination of TMOD (mode selection) and TCON (run control + flag status) orchestrates the entire counter operation. --- ### Question B2 (Question B Choice): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Design an 8051 ALP to implement a BCD Up Counter that counts from 00 to 99 using Timer 0 overflow events. The count value must be displayed on Port P1. Show how the `DA A` instruction is used to maintain BCD format. Include proper initialization and roll-over logic. $[14 \text{ marks}]$** #### Part (a): ALP Code $[7 \text{ marks}]$ ```assembly ORG 0000H LJMP MAIN ORG 000BH RETI MAIN: MOV TMOD, #01H ; Timer 0, Mode 1 MOV TH0, #00H MOV TL0, #00H MOV R1, #00H ; BCD count starts at 00 MOV P1, R1 LOOP: SETB TR0 WAIT: JNB TF0, WAIT CLR TR0 CLR TF0 MOV A, R1 ADD A, #01H ; Binary addition DA A ; Convert to BCD MOV R1, A CJNE A, #99H, SKIP MOV R1, #00H SKIP: MOV P1, R1 ACALL DELAY MOV TH0, #00H MOV TL0, #00H SJMP LOOP DELAY: MOV R0, #0FFH D1: DJNZ R0, D1 RET END ``` #### Part (b): Role of `DA A` and Roll-over Logic $[7 \text{ marks}]$ **The `DA A` Instruction:** Without `DA A`, incrementing $09_{H}$ would yield $0A_{H}$ — an invalid BCD value. The `DA A` instruction corrects this: - Detects that the lower nibble $A > 9$ - Adds $06_{H}$: $0A_{H} + 06_{H} = 10_{H}$ - Result: BCD $10$ — exactly what we need for a decimal display. Similarly, when the lower digit rolls from $09 \rightarrow 10$, the upper digit automatically increments to $1$ via BCD carry propagation. **Roll-over Logic:** The `CJNE A, #99H, SKIP` instruction compares the accumulator (current BCD value) with $99_{H}$ (BCD 99). If the values are **not equal**, execution jumps to `SKIP` and continues normally. If they **are equal**, the next two instructions execute, loading $00_{H}$ into R1 — effectively resetting the counter. The `SJMP LOOP` continues the infinite counting cycle. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Forgetting `CLR TF0` after polling:** The TF0 flag is set by hardware but **must** be cleared by software. If forgotten, the next `JNB TF0, WAIT` will immediately fail because TF0 is still $1$, and the program will appear to "skip" the wait — losing **2 marks**. > 2. **Forgetting to reload TH0/TL0:** Since Mode 1 is a 16-bit counter (not auto-reload), the registers must be manually reloaded before each counting cycle. Missing this causes erratic counts and a **3-mark deduction**. > 3. **Using `DA A` without prior `ADD`:** `DA A` is *not* a standalone BCD increment. It only corrects the result of an addition. Using it alone will produce wrong results — **2-mark deduction**. > 4. **Wrong TMOD nibble:** Students frequently configure Timer 1 instead of Timer 0, or set `C/T̄ = 0` (Timer Mode) instead of Counter Mode. Always double-check the lower nibble for Timer 0 settings — **2-mark deduction**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - 🎯 **TMOD Configuration for Counter 0 (Mode 1):** Value $= \mathbf{01_{H}}$ — must be loaded **before** setting `TR0`. - 🎯 **Counter Mode vs Timer Mode:** Set `C/T̄ = 1` in TMOD for Counter Mode (external pin pulses on P3.4 for Timer 0). - 🎯 **TCON Bits:** `TR0 = 1` starts the counter; `TF0 = 1` indicates overflow; both must be managed explicitly in polling mode. - 🎯 **Polling Pattern:** `WAIT: JNB TF0, WAIT` is the canonical 8051 polling idiom for counter overflow detection. - 🎯 **Hex Counter Range:** $00_{H}$ to $\text{FF}_H$ — natural 8-bit overflow behavior, no correction needed. - 🎯 **BCD Counter Range:** $00$ to $99$ — requires `DA A` after each increment, plus a `CJNE` roll-over check. - 🎯 **`DA A` Precondition:** Must follow an `ADD` or `ADDC` instruction; only operates on the accumulator. - 🎯 **Reload Requirement:** Mode 1 (16-bit) is **not** auto-reload. TH0 and TL0 must be reloaded manually after each overflow. - 🎯 **Mode 2 Advantage:** If auto-reload is needed, use Mode 2 (`TMOD = 02_{H}$` for Timer 0) — TH0 holds the reload value automatically. - 🎯 **Pin Mapping:** Counter 0 input $\rightarrow$ **P3.4**; Counter 1 input $\rightarrow$ **P3.5**. - 🎯 **Reset Vector:** All 8051 programs must begin with `ORG 0000H` followed by a jump to main, to avoid ISR vector area collisions. - 🎯 **Software Delay:** Always include a `DELAY` subroutine after updating P1, otherwise the LED display changes too fast for human observation. - 🎯 **Roll-over Logic:** Use `CJNE` for BCD comparison; use natural overflow for Hex counts.

topics.title: ALP Programming for Boolean and Logical Instructions (Bit Manipulation) and Conditional Call and Return Instructions (Toggling Port 1 Bits with 55H and AAH, Factorial of a Number)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL508 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** ALP Programming for Boolean and Logical Instructions (Bit Manipulation) and Conditional Call and Return Instructions (Toggling Port 1 Bits with 55H and AAH, Factorial of a Number)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Assembly Language Programming (ALP) — Formal Definition

**Assembly Language Programming (ALP)** for the **Intel 8051 microcontroller** is the process of writing machine-level instructions using human-readable **mnemonics** (e.g., `MOV A, #55H`, `ANL A, #0FH`) that the **8051 Assembler** translates directly into object code executed by the CPU. In the context of **PCEOL508 Lab Module 2**, ALP exercises focus on two instruction families:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> - **Boolean (Bit-Level) Instructions** — A specialized subset of the 8051 instruction set that operates on **individual bits** within the bit-addressable memory area (RAM addresses `20H–2FH`) and on selected SFRs. They enable direct bit manipulation, hardware flag control, and single-pin I/O handling.
> - **Logical Instructions** — Mnemonics that perform **bitwise logical operations** (AND, OR, XOR, NOT, Rotate) on the **accumulator (A)** or directly between two memory locations.
> - **Conditional Call and Return Instructions** — Program-control mnemonics (`JC`, `JNC`, `JB`, `JNB`, `JBC`, `CJNE`, `DJNZ`) that branch, call subroutines, or return from subroutines based on the state of one or more condition flags/bits.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Light Switch Panel

Imagine a wall panel with **8 light switches**, each controlling a separate bulb in a room (this is your **Port 1** of the 8051).

| Real-World Object | 8051 Equivalent |
|---|---|
| The 8-switch panel | Port 1 (`P1`) — an 8-bit I/O register at SFR address `90H` |
| Each individual switch | A single bit (`P1.0`, `P1.1`, …, `P1.7`) |
| Flicking switch UP (`1`) | `SETB P1.x` |
| Flicking switch DOWN (`0`) | `CLR P1.x` |
| "Invert every switch" (UP↔DOWN) | `CPL P1` — bitwise NOT |
| "Turn ON switches 1, 3, 5, 7 only" | `MOV P1, #55H` (binary `0101 0101`) |
| "Turn ON switches 2, 4, 6, 8 only" | `MOV P1, #0AAH` (binary `1010 1010`) |

> [!NOTE]
> The first experiment of this module literally **toggles** the panel between the "odd switches ON" pattern (`55H`) and the "even switches ON" pattern (`AAH`), creating a **chasing/blinking LED display** on Port 1.

The **factorial** program, on the other hand, is like a **reusable sub-recipe** kept in a notebook:
1. You write the recipe **once** as a subroutine.
2. Every time the chef (CPU) needs it, he **calls** it (`LCALL FACT`).
3. When the dish is done, the chef **returns** to the main cooking flow (`RET`).
4. A **conditional** call/return lets the chef skip the recipe if the ingredient is missing (e.g., the number is `0` → factorial is `1`).

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics in Bold

- **8051 Clock Frequency:** typically **11.0592 MHz** or **12 MHz** crystal (1 machine cycle = **12 oscillator periods**).
- **Port 1 SFR Address:** **`90H`** (bit-addressable, bits `90H–97H`).
- **Bit-addressable RAM:** internal RAM `20H–2FH` (16 bytes = **128 addressable bits**, `00H–7FH`).
- **Accumulator (A):** SFR at `E0H` (bit-addressable).
- **`55H` in binary:** `0101 0101` (decimal **85**).
- **`AAH` in binary:** `1010 1010` (decimal **170**).
- **Maximum 8-bit factorial:** `7! = 5040` fits in 16 bits; `8! = 40320` requires 32-bit storage.

---

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for KTU lab viva:** Examiners almost always ask *"What is the difference between a logical instruction and a Boolean instruction?"* The crisp answer is: **Logical instructions operate on the whole byte (8 bits at a time) of the accumulator**, whereas **Boolean instructions operate on a single bit** of the bit-addressable memory.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 8051 Instruction Grouping Used in This Experiment

The 8051 instruction set has **111 base instructions** (255 opcodes total). For Module 2 we focus on **three groups**:

### Group A — Bitwise Logical Instructions (operate on bytes)

| Mnemonic | Operation | Allowed Operands | Bytes | Machine Cycles |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `ANL A, Rn` | A ← A AND Rn | A, R0–R7 | 1 | 1 |
| `ANL A, direct` | A ← A AND (direct) | A, direct addr | 2 | 1 |
| `ANL A, @Ri` | A ← A AND (Ri) | A, @R0/@R1 | 1 | 1 |
| `ANL A, #data` | A ← A AND #data | A, immediate | 2 | 1 |
| `ANL direct, A` | (direct) ← (direct) AND A | direct, A | 2 | 1 |
| `ANL direct, #data` | (direct) ← (direct) AND #data | direct, immediate | 3 | 2 |
| `ORL A, …` (six forms) | A ← A OR operand | mirrors ANL | 1–3 | 1–2 |
| `XRL A, …` (six forms) | A ← A XOR operand | mirrors ANL | 1–3 | 1–2 |
| `CLR A` | A ← 00H | A only | 1 | 1 |
| `CPL A` | A ← NOT A (one's complement) | A only | 1 | 1 |
| `RL A` | Rotate A left (8 bits, no carry) | A | 1 | 1 |
| `RLC A` | Rotate A left **through** Carry | A | 1 | 1 |
| `RR A` | Rotate A right (no carry) | A | 1 | 1 |
| `RRC A` | Rotate A right through Carry | A | 1 | 1 |
| `SWAP A` | Swap nibbles of A | A | 1 | 1 |

### Group B — Boolean (Bit) Instructions

| Mnemonic | Function | Bytes | Cycles |
|---|---|---|---|
| `CLR bit` | Clear bit (bit ← 0) | 2 | 1 |
| `SETB bit` | Set bit (bit ← 1) | 2 | 1 |
| `CPL bit` | Complement bit | 2 | 1 |
| `ANL C, bit` | AND carry with bit | 2 | 2 |
| `ANL C, /bit` | AND carry with **NOT** bit | 2 | 2 |
| `ORL C, bit` | OR carry with bit | 2 | 2 |
| `ORL C, /bit` | OR carry with NOT bit | 2 | 2 |
| `MOV C, bit` | Carry ← bit | 2 | 1 |
| `MOV bit, C` | bit ← Carry | 2 | 2 |

**Bit addresses:** any address from `00H` to `FFH` that falls in the bit-addressable space. For Port 1, `P1.0` = bit address `90H`, `P1.7` = bit address `97H`.

### Group C — Conditional Jump / Call / Return

| Mnemonic | Condition Tested | Action if True |
|---|---|---|
| `JC rel` | Carry = 1 | Jump |
| `JNC rel` | Carry = 0 | Jump |
| `JB bit, rel` | bit = 1 | Jump |
| `JNB bit, rel` | bit = 0 | Jump |
| `JBC bit, rel` | bit = 1 | Jump **and Clear** the bit |
| `CJNE A, #data, rel` | A ≠ data | Jump |
| `CJNE A, direct, rel` | A ≠ (direct) | Jump |
| `CJNE Rn, #data, rel` | Rn ≠ data | Jump |
| `CJNE @Ri, #data, rel` | (Ri) ≠ data | Jump |
| `DJNZ Rn, rel` | Decrement Rn, jump if not zero | Dec + conditional jump |
| `DJNZ direct, rel` | Decrement (direct), jump if not zero | Dec + conditional jump |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **There is NO conditional `CALL` instruction in the 8051.** The phrase "Conditional Call and Return" used in the KTU module descriptor therefore means **a conditional JUMP to a label that contains a subroutine-style block followed by a `RET`**, OR a **conditional `RET`** simulated using a flag check before the `RET`. We will see both patterns below.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Reference Card

> [!NOTE]
> The table below uses `\vert` and `\mid` instead of the vertical bar `|` to keep the markdown table parser happy. Do not change them back to `|`.

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Range | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Machine cycle duration | $T_{mc} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | seconds | For $f_{osc} = 11.0592\,\text{MHz}$, $T_{mc} \approx 1.085\,\mu s$ |
| Delay loop count for n ms | $\text{count} = \dfrac{n \times 10^{-3}}{T_{mc}}$ | integer | Round up to nearest whole loop |
| Two-loop nested delay (R5 outer, R6 inner) | $\text{Delay} = (255 \times 2 + 3) \times R5_{max}\,\mu s$ (for 12 MHz) | microseconds | Inner DJNZ: 2 µs × 255 + final 2 µs |
| Bit mask for `55H` pattern | $0x55 = 0b0101\,0101$ | — | Sets bits 0,2,4,6 |
| Bit mask for `AAH` pattern | $0xAA = 0b1010\,1010$ | — | Sets bits 1,3,5,7 |
| Factorial overflow threshold | $n! > 255$ for $n \geq 6$ | bytes | $5! = 120$, $6! = 720$ (needs 2 bytes) |
| Accumulator NOT | $A_{new} = \bar{A}$ | 8-bit | Implemented as `CPL A` |
| Bitwise XOR identity | $A \oplus A = 0$ | — | Used to clear accumulator via `XRL A,A` |
| $n!$ recurrence | $n! = n \times (n-1)!$ | — | Base case $0! = 1! = 1$ |
| Carry after `ADD A,#data` | $C = 1$ if $A + \text{data} > 255$ | — | Used for factorial loop termination |

## 2.3 Why This Matters in Real Engineering

- **Industrial PLCs and relay logic replacement** — A single `ANL` or `ORL` can replace an entire combinational-logic gate network, saving PCB area.
- **LED matrix driving** — `55H`/`AAH` alternating patterns are the foundation of running-light displays, scoreboards, and rotating beacon simulations.
- **Masked I/O control** — `ANL P1, #0F0H` clears the lower nibble **without disturbing** the upper nibble, a common technique to drive multiplexed 7-segment displays.
- **Subroutine re-use** — Factorial subroutines are the smallest example of a **re-entrant computational kernel**, the same idea behind `pow()`, `sin()`, and `printf()` in high-level libraries.
- **Stack discipline** — Conditional `RET` teaches the same `PUSH`/`POP` discipline needed for interrupt service routines (ISRs) in later modules.

---
