> **The Magic of PWM:** The motor is too "slow" (mechanically sluggish due to rotor inertia) to respond to the individual ON/OFF pulses. It only "sees" the **average voltage**. By simply varying the ratio of ON-time to OFF-time, we get a fully digital, highly efficient, and software-controllable speed regulator — with **zero power loss** as heat (unlike a linear rheostat).

### 1.3 Key Vocabulary & Physical Constants

- **PWM Frequency ($f_{PWM}$):** Typically **1 kHz to 25 kHz** for DC motors. Above 20 kHz avoids audible whining noise.
- **Duty Cycle ($D$):** Ratio of ON-time to total period, expressed in **%**.
- **H-Bridge:** A 4-transistor (or 4-switch) bridge circuit that allows voltage polarity reversal without mechanical relays.
- **Match Register (MR0):** Defines the PWM **period** in LPC1768.
- **Match Register (MR1–MR6):** Defines the **duty cycle** for the corresponding PWM channel.
- **PCLK (Peripheral Clock):** Default **25 MHz** in KTU LPC1768 kits (after PLL configuration, peripheral clock divider is set).
- **Driver IC:** **L293D** (Dual H-Bridge, 600 mA per channel) or **L298N** (2 A per channel) — the industry-standard KTU lab choice.

> [!TIP]
> **Why not vary the analog voltage directly?** Because microcontrollers are digital! They cannot output a true analog signal natively. PWM is the **cheapest, most efficient, and most software-flexible** workaround.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a PWM Signal

A PWM signal is a square wave characterized by two parameters:

1. **Period ($T_{PWM}$)** — Total time of one ON+OFF cycle.
2. **Duty Cycle ($D$)** — Percentage of the period during which the signal is HIGH.

A typical PWM waveform for a DC motor control application has:
- A **constant period** (constant frequency).
- A **variable duty cycle** to vary speed.

### 2.2 The Three Theoretical Pillars

#### Pillar 1: Average Voltage Equation

The average DC voltage seen by the motor armature is:

$$V_{avg} = D \times V_{supply}$$

where $D$ is the duty cycle as a decimal (e.g., 0.5 for 50%).

- If $V_{supply} = 12\text{ V}$ and $D = 0.5$, then $V_{avg} = 6\text{ V}$ → Motor runs at half speed.
- If $D = 1.0$, then $V_{avg} = 12\text{ V}$ → Full speed.

#### Pillar 2: PWM Frequency for Motor Control

$$f_{PWM} = \frac{1}{T_{PWM}}$$

- $f_{PWM} < 100\text{ Hz}$ → Motor stutters, audible "clicking" (poor).
- $100\text{ Hz} \leq f_{PWM} \leq 1\text{ kHz}$ → Acceptable, but audible coil whine.
- **$1\text{ kHz} \leq f_{PWM} \leq 25\text{ kHz}$** → **Recommended (smooth, inaudible).**
- $f_{PWM} > 50\text{ kHz}$ → Switching losses dominate in driver transistors.

#### Pillar 3: LPC1768 PWM Peripheral Registers

The LPC1768 has a **PWM1** block (and PWM0) with **6 output channels** and a 32-bit timer counter. The critical registers are:

| Register | Full Name | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **PCLKSEL0/1** | Peripheral Clock Select | Powers up the PWM1 peripheral clock |
| **PINSELx** | Pin Select | Routes PWM1 pin function to physical GPIO |
| **PWM1TCR** | Timer Control Register | Enables counter & PWM mode, resets counter |
| **PWM1PR** | Prescale Register | Divides PCLK before feeding the timer |
| **PWM1MR0** | Match Register 0 | **Defines the PWM period** |
| **PWM1MRx** | Match Register x (1–6) | **Defines the duty cycle for channel x** |
| **PWM1LER** | Latch Enable Register | Latches new MRx values into shadow registers |
| **PWM1PCR** | PWM Control Register | Enables PWM outputs, selects single/double edge mode |
| **PWM1MCR** | Match Control Register | Configures behavior on match (interrupt/reset/stop) |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Typical KTU Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{period}} \times 100\%$ | Duty Cycle percentage | 0% – 100% |
| 2 | $V_{avg} = D \times V_{supply}$ | Average armature voltage | 0 – 12 V |
| 3 | $N_{motor} \approx k \times V_{avg}$ | Motor speed (RPM) | Linear approximation |
| 4 | $T_{PWM} = (MR0 + 1) \times \dfrac{PR+1}{f_{PCLK}}$ | PWM Period in seconds | Depends on values |
| 5 | $D = \dfrac{MRx}{MR0+1} \times 100\%$ | Duty cycle from registers | Percentage |
| 6 | $f_{PWM} = \dfrac{f_{PCLK}}{(MR0+1)(PR+1)}$ | PWM Frequency in Hz | 1 kHz – 25 kHz |
| 7 | $f_{PCLK}$ | Peripheral clock (default) | **25 MHz** |
| 8 | $Resolution_{bits} = \log_2(MR0+1)$ | PWM resolution in bits | 8 to 12 bits typical |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Trick for KTU Lab Calculation:**
> Given: $f_{PCLK} = 25\text{ MHz}$, desired $f_{PWM} = 1\text{ kHz}$, prescale $PR = 0$
> 
> $$\therefore MR0 = \frac{f_{PCLK}}{f_{PWM} \times (PR+1)} - 1 = \frac{25 \times 10^6}{1000} - 1 = 24999$$
> 
> For 50% duty cycle: $MR1 = 12500$. For 25% duty cycle: $MR1 = 6250$. **Memorize this calculation!**

### 2.4 H-Bridge Direction Control Logic

The H-Bridge allows **bidirectional** current flow through the motor:

| IN1 (GPIO) | IN2 (GPIO) | Motor State |
|---|---|---|
| HIGH | LOW | **Forward Rotation** (CW) |
| LOW | HIGH | **Reverse Rotation** (CCW) |
| LOW | LOW | **Stop (Coast)** — motor freewheels |
| HIGH | HIGH | **Brake** — motor resists rotation |

> [!NOTE]
> **NEVER set IN1 = IN2 = HIGH** for extended periods in some driver ICs — this creates a short circuit (shoot-through). Always consult the L293D/L298N datasheet's truth table.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

PWM-based DC motor control is the **backbone of modern mechatronics**:

- **Robotics:** Wheel speed control in differential-drive robots.
- **Drones (Multicopters):** ESC (Electronic Speed Controllers) use high-frequency PWM (~50 Hz for hobby, ~500 Hz for racing).
- **Industrial Automation:** Conveyor belts, CNC spindle control.
- **Automotive:** Fuel injection pumps, electric vehicle traction motors.
- **Home Appliances:** Washing machine drum speed, ceiling fan regulators (modern BLDC versions).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 PWM Period & Duty Cycle Derivation (LPC1768)

The LPC1768 PWM peripheral uses a 32-bit **Timer Counter (TC)** that increments on every PCLK pulse (after prescaling). When TC matches MR0, it resets to 0 (if configured) and starts the next PWM cycle.

**Given:**
- $f_{PCLK} = 25 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$ (default peripheral clock on KTU LPC1768)
- Desired $f_{PWM} = 1 \text{ kHz}$ (1 millisecond period)
- Prescale Register $PR = 0$ (no division)

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{PCLK} &= \frac{1}{f_{PCLK}} = \frac{1}{25 \times 10^6} = 4 \times 10^{-8} \text{ s} = 40 \text{ ns} \\
\\
\text{Number of PCLK ticks per PWM period} &= \frac{T_{PWM}}{T_{PCLK}} = \frac{1/f_{PWM}}{1/f_{PCLK}} = \frac{f_{PCLK}}{f_{PWM}} \\
&= \frac{25 \times 10^6}{1 \times 10^3} = 25000 \text{ ticks} \\
\\
\therefore MR0 + 1 &= 25000 \\
\therefore MR0 &= 25000 - 1 = 24999
\end{aligned}
$$

**Duty cycle for, say, 50%:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= 50\% = 0.50 \\
MR1 &= D \times (MR0 + 1) = 0.50 \times 25000 = 12500
\end{aligned}
$$

**For 25% duty cycle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MR1 &= 0.25 \times 25000 = 6250
\end{aligned}
$$

**For 75% duty cycle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MR1 &= 0.75 \times 25000 = 18750
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Exhaustive C Program for LPC1768 (KEIL / Embedded C)

```c
/*============================================================
 * Experiment 2: DC Motor Direction and Speed Control using PWM
 * Target Board: LPC1768 (ARM Cortex-M3)
 * Compiler   : Keil µVision 5
 * Crystal    : 12 MHz external, PCLK = 25 MHz (default)
 * PWM Freq   : 1 kHz, PWM1 Channel 2 (P1.1 → PWM1.2)
 * H-Bridge   : L293D
 *   - IN1 = P0.10  (Direction control bit 1)
 *   - IN2 = P0.11  (Direction control bit 2)
 *   - EN1 = PWM1.2 (Speed control via PWM)
 * Motor Vcc  : External 12 V supply
 *============================================================*/

#include <LPC17xx.h>

/* ---------- Function Prototypes ---------- */
void PWM1_Init(void);
void PWM1_SetDutyCycle(unsigned int duty_percent);
void Motor_Forward(void);
void Motor_Reverse(void);
void Motor_Stop(void);
void Delay_ms(unsigned int ms);

/* ---------- Constants ---------- */
#define MR0_VALUE  24999U     // For 1 kHz PWM @ 25 MHz PCLK
#define FORWARD    0x01
#define REVERSE    0x02
#define STOP       0x00

/* ---------- Main Function ---------- */
int main(void)
{
    /* Step 1: Configure direction control GPIO pins (P0.10, P0.11) */
    LPC_GPIO0->FIODIR |= (1U << 10) | (1U << 11);   // Set as OUTPUT
    LPC_GPIO0->FIOCLR |= (1U << 10) | (1U << 11);   // Initialize both LOW

    /* Step 2: Initialize PWM1 Channel 2 */
    PWM1_Init();

    /* Step 3: Infinite demonstration loop */
    while (1)
    {
        /* --- Run motor FORWARD at varying speeds --- */
        Motor_Forward();
        PWM1_SetDutyCycle(25);   // 25% speed
        Delay_ms(2000);
        PWM1_SetDutyCycle(50);   // 50% speed
        Delay_ms(2000);
        PWM1_SetDutyCycle(75);   // 75% speed
        Delay_ms(2000);
        PWM1_SetDutyCycle(100);  // 100% (full) speed
        Delay_ms(2000);

        /* --- Run motor REVERSE at varying speeds --- */
        Motor_Reverse();
        PWM1_SetDutyCycle(25);
        Delay_ms(2000);
        PWM1_SetDutyCycle(50);
        Delay_ms(2000);
        PWM1_SetDutyCycle(75);
        Delay_ms(2000);
        PWM1_SetDutyCycle(100);
        Delay_ms(2000);

        /* --- Stop the motor --- */
        Motor_Stop();
        Delay_ms(2000);
    }
    return 0;  // Never reached
}

/*============================================================
 * Function: PWM1_Init
 * Purpose : Configure PWM1 Channel 2 on P1.1
 * Output  : 1 kHz PWM with 0% initial duty cycle
 *============================================================*/
void PWM1_Init(void)
{
    /* Step A: Power up the PWM1 peripheral (bit 6 in PCONP) */
    LPC_SC->PCONP |= (1U << 6);

    /* Step B: Select PCLK = CCLK/4 by default (already 25 MHz on KTU kits).
     *         PCLKSEL0 bits [13:12] = 01 (default) for PWM1 */

    /* Step C: Configure P1.1 pin function as PWM1.2 (PINSEL3 bits [3:2] = 01) */
    LPC_PINCON->PINSEL3 &= ~(3U << 2);   // Clear bits
    LPC_PINCON->PINSEL3 |=  (1U << 2);   // Set PWM1.2 function

    /* Step D: Reset and disable PWM timer counter */
    LPC_PWM1->TCR = (1U << 1);           // Bit 1 = Counter Reset

    /* Step E: Set prescale register to 0 (divide by 1) */
    LPC_PWM1->PR  = 0U;

    /* Step F: Set MR0 to define PWM period (1 kHz) */
    LPC_PWM1->MR0 = MR0_VALUE;           // 24999

    /* Step G: Set MR2 to 0 initially (0% duty cycle - motor stopped) */
    LPC_PWM1->MR2 = 0U;

    /* Step H: Match Control Register: Reset TC on MR0 match */
    LPC_PWM1->MCR = (1U << 1);           // Bit 1 = Reset on MR0

    /* Step I: PWM Control Register: Enable PWM1.2 output, single-edge mode */
    LPC_PWM1->PCR = (1U << 10);          // Bit 10 = PWM1.2 output enable
    // Bits 1,3,5,7,9 = 0 → Single edge mode (active HIGH)
    // Bits 2,4,6,8,10 = 1 → Enable outputs

    /* Step J: Latch the new MR0 and MR2 values */
    LPC_PWM1->LER = (1U << 0) | (1U << 2);

    /* Step K: Enable PWM timer and counter (clear reset, set enable) */
    LPC_PWM1->TCR = (1U << 0) | (1U << 3);  // Bit 0 = Enable, Bit 3 = PWM enable
}

/*============================================================
 * Function: PWM1_SetDutyCycle
 * Input   : duty_percent (0 to 100)
 * Output  : Updates MR2 and latches it for PWM1.2
 *============================================================*/
void PWM1_SetDutyCycle(unsigned int duty_percent)
{
    unsigned long match_value;

    if (duty_percent > 100) duty_percent = 100;

    /* Calculate MR2 value:
     *   MR2 = (duty_percent / 100) * (MR0 + 1)
     *        = (duty_percent / 100) * 25000
     */
    match_value = (duty_percent * (MR0_VALUE + 1U)) / 100U;

    LPC_PWM1->MR2 = match_value;          // Update duty cycle
    LPC_PWM1->LER = (1U << 2);            // Latch MR2
}

/*============================================================
 * Function: Motor_Forward  →  IN1=HIGH, IN2=LOW
 *============================================================*/
void Motor_Forward(void)
{
    LPC_GPIO0->FIOSET = (1U << 10);       // IN1 = HIGH
    LPC_GPIO0->FIOCLR = (1U << 11);       // IN2 = LOW
}

/*============================================================
 * Function: Motor_Reverse  →  IN1=LOW, IN2=HIGH
 *============================================================*/
void Motor_Reverse(void)
{
    LPC_GPIO0->FIOCLR = (1U << 10);       // IN1 = LOW
    LPC_GPIO0->FIOSET = (1U << 11);       // IN2 = HIGH
}

/*============================================================
 * Function: Motor_Stop  →  IN1=LOW, IN2=LOW (Coast)
 *============================================================*/
void Motor_Stop(void)
{
    LPC_GPIO0->FIOCLR = (1U << 10) | (1U << 11);
    PWM1_SetDutyCycle(0);                 // Also set PWM to 0 for safety
}

/*============================================================
 * Function: Delay_ms (approximate software delay)
 *============================================================*/
void Delay_ms(unsigned int ms)
{
    unsigned int i, j;
    for (i = 0; i < ms; i++)
        for (j = 0; j < 6000; j++);       // Tuned for 25 MHz
}
```

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Register-by-Register Analysis

| Step | Register / Pin | Value Set | Reason / Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `LPC_SC->PCONP` | Set bit 6 = 1 | Powers on PWM1 peripheral block |
| 2 | `LPC_PINCON->PINSEL3` | Bits [3:2] = 01 | Routes P1.1 to PWM1.2 function |
| 3 | `LPC_PWM1->TCR` | `0x02` → then `0x09` | First resets, then enables counter + PWM mode |
| 4 | `LPC_PWM1->PR` | `0x00000000` | No prescaling — PCLK directly drives TC |
| 5 | `LPC_PWM1->MR0` | `24999` | Defines 1 kHz PWM period |
| 6 | `LPC_PWM1->MR2` | Variable (0–25000) | Defines duty cycle for Channel 2 |
| 7 | `LPC_PWM1->MCR` | `0x02` | Resets TC on MR0 match (creates repeating waveform) |
| 8 | `LPC_PWM1->PCR` | `0x0400` (bit 10) | Enables PWM1.2 output in single-edge mode |
| 9 | `LPC_PWM1->LER` | Bit 0 + Bit 2 | Latches new MR0 and MR2 into shadow registers |
| 10 | `LPC_GPIO0->FIODIR` | Bits 10, 11 = OUTPUT | Configures direction pins |
| 11 | `LPC_GPIO0->FIOSET / FIOCLR` | Toggle bits 10, 11 | Sets motor rotation direction |

### 3.4 Expected Output Verification

When the program executes correctly:

| Test Point | Expected Observation |
|---|---|
| **DSO/CRO on PWM1.2 (P1.1)** | 1 kHz square wave, 0%–100% duty cycle varying as per code |
| **DSO/CRO on L293D output** | Same waveform but at motor's supply voltage (e.g., 12 V) |
| **Motor shaft visual** | Spins forward (CW) → accelerates → decelerates → stops → reverses (CCW) |
| **Tachometer (if available)** | RPM increases linearly with duty cycle |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Lab Viva Quick-Answer:** *"Why do we use the Latch Enable Register (LER)?"*
> **Answer:** MR0 and MR1–MR6 are **double-buffered**. Changes are not applied immediately to avoid glitches mid-cycle. LER acts as a "commit" button — the new values are copied to the working shadow registers only when LER's corresponding bit is set, and they take effect at the **next TC reset** (start of new PWM period).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power-On Reset] --> B[Initialize System Clock 25 MHz]
    B --> C[Configure P0.10 and P0.11 as GPIO Output for H-Bridge Direction Lines]
    C --> D[Power Up PWM1 Peripheral via PCONP]
    D --> E[Configure P1.1 as PWM1.2 Output via PINSEL3]
    E --> F[Set PWM Prescale PR to 0]
    F --> G[Load MR0 equals 24999 for 1 kHz Period]
    G --> H[Configure MCR to Reset TC on MR0 Match]
    H --> I[Enable PWM1.2 Output in PCR Bit 10]
    I --> J[Latch MR0 and MR2 via LER]
    J --> K[Enable Counter and PWM Mode in TCR]
    K --> L[Enter Infinite Loop]
    L --> M[Set Direction GPIO to FORWARD or REVERSE]
    M --> N[Call PWM1 SetDutyCycle with 0 to 100]
    N --> O[Latch New MR2 via LER]
    O --> P[PWM1.2 Pin Generates Variable Duty Cycle Signal]
    P --> Q[L293D H-Bridge Amplifies Signal to 12V Level]
    Q --> R[DC Motor Spins at Proportional Speed in Set Direction]
    R --> L
```

### 4.2 H-Bridge Motor Driver Schematic (L293D)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MCU_PWM[P1.1 PWM1.2 Output] -->|1 kHz PWM Signal| L293D_IN1
    MCU_DIR1[P0.10 GPIO] -->|Logic HIGH or LOW| L293D_IN2
    MCU_DIR2[P0.11 GPIO] -->|Logic HIGH or LOW| L293D_IN3
    L293D_IN1[L293D Enable 1] --> H1[H-Bridge Left Switch Pair]
    L293D_IN2[L293D Input 1A] --> H1
    L293D_IN3[L293D Input 2A] --> H2[H-Bridge Right Switch Pair]
    H1 --> M[DC Motor Terminal A]
    H2 --> M
    M --> M2[DC Motor Terminal B]
    VCC[External 12V Supply] --> L293D_V[VS Pin of L293D]
    L293D_V --> H1
    L293D_V --> H2
    GND[Common Ground] --> L293D_G[Ground Pins of L293D]
```

### 4.3 PWM Waveform Visual Representation (Duty Cycle Variations)

```mermaid
gantt
    title PWM Duty Cycle Variations on PWM1.2 Pin
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %S ms
    
    section 25 percent Duty Cycle
    ON   :a1, 0, 250us
    OFF  :a2, after a1, 750us
    
    section 50 percent Duty Cycle
    ON2  :b1, 0, 500us
    OFF2 :b2, after b1, 500us
    
    section 75 percent Duty Cycle
    ON3  :c1, 0, 750us
    OFF3 :c2, after c1, 250us
    
    section 100 percent Duty Cycle
    ON4  :d1, 0, 1000us
```

### 4.4 Software Execution State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Init
    Init: System and PWM Initialization
    Init --> ForwardRun
    ForwardRun: Motor_Forward + Vary Duty 25 to 50 to 75 to 100
    ForwardRun --> ReverseRun
    ReverseRun: Motor_Reverse + Vary Duty 25 to 50 to 75 to 100
    ReverseRun --> StopState
    StopState: Motor_Stop + 0 percent Duty Cycle
    StopState --> ForwardRun
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The above flowcharts describe the **functional architecture** of the firmware and hardware interface. They are not literal circuit schematics but are designed to give a KTU 2024 examiner a clean, professional visual representation of the system's data flow, exactly as expected in lab record submissions.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Define PWM. Write the formula relating the average voltage across a DC motor to the supply voltage and duty cycle.*

**Model Answer:**

**PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** is a digital modulation technique in which the width of a rectangular pulse is varied while keeping its period constant. It is used to control the average power delivered to a load such as a DC motor.

The average voltage formula is:

$$V_{avg} = D \times V_{supply}$$

where $D$ is the duty cycle expressed as a decimal. If $V_{supply} = 12 \text{ V}$ and $D = 0.6$ (60%), then $V_{avg} = 7.2 \text{ V}$, causing the motor to run at approximately 60% of its rated speed.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*List any four registers of the LPC1768 PWM peripheral and state their function.*

**Model Answer:**

| Register | Function |
|---|---|
| **PWM1TCR** | Timer Control Register — enables/disables the PWM counter and PWM mode |
| **PWM1MR0** | Match Register 0 — defines the PWM period (reset value for TC) |
| **PWM1MR1–MR6** | Match Registers 1–6 — define duty cycle for PWM1.1 to PWM1.6 |
| **PWM1PCR** | PWM Control Register — enables individual PWM outputs and selects single/double edge mode |
| **PWM1LER** | Latch Enable Register — latches new MRx values into shadow registers |
| **PWM1MCR** | Match Control Register — configures reset/interrupt/stop on MR0 match |

*(Any four correctly described registers fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 2 Lab]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the interfacing of a DC motor to the LPC1768 microcontroller using an L293D H-Bridge driver for PWM-based speed and direction control. (7 marks)

**(b)** An LPC1768 system uses PCLK = 25 MHz. Compute the value of MR0 required to generate a PWM frequency of 2 kHz with a prescaler value of 0. Also calculate the MR1 value for 40% duty cycle. Write the relevant C code snippet to initialize and update this PWM. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Block Diagram & Interfacing Explanation (7 marks):**

The DC motor is interfaced to the LPC1768 through the **L293D dual H-Bridge driver IC**. The connections are:

1. **PWM Signal (Speed Control):** The PWM1.2 output of LPC1768 on pin **P1.1** is fed to the **EN1 (Enable 1)** pin of L293D. The PWM duty cycle controls the average voltage applied to the motor.

2. **Direction Control (GPIO):** Two GPIO pins **P0.10** and **P0.11** of LPC1768 are connected to the **IN1 (1A)** and **IN2 (2A)** logic input pins of L293D.
   - When IN1 = HIGH and IN2 = LOW → current flows one way → **Forward rotation**.
   - When IN1 = LOW and IN2 = HIGH → current reverses → **Reverse rotation**.

3. **Power Supply:** The L293D has **two supply pins**:
   - **VSS (Logic supply):** +5 V from LPC1768 board.
   - **VS (Motor supply):** External 12 V for the motor (separate high-current source).

4. **Output:** The **OUT1 and OUT2** pins of L293D drive the two terminals of the DC motor.

5. **Protection:** **Free-wheeling diodes** (built into L293D) protect the IC from back-EMF generated by the motor's inductance during switching.

**Block Diagram (text representation):**

```
  LPC1768                     L293D                  DC Motor
+-----------+              +-----------+          +-----------+
|  P1.1     |----PWM1.2----|EN1    OUT1|----------|   M+      |
|  (PWM)    |              |        OUT2|----------|   M-      |
+-----------+              |           |          +-----------+
|  P0.10    |------HIGH/LOW|IN1        |
|  (DIR1)   |              |           |
+-----------+              |           |
|  P0.11    |------HIGH/LOW|IN2        |
|  (DIR2)   |              |           |
+-----------+              +-----------+
                                  |
                            VS = +12 V (Motor)
                            VSS = +5 V (Logic)
```

**(b) Numerical Computation & Code (7 marks):**

**Given:** $f_{PCLK} = 25 \text{ MHz}$, $f_{PWM} = 2 \text{ kHz}$, $PR = 0$, $D = 40\%$

**Step 1: Calculate MR0**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{PWM} &= \frac{f_{PCLK}}{(MR0+1)(PR+1)} \\
2 \times 10^3 &= \frac{25 \times 10^6}{(MR0+1)(0+1)} \\
MR0 + 1 &= \frac{25 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^3} = 12500 \\
\therefore MR0 &= 12499
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate MR1 for 40% duty cycle**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MR1 &= \frac{D}{100} \times (MR0+1) = \frac{40}{100} \times 12500 = 5000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: C Code Snippet**

```c
void PWM1_Init_2kHz(void)
{
    LPC_SC->PCONP |= (1U << 6);          // Power up PWM1
    LPC_PINCON->PINSEL3 |= (1U << 2);    // P1.1 as PWM1.2
    LPC_PWM1->TCR  = (1U << 1);          // Reset counter
    LPC_PWM1->PR   = 0U;                  // No prescaling
    LPC_PWM1->MR0  = 12499U;              // 2 kHz period
    LPC_PWM1->MR1  = 5000U;               // 40% duty cycle
    LPC_PWM1->MCR  = (1U << 1);          // Reset TC on MR0
    LPC_PWM1->PCR  = (1U << 10);         // Enable PWM1.2 output
    LPC_PWM1->LER  = (1U << 0) | (1U << 1);  // Latch MR0 and MR1
    LPC_PWM1->TCR  = (1U << 0) | (1U << 3);  // Enable counter and PWM
}
```

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 2 Lab]**

**(a)** Explain the working of an H-Bridge circuit for DC motor direction control. Draw its circuit diagram and truth table. Why are free-wheeling diodes necessary? (7 marks)

**(b)** Differentiate between single-edge and double-edge PWM modes in LPC1768. Write a C program to generate a 5 kHz PWM signal on PWM1.3 with 60% duty cycle, assuming PCLK = 25 MHz. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution:

**(a) H-Bridge Working (7 marks):**

An **H-Bridge** consists of four switching elements (transistors or MOSFETs) arranged in an "H" configuration, with the DC motor forming the horizontal bar of the H. By selectively turning on diagonally opposite pairs of switches, the polarity of voltage applied to the motor can be reversed, achieving bidirectional rotation.

**Circuit Topology (ASCII representation):**

```
        +V (12V)
         |
       S1 (Q1)            S3 (Q3)
         |                  |
         +---- Motor ----+   |
         |     (M)      |   |
         |              |   |
         |              |   |
       S2 (Q2)            S4 (Q4)
         |                  |
        GND                GND
```

**Truth Table:**

| S1 | S2 | S3 | S4 | Motor State |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ON | OFF | OFF | ON | **Forward (CW)** |
| OFF | ON | ON | OFF | **Reverse (CCW)** |
| OFF | OFF | OFF | OFF | **Coast (Stop)** |
| ON | ON | OFF | OFF | **Brake (Dynamic)** |
| ON | ON | ON | ON | **SHORT CIRCUIT (Forbidden!)** |

**Free-wheeling Diodes Necessity:**

When a DC motor's current path is suddenly interrupted (e.g., switching from ON to OFF), the motor's inductance generates a large reverse-voltage spike called **back-EMF**. This can exceed 100 V and destroy the switching transistors. **Free-wheeling diodes** (placed anti-parallel to each transistor) provide a safe path for this inductive current to dissipate, protecting the H-Bridge.

**(b) Single-Edge vs Double-Edge PWM & Code (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Single-Edge PWM | Double-Edge PWM |
|---|---|---|
| PWM1PCR bit | Bit 1,3,5,7,9 = **0** | Bit 1,3,5,7,9 = **1** |
| Output transition | One edge per cycle (only rising or falling changes with duty) | Both edges (center-aligned) |
| Use case | Standard motor control, LEDs | Advanced motor control (better symmetry, reduced harmonics) |
| Match register | MRx compared with TC rising | TC = MRx on count-up **AND** TC = (MR0+1−MRx) on count-down |

**C Program for 5 kHz PWM on PWM1.3 with 60% duty cycle:**

```c
#include <LPC17xx.h>

int main(void)
{
    /* Compute MR0 for 5 kHz at PCLK = 25 MHz, PR = 0 */
    /* MR0 = (fPCLK / fPWM) - 1 = (25e6 / 5e3) - 1 = 4999 */
    /* MR3 (for PWM1.3) = 0.60 * (4999+1) = 3000 */

    LPC_SC->PCONP |= (1U << 6);                // Power up PWM1
    LPC_PINCON->PINSEL3 |= (1U << 4);          // P1.2 as PWM1.3
    LPC_PWM1->TCR = (1U << 1);                 // Reset counter
    LPC_PWM1->PR  = 0U;
    LPC_PWM1->MR0 = 4999U;                     // 5 kHz period
    LPC_PWM1->MR3 = 3000U;                     // 60% duty cycle
    LPC_PWM1->MCR = (1U << 1);                 // Reset TC on MR0
    LPC_PWM1->PCR = (1U << 11);                // Enable PWM1.3 output
    LPC_PWM1->LER = (1U << 0) | (1U << 3);     // Latch MR0, MR3
    LPC_PWM1->TCR = (1U << 0) | (1U << 3);     // Enable counter and PWM

    while (1) { /* PWM runs continuously */ }
    return 0;
}
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to latch via LER:** Many students update MR0/MRx but forget `PWM1->LER = (1 << x)`. The PWM never changes duty cycle. **−3 marks.**
> 2. **Wrong pin function in PINSEL:** Setting PINSEL3 bits incorrectly (e.g., for P1.1 use bits [3:2] = 01, NOT [2:3]). The PWM output never appears. **−2 marks.**
> 3. **TCR reset vs enable confusion:** Writing only `TCR = 0x02` (reset) but never writing `TCR = 0x09` (enable) keeps the counter stuck at zero. **−3 marks.**
> 4. **MR0 computation error:** Off-by-one mistake (using $f_{PCLK}/f_{PWM}$ directly instead of subtracting 1). **−2 marks.**
> 5. **No free-wheeling diode discussion:** In H-Bridge questions, omitting diode explanation costs 2–3 marks almost every time.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 15 Golden Points You MUST Memorize for KTU Lab Exam:**

1. **PWM Definition:** Pulse Width Modulation — varying duty cycle at constant frequency.
2. **Duty Cycle Formula:** $D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{period}} \times 100\%$
3. **Average Voltage:** $V_{avg} = D \times V_{supply}$
4. **MR0 Period Formula:** $MR0 = \dfrac{f_{PCLK}}{f_{PWM} \times (PR+1)} - 1$
5. **Duty Match Formula:** $MRx = \dfrac{D}{100} \times (MR0 + 1)$
6. **LPC1768 PCLK Default:** **25 MHz** (after PLL on KTU kits).
7. **H-Bridge Forward:** IN1=HIGH, IN2=LOW.
8. **H-Bridge Reverse:** IN1=LOW, IN2=HIGH.
9. **H-Bridge Forbidden State:** IN1=IN2=HIGH (shoot-through) — except for brake mode in L298N.
10. **LER Purpose:** Double-buffered latch for glitch-free duty cycle updates.
11. **PCR Bit Position:** PWM1.x output enable is at bit $(2x+8)$ for channels 1–6.
12. **TCR Enable Bits:** Bit 0 = TC enable, Bit 3 = PWM mode enable. **Both needed.**
13. **MCR Reset Bit:** Bit 1 = Reset TC when TC matches MR0 (creates repeating waveform).
14. **PWM Frequency Range for Motors:** **1 kHz – 25 kHz** (avoid audible noise).
15. **Driver IC Choice:** **L293D** (600 mA, KTU default) or **L298N** (2 A, for high-power motors).

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** *"**P**ins **P**ower **P**rescale **P**eriod **M**atch **P**WM **L**atch **E**nable"*
> = **P**INSEL → **P**CONP → **P**R → **P**eriod (MR0) → **M**CR (reset) → **P**CR (output enable) → **L**ER (latch) → **E**nable (TCR)
> Memorize this 7-step initialization sequence — it appears in **every KTU lab exam** related to PWM!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: C Program for Alphanumerical LCD Panel/Keyboard Interface
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL508 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** C Program for Alphanumerical LCD Panel/Keyboard Interface

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Alphanumerical LCD Panel (16×2 Character LCD)

An **Alphanumerical LCD Panel** is a flat-panel electronic visual display that uses **Liquid Crystal Technology** to render ASCII characters, numbers, and a limited set of custom symbols. The most common form-factor used in KTU 8051/ARM/PIC laboratory experiments is the **HD44780-compatible 16×2 LCD**, which can display **16 characters per row across 2 rows** (i.e., a total of 32 characters), with each character built from a **5×8 dot-matrix** pattern.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Conceptual Mapping):**
> The alphanumerical LCD panel is a peripheral output device interfaced to the microcontroller through a parallel data bus (typically 4-bit or 8-bit mode) using standard control lines — **RS (Register Select), R/W (Read/Write), and E (Enable)** — and is used to display real-time system status, sensor values, and user prompts in embedded applications.

### Physical & Electrical Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Operating Voltage (Vdd) | **+4.5 V to +5.5 V** |
| Logic HIGH voltage (VIH) | **≥ 2.2 V** |
| Logic LOW voltage (VIL) | **≤ 0.6 V** |
| Display Characters | **16 × 2 = 32 characters** |
| Character Font | **5 × 8 dot matrix** (5 columns × 8 rows) |
| Data Interface Width | **4-bit or 8-bit** |
| Operating Current | **~1.5 mA (typical), 2.5 mA (max)** |
| Backlight Current | **~120 mA** (LED backlight) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **HD44780 controller** is the de-facto industry standard. Every LCD module used in KTU labs (JHD162A, LMB162A, etc.) contains this controller or a pin-compatible clone, guaranteeing software portability.

---

## 1.2 Matrix Keyboard (4×4 Hex Keypad)

A **4×4 Matrix Keyboard** is an input peripheral constructed from **16 mechanical tactile push-buttons** arranged in a grid of **4 rows and 4 columns**. Unlike direct-switch input, the matrix arrangement uses a **scanning technique** to identify the pressed key, reducing the I/O pin consumption from **16 pins (direct)** to just **8 pins (4 Rows + 4 Columns)**.

### Key Layout (Standard KTU 8051 Lab Keypad)

```
        Col0   Col1   Col2   Col3
Row0  [  1  ] [  2 ] [  3 ] [  A ]
Row1  [  4  ] [  5 ] [  6 ] [  B ]
Row2  [  7  ] [  8 ] [  9 ] [  C ]
Row3  [  *  ] [  0 ] [  # ] [  D ]
```

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — "The Library Notice Board & Suggestion Box":**
> - The **LCD Panel** behaves like a **Notice Board** outside a librarian's office. It only *displays* information (output); it never *accepts* information from visitors. The librarian (the **microcontroller**) pins new notices (writes ASCII codes) onto the board via the data bus whenever needed.
> - The **Matrix Keyboard** behaves like a **Suggestion Box with a Pre-Printed Stamp Sheet**. It contains 16 stamps arranged in a grid. The librarian does not stand there waiting for visitors; instead, he periodically *flips a row-high voltage* and *reads the columns*. When a column reads HIGH, he knows *which row* was active and *which column* was pressed — uniquely identifying the key.
> - **Combined System**: When you type a PIN on an ATM, the keypad is the *Suggestion Box* (input) and the LCD is the *Notice Board* (output). The microcontroller acts as the **librarian** coordinating both.

---

## 1.4 Why Interface Both Together?

In almost every embedded product (washing machines, microwave ovens, industrial controllers, medical pumps), the LCD provides **visual feedback** while the keyboard provides **user control**. The combined C program in KTU Module Experiment 2 demonstrates **bi-directional human-machine interaction (HMI)** — the cornerstone of any embedded UI.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 HD44780 LCD — Pin Architecture

The LCD module has **16 pins** (14 data/control + 2 for backlight power).

| Pin No. | Symbol | Name | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | VSS | Ground | **0 V** reference |
| 2 | VDD | Power Supply | **+5 V** DC |
| 3 | VEE | Contrast Adjust | Wiper of 10 kΩ pot between VDD & VSS |
| 4 | RS | Register Select | **0 = Command Reg**, **1 = Data Reg** |
| 5 | R/W | Read/Write | **0 = Write to LCD**, **1 = Read from LCD** |
| 6 | E | Enable | Latch data on **HIGH → LOW falling edge** |
| 7–10 | DB0–DB3 | Lower Data Bus | Used in **8-bit mode only** |
| 11–14 | DB4–DB7 | Upper Data Bus | Used in **4-bit mode** (only DB4–DB7) |
| 15 | LED+ | Backlight Anode | **+5 V** via 220 Ω resistor |
| 16 | LED- | Backlight Cathode | **GND** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 8051 lab kits, the LCD is **almost always connected in 4-bit mode** to conserve Port pins. This means only **DB4, DB5, DB6, DB7** are physically wired, and the upper nibble of every byte is transmitted first, followed by the lower nibble.

---

## 2.2 LCD Internal Registers — The Two Registers

The HD44780 has **two internal 8-bit registers** selected by the **RS pin**:

- **Command Register (RS = 0):** Accepts instructions like *Clear Display*, *Cursor Home*, *Shift*, *Function Set*.
- **Data Register (RS = 1):** Accepts the **ASCII code** of the character to be displayed at the current cursor position.

---

## 2.3 HD44780 Command Set (KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| Hex Code | Binary (DB7..DB0) | Instruction | Execution Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| **0x01** | 0000 0001 | Clear Display | **1.53 ms** |
| **0x02** | 0000 0010 | Return Cursor Home | **1.53 ms** |
| **0x04** | 0000 0100 | Decrement Cursor (Shift Left) | **39 µs** |
| **0x06** | 0000 0110 | Increment Cursor (Shift Right) — *Default* | **39 µs** |
| **0x05** | 0000 0101 | Shift Display Right | **39 µs** |
| **0x07** | 0000 0111 | Shift Display Left | **39 µs** |
| **0x08** | 0000 1000 | Display OFF, Cursor OFF | **39 µs** |
| **0x0A** | 0000 1010 | Display OFF, Cursor ON | **39 µs** |
| **0x0C** | 0000 1100 | Display ON, Cursor OFF | **39 µs** |
| **0x0E** | 0000 1110 | Display ON, Cursor ON | **39 µs** |
| **0x0F** | 0000 1111 | Display ON, Cursor Blinking | **39 µs** |
| **0x10** | 0001 XXXX | Move Cursor Left by N positions | **39 µs** |
| **0x14** | 0001 XXXX | Move Cursor Right by N positions | **39 µs** |
| **0x18** | 0001 XXXX | Shift Entire Display Left | **39 µs** |
| **0x1C** | 0001 XXXX | Shift Entire Display Right | **39 µs** |
| **0x20** | 0010 NFxx | 4-bit, 1-line, 5×8 (Function Set) | **39 µs** |
| **0x28** | 0010 NFxx | **4-bit, 2-line, 5×8** — *Default* | **39 µs** |
| **0x30** | 0011 NFxx | 8-bit, 1-line, 5×8 | **39 µs** |
| **0x38** | 0011 NFxx | **8-bit, 2-line, 5×8** | **39 µs** |
| **0x80** | 1xxx xxxx | Force Cursor to Beginning of Line 1 | **39 µs** |
| **0xC0** | 11xx xxxx | Force Cursor to Beginning of Line 2 | **39 µs** |

> [!NOTE]
> **The DDRAM Address Map** — *critical for KTU exams:*
> - **Line 1:** `0x00` to `0x0F` (DDRAM addresses 0–15)
> - **Line 2:** `0x40` to `0x4F` (DDRAM addresses 64–79)
> - To jump to Line 2 Column 5, send the command `0xC0 + 4 = 0xC4`.

---

## 2.4 LCD Initialization Sequence (4-bit Mode — KTU Standard)

The HD44780 boots in **8-bit mode** internally. To switch to 4-bit mode, the following mandatory wake-up sequence is executed:

1. Wait for **≥ 15 ms** after VDD rises to 4.5 V.
2. Send command **0x30** (8-bit Function Set). Wait 4.1 ms.
3. Send command **0x30** again. Wait 100 µs.
4. Send command **0x30** again. Wait 100 µs.
5. Send command **0x20** — *this is the magic command that switches the LCD to 4-bit mode*.
6. Now send **0x28** (Function Set: 4-bit, 2-line, 5×8 font).
7. Send **0x0C** (Display ON, Cursor OFF).
8. Send **0x06** (Entry Mode: Increment cursor).
9. Send **0x01** (Clear Display) — wait 1.53 ms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Steps 2–5 must be sent as **single-byte commands** (upper nibble only), because at this point the LCD is *still listening in 8-bit mode*. After step 5, *every subsequent byte* must be transmitted as **two 4-bit nibbles**.

---

## 2.5 Matrix Keypad Scanning Theory

The 4×4 keypad uses a **row-column scanning algorithm**:

- **All 4 rows are configured as OUTPUT (initially HIGH or LOW).**
- **All 4 columns are configured as INPUT.**
- **Phase 1 (Row Scanning):** Drive one row LOW at a time; read the columns.
  - If any column reads **LOW**, the key at (row, column) is pressed.
- **Phase 2 (Column Scanning — alternate method):** Drive one column LOW at a time; read the rows.

### 2.5.1 Key-Identification Formula

Let the pressed key be located at row index $r$ (0 to 3) and column index $c$ (0 to 3). The **ASCII value** to display is obtained from a lookup table `keymap[r][c]`.

$$k_{ascii} = \text{keymap}[r][c]$$

The standard 4×4 keymap used in KTU labs:

$$
\text{keymap} = \begin{bmatrix}
'1' & '2' & '3' & 'A' \\
'4' & '5' & '6' & 'B' \\
'7' & '8' & '9' & 'C' \\
'*' & '0' & '\#' & 'D'
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### 2.5.2 Debounce Delay Constant

Mechanical key contacts *bounce* for approximately **5 ms to 20 ms** after actuation. The KTU-standard debounce constant is:

$$t_{debounce} = \mathbf{10 \text{ ms}} \;\; (\text{or higher, up to } \mathbf{50 \text{ ms}})$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often set debounce to **100 µs** (the LCD delay). This is **100× too small** and results in a single keypress producing multiple ghost characters on the LCD.

---

## 2.6 Port-Pin Allocation Reference (KTU 8051 Standard)

| Signal | 8051 Pin | Port |
|---|---|---|
| LCD RS | P3.0 | **P3** |
| LCD R/W | P3.1 | **P3** |
| LCD E | P3.2 | **P3** |
| LCD DB4–DB7 | P2.0–P2.3 | **P2** |
| Keypad Row0–Row3 | P1.0–P1.3 | **P1** |
| Keypad Col0–Col3 | P1.4–P1.7 | **P1** |

> [!NOTE]
> Port allocation may vary by lab kit. The **C code structure** remains identical — only the `sbit` declarations and port-mask macros need to be updated.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step C Code, Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Complete C Program — LCD + Keypad Interfacing (8051 Keil C)

> [!NOTE]
> The following C program is the **KTU Board Exam reference answer** for Module Experiment 2. It uses the standard **8051 Keil C** syntax with `<reg51.h>`. The program displays the pressed key on a 16×2 LCD. Every function is fully derived and explained in the subsections that follow.

```c
#include <reg51.h>

/* ---------- LCD Pin Definitions (4-bit mode) ---------- */
sbit RS  = P3^0;        /* Register Select  */
sbit RW  = P3^1;        /* Read/Write       */
sbit EN  = P3^2;        /* Enable           */
#define LCD_DATA  P2    /* Upper nibble P2.0-P2.3 -> DB4-DB7 */

/* ---------- Keypad Pin Definitions ---------- */
#define KEYPAD  P1
sbit R0 = P1^0;  sbit R1 = P1^1;  sbit R2 = P1^2;  sbit R3 = P1^3;
sbit C0 = P1^4;  sbit C1 = P1^5;  sbit C2 = P1^6;  sbit C3 = P1^7;

/* ---------- Keymap Lookup Table ---------- */
unsigned char keymap[4][4] = {
    {'1','2','3','A'},
    {'4','5','6','B'},
    {'7','8','9','C'},
    {'*','0','#','D'}
};

/* ---------- Delay Function ---------- */
void delay_ms(unsigned int ms) {
    unsigned int i, j;
    for (i = 0; i < ms; i++)
        for (j = 0; j < 120; j++);   /* 1 ms at 11.0592 MHz */
}

/* ---------- LCD Pulse Enable ---------- */
void LCD_Enable(void) {
    EN = 1;
    delay_ms(1);
    EN = 0;
    delay_ms(2);
}

/* ---------- Send 4-bit nibble to LCD ---------- */
void LCD_SendNibble(unsigned char nib) {
    LCD_DATA = (LCD_DATA & 0x0F) | (nib & 0xF0);
    LCD_Enable();
}

/* ---------- Send Command to LCD ---------- */
void LCD_Cmd(unsigned char cmd) {
    RS = 0;   /* Command Register */
    RW = 0;   /* Write            */
    LCD_SendNibble(cmd & 0xF0);          /* Upper nibble */
    LCD_SendNibble((cmd << 4) & 0xF0);   /* Lower nibble */
    if (cmd == 0x01 || cmd == 0x02)
        delay_ms(2);                     /* Clear / Home = 1.53 ms */
    else
        delay_ms(1);
}

/* ---------- Send Data (Character) to LCD ---------- */
void LCD_Char(unsigned char ch) {
    RS = 1;   /* Data Register */
    RW = 0;   /* Write         */
    LCD_SendNibble(ch & 0xF0);
    LCD_SendNibble((ch << 4) & 0xF0);
    delay_ms(1);
}

/* ---------- Send String to LCD ---------- */
void LCD_String(unsigned char *str) {
    while (*str)
        LCD_Char(*str++);
}

/* ---------- LCD Initialization (4-bit mode) ---------- */
void LCD_Init(void) {
    delay_ms(20);          /* Power-on wait */
    LCD_SendNibble(0x30);  delay_ms(5);
    LCD_SendNibble(0x30);  delay_ms(1);
    LCD_SendNibble(0x30);  delay_ms(1);
    LCD_SendNibble(0x20);  /* Switch to 4-bit */
    LCD_Cmd(0x28);         /* 4-bit, 2-line, 5x8 */
    LCD_Cmd(0x0C);         /* Display ON, Cursor OFF */
    LCD_Cmd(0x06);         /* Increment cursor */
    LCD_Cmd(0x01);         /* Clear display */
    delay_ms(2);
}

/* ---------- Keypad Scan Function ---------- */
unsigned char Keypad_GetKey(void) {
    unsigned char row, col;
    /* Step 1: Make all rows HIGH, read columns */
    KEYPAD = 0x0F;             /* Rows HIGH, Cols as input */
    delay_ms(10);              /* Debounce */

    /* Step 2: Check if any key is pressed */
    if ((KEYPAD & 0xF0) == 0xF0)  /* No column LOW */
        return 0;                  /* No key */

    /* Step 3: Scan row by row */
    for (row = 0; row < 4; row++) {
        KEYPAD = ~(1 << row) & 0x0F;  /* Drive one row LOW */
        KEYPAD |= 0xF0;               /* Keep columns as input */
        delay_ms(1);
        col = (~KEYPAD >> 4) & 0x0F;  /* Read inverted column */
        if (col != 0x0F) {
            /* Key found at (row, col) */
            while ((KEYPAD & 0xF0) != 0xF0);  /* Wait for release */
            return keymap[row][col];
        }
    }
    return 0;
}

/* ---------- Main Program ---------- */
void main(void) {
    unsigned char key;
    LCD_Init();
    LCD_String("KEY PRESSED:");
    LCD_Cmd(0xC0);   /* Move cursor to Line 2 */

    while (1) {
        key = Keypad_GetKey();
        if (key != 0) {
            LCD_Char(key);
        }
    }
}
```

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation of the Key Scanning Algorithm

### 3.2.1 Step 1 — Initialise the Keypad Port

The upper nibble (P1.4–P1.7) is configured as **input** by writing **1s** to those pins (8051 convention: write 1 to make a pin input). The lower nibble is set HIGH initially so that *no row is active LOW*.

$$\text{KEYPAD} = 0x0F = 0000 \, 1111_2$$

| P1.7 | P1.6 | P1.5 | P1.4 | P1.3 | P1.2 | P1.1 | P1.0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| C3 | C2 | C1 | C0 | R3 | R2 | R1 | R0 |
| 1 (IN) | 1 (IN) | 1 (IN) | 1 (IN) | 1 (HI) | 1 (HI) | 1 (HI) | 1 (HI) |

### 3.2.2 Step 2 — Detect Any Key Press

If **no key** is pressed, all 4 columns remain **HIGH** (pull-ups). The check is:

$$(P1 \;\&\; 0xF0) \;\stackrel{?}{=}\; 0xF0$$

If yes → return 0 (no key). If no → some key is pressed; proceed to row scanning.

### 3.2.3 Step 3 — Row-by-Row Scan

For each row $r$ from 0 to 3:

$$\text{KEYPAD}_{low\,nibble} = \lnot(1 \ll r) \;\&\; 0x0F$$

| $r$ | $\lnot(1 \ll r)$ | Row driven LOW |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | `0x0E = 1110` | **R0** |
| 1 | `0x0D = 1101` | **R1** |
| 2 | `0x0B = 1011` | **R2** |
| 3 | `0x07 = 0111` | **R3** |

When row $r$ is LOW and the key at column $c$ is pressed, the column $c$ line is **shorted to ground** through the switch contact, so it reads **LOW**.

### 3.2.4 Step 4 — Read the Column

The column index is recovered by inverting P1 and masking the upper nibble:

$$c = \lnot(\text{KEYPAD}) \gg 4 \;\&\; 0x0F$$

For example, if `KEYPAD = 0xDF` (R1 LOW, C2 LOW):
$$\lnot(0xDF) = 0x20 = 0010 \, 0000_2$$
$$0x20 \gg 4 = 0x02 = 0000 \, 0010_2$$
$$0x02 \;\&\; 0x0F = 0x02 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; c = 2$$

### 3.2.5 Step 5 — Look Up the ASCII Value

$$k_{ascii} = \text{keymap}[1][2] = '6'$$

> [!NOTE]
> The `while ((KEYPAD & 0xF0) != 0xF0);` line is the **wait-for-key-release** guard. It prevents a single long press from being registered multiple times.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Function-by-Function Summary

| Function | Purpose | Critical Constant | Execution Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| `delay_ms()` | Generate millisecond delay | Inner loop = **120 iterations** (for 11.0592 MHz crystal) | Variable |
| `LCD_Enable()` | Latch data into LCD | EN pulse = **1 ms HIGH** | **3 ms** |
| `LCD_SendNibble()` | Send 4 bits | Bit-mask `0xF0` preserves lower P2 pins | **3 ms** |
| `LCD_Cmd()` | Send command byte | Two nibble transfers, RS=0, RW=0 | **5–7 ms** |
| `LCD_Char()` | Send data byte | Two nibble transfers, RS=1, RW=0 | **5 ms** |
| `LCD_String()` | Send null-terminated string | Loops until `'\0'` | `5ms × length` |
| `LCD_Init()` | 4-bit wake-up sequence | First nibble = **0x20** (mode switch) | **~30 ms** |
| `Keypad_GetKey()` | Identify pressed key | Debounce = **10 ms** | **~11 ms** |
| `main()` | Infinite loop | "KEY PRESSED:" + cursor to 0xC0 | Infinite |

---

## 3.4 Timing Analysis of One Full Display Cycle

Suppose the user presses the key **'5'** (located at row 1, col 1). The total time to display it on the LCD is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{total} &= T_{keyscan} + T_{lcd\_char} \\
T_{keyscan} &= T_{debounce} + 4 \times T_{rowscan} + T_{readcol} \\
&= 10\,ms + 4 \times 1\,ms + 1\,ms = 15\,ms \\
T_{lcd\_char} &= 2 \times T_{enable} + T_{delay} = 2 \times 3\,ms + 1\,ms = 7\,ms \\
T_{total} &= 15\,ms + 7\,ms = \mathbf{22\,ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

This means the system can register and display up to $\frac{1000}{22} \approx \mathbf{45}$ keys per second — well above human typing speed (≈ 5 keys/sec).

## 4.2 LCD Write Operation — Detailed Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Program calls LCD_Char or LCD_Cmd]) --> setRS{Command or\nData?}
    setRS -- "Command" --> rs0[RS = 0]
    setRS -- "Data" --> rs1[RS = 1]
    rs0 --> rw0a[RW = 0]
    rs1 --> rw0b[RW = 0]
    rw0a --> upper1[Send upper nibble on DB4-DB7]
    rw0b --> upper2[Send upper nibble on DB4-DB7]
    upper1 --> pulse1a[EN = 1, wait 1 ms, EN = 0]

    pulse1a --> lower1[Send lower nibble on DB4-DB7]
    pulse1b --> lower2[Send lower nibble on DB4-DB7]
    lower1 --> pulse2a[EN = 1, wait 1 ms, EN = 0]
    lower2 --> pulse2b[EN = 1, wait 1 ms, EN = 0]
    pulse2a --> dly1a[Wait command execution delay]
    pulse2b --> dly1b[Wait command execution delay]

    dly1b --> endA
```

## 4.3 Keypad Scan Algorithm — Row-by-Row State Machine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1([Start Keypad_GetKey]) --> init[KEYPAD = 0x0F\nAll rows HIGH, cols input]
    init --> deb[delay_ms 10\nDebounce wait]
    deb --> chk{(KEYPAD AND 0xF0) == 0xF0?}
    chk -- "Yes, no key" --> ret0[Return 0]
    chk -- "No, key detected" --> loop[For row = 0 to 3]
    loop --> drow[Drive current row LOW\nOther rows HIGH]
    drow --> rwait[delay_ms 1\nSettling time]
    rwait --> rcol[Read columns\ncol = NOT P1 SHIFT-RIGHT 4 AND 0x0F]
    rcol --> rchk{col not equal 0x0F?}
    rchk -- "No" --> loop
    rchk -- "Yes, key found" --> waitRel[Wait for key release\nwhile column not all 1s]
    waitRel --> retKey[Return keymap row col]
    classDef ok fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef wt fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#8B8B00,color:#000
    classDef stop fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    class init,deb,loop,drow,rwait,rcol,waitRel ok
    class chk,rchk wt
    class ret0,retKey stop
```

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram

    participant L as LCD Hardware

    Note over M: Keypad scan loop reaches row 1
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
*With a neat diagram, explain the interfacing of a 16×2 LCD with the 8051 microcontroller in 4-bit mode. List the signals used.**

**Model Answer:**

A 16×2 LCD module is interfaced to the 8051 using **6 signal lines** in 4-bit mode:
- **RS (Register Select)** — P3.0 — selects Command (0) or Data (1) register.
- **R/W (Read/Write)** — P3.1 — Write (0) or Read (1).
- **E (Enable)** — P3.2 — Latches data on the **HIGH-to-LOW falling edge**.
- **DB4, DB5, DB6, DB7** — P2.0–P2.3 — carry the upper nibble first, then the lower nibble.
- **VSS, VDD, VEE** — power (5 V) and contrast (via 10 kΩ potentiometer).
- **LED+, LED−** — backlight power.

In 4-bit mode, only DB4–DB7 are connected; each byte is transmitted as **two consecutive 4-bit nibbles**, saving 4 microcontroller pins. The LCD is initialised by sending 0x30 three times (to confirm 8-bit mode), then 0x20 to switch to 4-bit mode, followed by 0x28 (Function Set), 0x0C (Display ON), 0x06 (Entry Mode), 0x01 (Clear Display).

### **Question A2** (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
*Explain the row-column scanning technique used for a 4×4 matrix keypad.*

**Model Answer:**

A 4×4 matrix keypad uses **8 I/O lines** (4 rows + 4 columns) instead of 16 for 16 keys. Scanning is done in three steps:
1. **All 4 rows are driven HIGH** (or LOW) and columns are read as inputs.
2. **The microcontroller checks** if any column reads a value different from its idle state. If not, no key is pressed.
3. **Each row is driven LOW (or HIGH) one at a time**, and the columns are read after each row activation. The moment a column reads an active value, the key location (row, col) is uniquely identified.

A lookup table `keymap[row][col]` returns the corresponding ASCII character. A **debounce delay of 10 ms** is used to filter out the mechanical switch bounce. The technique reduces pin count by 50% and is the standard method in all KTU lab experiments.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice 1*

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of the HD44780 LCD controller. Explain the function of the RS, R/W, and E pins in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete 8051 C program to display the string **"KTU"** on the first line of a 16×2 LCD in 4-bit mode, then display the message **"EMBEDDED"** on the second line. Use a port of your choice. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) HD44780 Architecture and Pin Functions (7 Marks)**

The HD44780 controller contains three internal blocks:
- **Character Generator ROM (CGROM):** Stores the permanent **5×8 dot patterns** for 192 ASCII characters.
- **Character Generator RAM (CGRAM):** User-programmable memory for **8 custom characters**.
- **Display Data RAM (DDRAM):** 80-byte RAM (addresses 0x00–0x4F) holding the ASCII codes currently displayed.

Pin functions:
- **RS (Register Select, Pin 4):** A **logic LOW** (0) on RS directs data on DB0–DB7 to the **Command Register** (used for instructions like Clear, Home, Function Set). A **logic HIGH** (1) directs data to the **Data Register** (used to write ASCII characters to DDRAM).
- **R/W (Read/Write, Pin 5):** A **LOW** puts the LCD in **Write mode** (MCU → LCD). A **HIGH** puts the LCD in **Read mode** (LCD → MCU, used to read the busy flag).
- **E (Enable, Pin 6):** Data is **latched into the LCD** on the **HIGH-to-LOW falling edge** of this signal. The E pulse must be at least **450 ns** wide for 8-bit mode and **1 µs** for 4-bit mode.

**(b) Complete 8051 C Program (7 Marks)**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

sbit RS  = P3^0;
sbit RW  = P3^1;
sbit EN  = P3^2;
#define LCD P2

void delay_ms(unsigned int ms) {
    unsigned int i, j;
    for (i = 0; i < ms; i++)
        for (j = 0; j < 120; j++);
}

void pulse(void) {
    EN = 1; delay_ms(1); EN = 0; delay_ms(2);
}

void LCD_Nibble(unsigned char n) {
    LCD = (LCD & 0x0F) | (n & 0xF0);
    pulse();
}

void LCD_Cmd(unsigned char c) {
    RS = 0; RW = 0;
    LCD_Nibble(c & 0xF0);
    LCD_Nibble((c << 4) & 0xF0);
    delay_ms(2);
}

void LCD_Char(unsigned char ch) {
    RS = 1; RW = 0;
    LCD_Nibble(ch & 0xF0);
    LCD_Nibble((ch << 4) & 0xF0);
    delay_ms(1);
}

void LCD_String(unsigned char *s) {
    while (*s) LCD_Char(*s++);
}

void LCD_Init(void) {
    delay_ms(20);
    LCD_Nibble(0x30); delay_ms(5);
    LCD_Nibble(0x30); delay_ms(1);
    LCD_Nibble(0x30); delay_ms(1);
    LCD_Nibble(0x20);     /* Switch to 4-bit */
    LCD_Cmd(0x28);        /* 4-bit, 2-line, 5x8 */
    LCD_Cmd(0x0C);        /* Display ON */
    LCD_Cmd(0x06);        /* Increment mode */
    LCD_Cmd(0x01);        /* Clear */
    delay_ms(2);
}

void main(void) {
    LCD_Init();
    LCD_String("KTU");
    LCD_Cmd(0xC0);        /* Jump to Line 2 */
    LCD_String("EMBEDDED");
    while (1);
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in Board Valuation:**
> 1. Forgetting to send **0x20** during initialisation → LCD remains in 8-bit mode and all subsequent commands are interpreted wrongly.
> 2. Calling `delay_ms(1)` for the *Clear Display* command → LCD has not finished clearing when the next byte is sent, causing display corruption. The correct wait is **≥ 1.53 ms**.
> 3. Forgetting to set **RW = 0** before writing → LCD may output data on the bus, causing bus contention.

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice 2*

**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]`**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the hardware interfacing of a 4×4 matrix keypad to the 8051 microcontroller. Mention the role of pull-up resistors. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete 8051 C program to scan a 4×4 matrix keypad connected to **Port 1** and display the pressed key on a 16×2 LCD. Use 4-bit LCD mode. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) 4×4 Keypad Hardware Interfacing (7 Marks)**

The 4×4 keypad consists of 16 push-button switches arranged in a 4×4 grid. Each switch is at the intersection of one row and one column. The four row pins (R0–R3) and four column pins (C0–C3) are brought out to a single 8-pin connector.

**Connection to 8051 (Port 1):**
- Rows P1.0–P1.3 are configured as **output**.
- Columns P1.4–P1.7 are configured as **input**.

**Role of Pull-up Resistors (≈ 10 kΩ):**
Pull-up resistors connected between VCC and each column line ensure that:
1. When **no key is pressed**, the column lines read a **definitive logic HIGH** (no floating voltage).
2. **False triggering** due to noise is avoided.
3. When a switch closes (connecting a row to a column), it overrides the pull-up and pulls the column to the row's driven logic level.

Without pull-ups, the columns would be in a **floating (high-impedance) state**, leading to random garbage values being read by the microcontroller.

**(b) Complete C Program — Keypad + LCD (7 Marks)**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

sbit RS  = P3^0;
sbit RW  = P3^1;
sbit EN  = P3^2;
#define LCD P2
#define KP  P1

unsigned char keymap[4][4] = {
    {'1','2','3','A'}, {'4','5','6','B'},
    {'7','8','9','C'}, {'*','0','#','D'}
};

void delay_ms(unsigned int ms) {
    unsigned int i, j;
    for (i = 0; i < ms; i++)
        for (j = 0; j < 120; j++);
}

void pulse(void) {
    EN = 1; delay_ms(1); EN = 0; delay_ms(2);
}

void LCD_Nibble(unsigned char n) {
    LCD = (LCD & 0x0F) | (n & 0xF0);
    pulse();
}

void LCD_Cmd(unsigned char c) {
    RS = 0; RW = 0;
    LCD_Nibble(c & 0xF0);
    LCD_Nibble((c << 4) & 0xF0);
    delay_ms(2);
}

void LCD_Char(unsigned char ch) {
    RS = 1; RW = 0;
    LCD_Nibble(ch & 0xF0);
    LCD_Nibble((ch << 4) & 0xF0);
    delay_ms(1);
}

void LCD_String(unsigned char *s) {
    while (*s) LCD_Char(*s++);
}

void LCD_Init(void) {
    delay_ms(20);
    LCD_Nibble(0x30); delay_ms(5);
    LCD_Nibble(0x30); delay_ms(1);
    LCD_Nibble(0x30); delay_ms(1);
    LCD_Nibble(0x20);
    LCD_Cmd(0x28); LCD_Cmd(0x0C);
    LCD_Cmd(0x06); LCD_Cmd(0x01);
    delay_ms(2);
}

unsigned char Keypad_Scan(void) {
    unsigned char r, c;
    KP = 0x0F;
    delay_ms(10);
    if ((KP & 0xF0) == 0xF0) return 0;
    for (r = 0; r < 4; r++) {
        KP = (~(1 << r) & 0x0F) | 0xF0;
        delay_ms(1);
        c = ((~KP) >> 4) & 0x0F;
        if (c != 0x0F) {
            while ((KP & 0xF0) != 0xF0);
            return keymap[r][c];
        }
    }
    return 0;
}

void main(void) {
    unsigned char k;
    LCD_Init();
    LCD_String("KEY:");
    LCD_Cmd(0xC0);
    while (1) {
        k = Keypad_Scan();
        if (k != 0) LCD_Char(k);
    }
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in Board Valuation:**
> 1. **Missing debounce delay** (10 ms) → multiple characters appear for one keypress.
> 2. **No wait-for-release loop** → a single long press produces a stream of identical characters.
> 3. **Forgetting to set `RS = 1` in `LCD_Char`** → ASCII values are treated as commands, causing bizarre display behaviour.
> 4. **Wrong row logic** — students sometimes set the inactive row LOW instead of HIGH, causing **ghosting** (two keys appear pressed simultaneously).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorise Before Exam:**

- **LCD is 16×2** in KTU labs; **HD44780 controller** is the standard; **4-bit mode** uses only **DB4–DB7**.
- **RS = 0 → Command**, **RS = 1 → Data**, **R/W = 0 → Write**, **E = HIGH-to-LOW** latches data.
- **Initialization sequence:** three times `0x30` → one time `0x20` → `0x28` → `0x0C` → `0x06` → `0x01`.
- **Line 1 starts at DDRAM `0x00`**, **Line 2 starts at `0x40`**. Use `LCD_Cmd(0xC0)` to move to Line 2 start.
- **Clear Display (`0x01`)** takes **1.53 ms** — must wait at least 2 ms.
- **Keypad uses 4 rows + 4 columns = 8 pins**, scanned row-by-row.
- **Debounce delay = 10 ms** is the KTU standard.
- **Pull-up resistors (10 kΩ)** are mandatory on column lines to prevent floating inputs.
- **Keymap is a 4×4 2-D array** — row index = scanned row, column index = scanned column.
- **Wait-for-release** loop prevents repeated registrations from one long keypress.
- **Key pins**: `0x30, 0x30, 0x30, 0x20` are sent as **single nibbles** (not split), because the LCD is *still in 8-bit mode* during initialisation.
- **Timing constants**: Enable pulse ≥ 1 µs, command execution ≤ 1.53 ms, debounce ≈ 10 ms.
- **C code structure**: `#include <reg51.h>`, `sbit` for individual pins, `#define` for ports, lookup table for keys.
- **8-bit mode vs 4-bit mode**: 8-bit is faster but uses more pins (10 data/control + 2 power + 2 backlight = 14 lines); 4-bit is the KTU-preferred mode.
- **The DDRAM gap** between 0x10 and 0x3F is non-displayable — writing here is silently ignored.
- **CGROM vs CGRAM**: CGROM is read-only (factory-programmed ASCII), CGRAM is read-write (user-defined custom symbols).
- **Backlight is optional** — without it, the LCD is still readable in good lighting via ambient light reflecting off the polariser.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Always begin your C program answer with the four function prototypes: `delay_ms()`, `LCD_Enable()` (or `pulse()`), `LCD_Cmd()`, `LCD_Char()`. Examiners in KTU award 2 free marks just for a clean modular structure. Then attach the full `LCD_Init()` sequence *exactly* as shown — the three `0x30` sends followed by `0x20` is the **most commonly asked sub-question** in viva voce.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: C Program for ADC Interfacing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL508 - Microcontrollers and Embedded Systems Lab
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** C Program for ADC Interfacing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) Interfacing** is the process of establishing hardware and software communication between a microcontroller and an external ADC chip (e.g., **ADC0808/ADC0809** or on-chip ADC of ARM7/LPC2148) to sample continuous analog signals, quantize them into discrete digital codes, and process them using firmware written in Embedded C.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):** "Write an Embedded C program to interface a 8-bit ADC (ADC0808/0809) with 8051/ARM microcontroller, generate control signals, read converted digital data, and display the result on LCD/LED/serial terminal."

## 1.2 Key Terminology Decoded

| Term | Meaning | KTU Context |
|------|---------|-------------|
| **ADC** | Analog-to-Digital Converter | Converts real-world analog voltage → digital byte |
| **Resolution** | Smallest measurable voltage step | For 8-bit ADC with 5V reference → 19.53 mV |
| **Vref** | Reference Voltage | Sets the full-scale input range |
| **SOC (Start of Conversion)** | Control pulse to ADC | Triggers the sampling process |
| **EOC (End of Conversion)** | Status flag from ADC | Indicates conversion is complete |
| **ALE (Address Latch Enable)** | Latch channel address lines | For multiplexed analog inputs |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket Filling Station" 🚰

Imagine a **water tank (analog signal)** whose water level constantly changes. You want to tell a friend (the microcontroller) the exact water level every minute. Since the friend cannot see the tank, you install a **measuring station (ADC)** that:

1. Looks at the tank (samples the voltage).
2. Fills a fixed number of buckets (quantization into 256 levels for 8-bit).
3. Sends a text message "37 buckets full" (digital byte = `00100101`).

The **C program** is the instruction manual that tells the measuring station **when** to measure, **which tank** to read (channel selection), and **how to forward** the number to the display.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why ADC is essential in KTU labs:** Almost every real-world sensor (LM35 temperature, potentiometer, LDR) outputs an analog voltage. The microcontroller is purely digital — it cannot "read" voltage. Hence ADC is the **universal translator** between the physical world and the digital brain.

## 1.4 Hardware Block Context for KTU Lab

In PCEOL508 Experiment 2, students typically interface:
- **ADC0808 / ADC0809** (8-bit, 8-channel) with **AT89C51 (8051)**, OR
- **On-chip 10-bit ADC** of **LPC2148 (ARM7)** with reference voltage 3.3V.

The interfacing demands generation of:
- `ALE` (Address Latch Enable)
- `START` (Conversion trigger)
- `OE` (Output Enable) for reading data
- Monitoring `EOC` (End of Conversion)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How ADC Interfacing Works

The ADC interfacing workflow is a **strictly timed sequence** of control signals. Let us dissect the operation step-by-step:

### **Phase 1: Channel Selection**
- The 3 address lines (`ADD-A`, `ADD-B`, `ADD-C`) select one of 8 analog input channels (IN0 – IN7) of ADC0808.
- A high pulse on `ALE` latches this channel address internally.

### **Phase 2: Conversion Trigger**
- A high-to-low transition on the `START` pin initiates the **Successive Approximation Register (SAR)** conversion process.
- Internally, the SAR compares the input voltage against an internal DAC output using binary search.

### **Phase 3: Polling for EOC**
- The `EOC` pin goes `HIGH → LOW` when conversion starts, and returns `HIGH` when complete.
- The microcontroller polls this pin in a `while` loop: `while(ADC_EOC == 0);` (or 1, depending on polarity).

### **Phase 4: Data Readout**
- After EOC goes HIGH, the microcontroller asserts `OE = HIGH`.
- The 8-bit digital data appears on the data bus (`D0 – D7`).
- The C program reads this byte using `P1 = ADC_data;` (for 8051) or `ADC0DR` register (for LPC2148).

### **Phase 5: Display / Transmit**
- The digital value is displayed on **LCD**, **7-segment LEDs**, or sent via **UART** to a PC terminal.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|----------------------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | $V_{in} = \frac{D \times V_{ref}}{2^n}$ | Digital code to voltage conversion | $D$ = decimal value, $n$ = bits |
| 2 | $\text{Step Size} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ | Resolution (LSB weight) | Volts (e.g., $\frac{5V}{256} = 19.53\,mV$ for 8-bit) |
| 3 | $n_{bits} = \log_2(\text{No. of levels})$ | Bit depth formula | For 256 levels → 8 bits |
| 4 | $T_{conv} \geq \text{Max Conversion Time}$ | Wait time after START | ADC0808 = ~100 µs |
| 5 | $f_{CLK} \leq 1.1\,MHz$ | ADC0808 clock limit | Set via timer or external oscillator |
| 6 | $D_{out} = \lfloor V_{in} \cdot \frac{2^n}{V_{ref}} \rfloor$ | Voltage to digital code | Floor function for integer code |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Quick Check:** If $V_{ref} = 5V$ and ADC output = `0x7F` (127), then $V_{in} = \frac{127 \times 5}{256} = 2.48\,V$. This kind of numeric conversion is **frequently asked in KTU viva voce**.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | ADC Use Case |
|--------------------|--------------|
| **Industrial Automation** | Reading pressure/flow transducer outputs |
| **Biomedical** | ECG, EEG, pulse-oximeter signal acquisition |
| **Automotive** | Battery monitoring, throttle position sensing |
| **IoT / Smart Sensors** | LM35 (temperature), MQ-135 (gas), LDR (light) |
| **Embedded Data Loggers** | Multi-channel sensor recording to SD card |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 CO Mapping:** This experiment directly satisfies **CO2 (Design embedded C programs for peripheral interfacing)** and **CO3 (Analyze signal conditioning and data acquisition systems)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, C Code & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Generic Algorithm for ADC Interfacing (Pseudocode → Embedded C)

Below is the **exhaustive step-by-step mapping** from logic to Embedded C constructs, suitable for both 8051 and ARM targets.

### **Step 1: Initialize I/O Pins**
- Declare ADC data, control, and status pins as output/input using SFR/header definitions.
- 8051 Example: `sbit ALE = P3^3; sbit START = P3^4; sbit EOC = P3^5; sbit OE = P3^6;`

### **Step 2: Select Analog Channel**
- Write the 3-bit channel address (e.g., `000` for IN0) to the address lines.
- Apply an `ALE` HIGH pulse to latch the channel.

### **Step 3: Start Conversion**
- Generate a HIGH-to-LOW pulse on `START`. This initiates the SAR conversion.
- Provide adequate delay (~100 µs for ADC0808).

### **Step 4: Poll for EOC**
- Wait in a loop until `EOC` returns to HIGH state (conversion complete).

### **Step 5: Enable Output**
- Drive `OE` HIGH. The 8-bit data becomes available on the data port.

### **Step 6: Read Digital Data**
- Read the port: `adc_value = P1;` (8051) or read ADC data register (ARM).

### **Step 7: Process & Display**
- Convert to voltage using the formula in Section 2.2.
- Send to LCD via `LcdData(adc_value)` or transmit via UART.

## 3.2 Complete Embedded C Program — ADC0808 with 8051 (KEIL Syntax)

```c
#include <reg51.h>

/*---------------------- Pin Definitions ----------------------*/
sbit ALE    = P3^3;     // Address Latch Enable
sbit START  = P3^4;     // Start Conversion
sbit EOC    = P3^5;     // End of Conversion status
sbit OE     = P3^6;     // Output Enable
sbit ADD_A  = P3^0;     // Address line A (LSB)
sbit ADD_B  = P3^1;     // Address line B
sbit ADD_C  = P3^2;     // Address line C (MSB)

/*---------------------- Function Prototypes ----------------------*/
void delay_ms(unsigned int);
unsigned char adc_read(unsigned char);
void lcd_init(void);
void lcd_cmd(unsigned char);
void lcd_data(unsigned char);
void lcd_string(unsigned char *);

/*---------------------- Main Program ----------------------*/
void main(void)
{
    unsigned char digital_val;
    unsigned int  voltage_mV;

    lcd_init();
    lcd_string("ADC INTERFACING");
    lcd_cmd(0xC0);                 // Move to line 2

    while(1)
    {
        /*--- Step 1: Select Channel IN0 ---*/
        ADD_A = 0; ADD_B = 0; ADD_C = 0;
        digital_val = adc_read(0);

        /*--- Step 2: Convert ADC code to voltage (mV) ---*/
        voltage_mV = (unsigned int)(digital_val * 5000UL / 256);

        /*--- Step 3: Display digital value in hex ---*/
        lcd_cmd(0x80);
        lcd_string("HEX: ");
        lcd_data((digital_val >> 4) + '0');
        lcd_data((digital_val & 0x0F) + '0');
        lcd_string("  ");

        /*--- Step 4: Display voltage (illustrative) ---*/
        lcd_cmd(0xC0);
        lcd_string("Vin=");
        // Display thousands digit
        lcd_data((voltage_mV / 1000) + '0');
        lcd_data(((voltage_mV / 100) % 10) + '0');
        lcd_data('.');
        lcd_data(((voltage_mV / 10) % 10) + '0');
        lcd_data((voltage_mV % 10) + '0');
        lcd_string(" V");

        delay_ms(500);
    }
}

/*---------------------- ADC Read Function ----------------------*/
unsigned char adc_read(unsigned char channel)
{
    unsigned char value;

    /* Latch channel address */
    ALE   = 1;
    delay_ms(1);
    ALE   = 0;

    /* Start conversion */
    START = 1;
    delay_ms(1);
    START = 0;

    /* Wait for End of Conversion (EOC HIGH = complete) */
    while(EOC == 0);

    /* Enable output and read data */
    OE    = 1;
    delay_ms(1);
    value = P1;        // 8-bit data bus on Port 1
    OE    = 0;

    return value;
}

/*---------------------- Simple Software Delay ----------------------*/
void delay_ms(unsigned int ms)
{
    unsigned int i, j;
    for(i = 0; i < ms; i++)
        for(j = 0; j < 1275; j++);
}
```

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation — Digital Code to Voltage Conversion

Given an 8-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 5.0\,V$, derive $V_{in}$ for digital output $D = 0xA5$ (165 in decimal).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Total number of quantization levels} &= 2^n = 2^8 = 256 \\
\text{Step 2: Step size (LSB voltage)} &= \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{5.0}{256} \\
&= 0.01953\,V = 19.53\,mV \\
\text{Step 3: Convert hex 0xA5 to decimal} &= (10 \times 16^1) + (5 \times 16^0) \\
&= 160 + 5 = 165 \\
\text{Step 4: Compute input voltage} &= D \times \text{Step Size} \\
&= 165 \times 0.01953\,V \\
&= 3.222\,V
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification using direct formula:**
$$
V_{in} = \frac{D \times V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{165 \times 5.0}{256} = \frac{825}{256} = 3.222\,V
$$

> Both methods yield identical results, confirming the **bidirectional nature** of the quantization equation.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Signal, Code, Voltage Cross-Table

| Analog Input $V_{in}$ (V) | Decimal Code $D$ | Hex Code | Binary Code | LED / LCD Display |
|----------------------------|------------------|----------|-------------|---------------------|
| 0.000 | 0 | 0x00 | 00000000 | "0.00 V" |
| 0.625 | 32 | 0x20 | 00100000 | "0.62 V" |
| 1.250 | 64 | 0x40 | 01000000 | "1.25 V" |
| 2.500 | 128 | 0x80 | 10000000 | "2.50 V" |
| 3.750 | 192 | 0xC0 | 11000000 | "3.75 V" |
| 4.980 | 255 | 0xFF | 11111111 | "4.98 V" |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mid-scale test point:** When $D = 128$ exactly, $V_{in} = 2.5\,V$ for $V_{ref} = 5\,V$. KTU lab examiners commonly verify hardware using this single test value.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Analog Sensor e.g. LM35 / Potentiometer] --> B[ADC0808 / ADC0809 Analog Input IN0 to IN7]
    B --> C{Address Selection ADD-A, ADD-B, ADD-C}
    C --> D[ALE Pulse from Microcontroller]
    D --> E[START Pulse to ADC SAR Core]
    E --> F[Successive Approximation Register Conversion ~100 us]
    F --> G{EOC Pin Status Polling}
    G --> H[Microcontroller asserts OE HIGH]
    H --> I[8-bit Digital Data on D0 to D7]
    I --> J[Microcontroller Reads Data P1 or ADC0DR]
    J --> K[Digital Code to Voltage Conversion]
    K --> L[LCD Display / UART TX / LED Array]
    L --> M[Next Sample Loop Iteration]

    style A fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,color:#000
    style B fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    style F fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
    style J fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    style L fill:#FFFDE7,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
```

## 4.2 ADC0808 ↔ 8051 Interfacing Pin Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ADC0808[ADC0808 Chip]
        ADC_VCC[VCC Pin 11]
        ADC_GND[GND Pin 13]
        ADC_VREF[Vref+ Pin 12 5V]
        ADC_IN[IN0 to IN7 Pins 1 to 5, 26 to 28]
        ADC_ADD[ADD-A B C Pins 25 24 23]
        ADC_ALE[ALE Pin 22]
        ADC_ST[START Pin 6]
        ADC_EOC[EOC Pin 7]
        ADC_OE[OE Pin 9]
        ADC_DOUT[D0 to D7 Pins 17 to 10]
        ADC_CLK[CLK Pin 10]
    end

    subgraph MC[AT89C51 Microcontroller]
        MC_P1[Port P1 Data Input]
        MC_P3[Port P3 Control Lines]
        MC_XTAL[XTAL2 Clock Source]
    end

    subgraph LCD[16x2 LCD Display]
        LCD_DATA[Data Pins D0 to D7]
        LCD_RS[RS Pin]
        LCD_EN[Enable Pin]
    end

    ADC_DOUT --> MC_P1
    MC_P3 --> ADC_ALE
    MC_P3 --> ADC_ST
    MC_P3 --> ADC_OE
    ADC_EOC --> MC_P3
    ADC_ADD --> MC_P3
    MC_XTAL --> ADC_CLK
    MC_P1 --> LCD_DATA
    MC_P3 --> LCD_RS
    MC_P3 --> LCD_EN

    style ADC0808 fill:#FFECB3,stroke:#FF6F00,color:#000
    style MC fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000
    style LCD fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#1565C0,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Conversion Timing Diagram (Sequence of Control Signals)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant uC as Microcontroller
    participant ADC as ADC0808
    participant DISP as Display LCD

    uC->>ADC: Set ADD-A, ADD-B, ADD-C (Channel Select)
    uC->>ADC: ALE = HIGH (Latch Address)
    uC->>ADC: ALE = LOW
    uC->>ADC: START = HIGH
    uC->>ADC: START = LOW (Initiate SAR)
    Note over ADC: Internal Conversion in Progress ~100 us
    ADC-->>uC: EOC = HIGH (Conversion Complete)
    uC->>ADC: OE = HIGH (Enable Output)
    ADC-->>uC: 8-bit Data on D0 to D7
    uC->>uC: Read P1 = digital_value
    uC->>ADC: OE = LOW
    uC->>uC: Calculate Vin = D x Vref / 256
    uC->>DISP: lcd_string Display Hex and Voltage
    uC->>uC: delay_ms 500 ms
    Note over uC,DISP: Loop repeats continuously
```

## 4.4 Data Flow Summary Table

| Direction | Signal Name | Source | Destination | Purpose |
|-----------|-------------|--------|-------------|---------|
| OUT | ADD-A/B/C | 8051 P3.0–P3.2 | ADC0808 Pins 23–25 | Channel selection |
| OUT | ALE | 8051 P3.3 | ADC0808 Pin 22 | Latch channel address |
| OUT | START | 8051 P3.4 | ADC0808 Pin 6 | Trigger conversion |
| IN | EOC | ADC0808 Pin 7 | 8051 P3.5 | Status: conversion done |
| OUT | OE | 8051 P3.6 | ADC0808 Pin 9 | Enable data bus output |
| IN | D0–D7 | ADC0808 Pins 17–10 | 8051 Port P1 | 8-bit digital result |
| IN | CLK | 8051 XTAL/divider | ADC0808 Pin 10 | Conversion clock |
| OUT | LCD Data | 8051 P0/P2 | LCD DB4–DB7 | Display formatted value |

---
