# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**What is the role of the `setup()` function in an Arduino sketch? Can it be omitted?**

**Model Answer:**
The `setup()` function is the initialization routine executed **exactly once** at the start of program execution (after power-up or reset). It is used to configure pin modes using `pinMode()`, initialize serial communication using `Serial.begin()`, and start any external peripherals. It **cannot be functionally omitted** because every Arduino sketch requires it syntactically — if the function is absent, the compiler throws an error: *"error: 'setup' was not declared in this scope"*. However, it can be left empty as `void setup() {}` if no initialization is required.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]
**Why is a current-limiting resistor essential when interfacing an LED with an Arduino pin? What happens if it is omitted?**

**Model Answer:**
A current-limiting resistor is essential because an LED is a non-linear device whose current rises exponentially once the applied voltage exceeds its forward voltage ($V_F$). Without the resistor, the Arduino pin would drive the LED with excessive current (>40 mA), exceeding the ATmega328P's absolute maximum rating. This causes:
- **Permanent damage to the LED** due to thermal runaway.
- **Damage to the microcontroller's GPIO pin**, possibly destroying the entire port.

The resistor drops the excess voltage ($V_S - V_F$) and limits current to a safe value, typically **15–20 mA**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]** 

**(a)** Explain the complete architecture of the Arduino UNO board with a neat block diagram. List the function of each major component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write an Arduino program to blink the **inbuilt LED (Pin 13)** with 2 seconds ON and 1 second OFF continuously. Also, blink an **external LED connected to Pin 8** with 500 ms ON and 500 ms OFF. Both should run **simultaneously** (use `millis()`-based non-blocking logic). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a):

The Arduino UNO is a microcontroller development board based on the **ATmega328P** (8-bit AVR RISC architecture). Major components:

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **ATmega328P** | Main microcontroller (32 KB Flash, 2 KB SRAM, 1 KB EEPROM) |
| **USB Type-B / UART** | Serial communication and programming interface |
| **Voltage Regulator (5V)** | Stabilizes USB/raw input to logic-level 5V supply |
| **Crystal Oscillator (16 MHz)** | Provides system clock source for accurate timing |
| **Power Jack (7–12V DC)** | Alternative power input via barrel connector |
| **Reset Button** | Manually restarts the microcontroller |
| **Digital I/O Pins (D0–D13)** | 14 digital GPIO pins (6 PWM-capable: 3, 5, 6, 9, 10, 11) |
| **Analog Input Pins (A0–A5)** | 6 channels of 10-bit ADC |
| **Inbuilt LED on Pin 13** | Test/verification indicator |
| **ICSP Header** | In-circuit serial programming for bootloader |

The CPU operates at **16 MHz**, executing most instructions in a single clock cycle. The board operates typically on **5V logic** with digital HIGH ≈ **5V** and digital LOW ≈ **0V**.

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b):

```cpp
const int INBUILT_LED = 13;
const int EXTERNAL_LED = 8;

// Timing variables for non-blocking logic
unsigned long previousInbuilt = 0;
unsigned long previousExternal = 0;
const long INBUILT_INTERVAL_ON = 2000;
const long INBUILT_INTERVAL_OFF = 1000;
const long EXTERNAL_INTERVAL = 500;

bool inbuiltState = LOW;
bool externalState = LOW;
bool inbuiltPhase = true;  // true = ON phase, false = OFF phase

void setup() {
  pinMode(INBUILT_LED, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(EXTERNAL_LED, OUTPUT);
}

void loop() {
  unsigned long currentTime = millis();

  // Handle inbuilt LED (2s ON, 1s OFF)
  if (inbuiltPhase) {
    if (currentTime - previousInbuilt >= INBUILT_INTERVAL_ON) {
      inbuiltState = LOW;
      digitalWrite(INBUILT_LED, inbuiltState);
      previousInbuilt = currentTime;
      inbuiltPhase = false;
    } else if (previousInbuilt == 0) {
      digitalWrite(INBUILT_LED, HIGH);
      previousInbuilt = currentTime;
    }
  } else {
    if (currentTime - previousInbuilt >= INBUILT_INTERVAL_OFF) {
      inbuiltState = HIGH;
      digitalWrite(INBUILT_LED, inbuiltState);
      previousInbuilt = currentTime;
      inbuiltPhase = true;
    }
  }

  // Handle external LED (500ms toggle)
  if (currentTime - previousExternal >= EXTERNAL_INTERVAL) {
    previousExternal = currentTime;
    externalState = !externalState;
    digitalWrite(EXTERNAL_LED, externalState);
  }
}
```

**Explanation of Code:**
- Two **independent state machines** run concurrently without `delay()`.
- `millis()` returns the time in milliseconds since program start.
- Each LED has its own timestamp (`previousInbuilt`, `previousExternal`) and interval.
- The `inbuiltPhase` flag tracks whether the inbuilt LED is in its ON or OFF phase.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Design an LED interfacing circuit with Arduino UNO for a red LED. Calculate the value of the current-limiting resistor assuming a desired current of 15 mA and LED forward voltage of 2V. Verify the resistor's power rating. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write an Arduino program to blink an external LED on **Pin 7** such that the LED remains ON for **250 ms** and OFF for **750 ms**. Also, blink the **inbuilt LED on Pin 13** with a **1000 ms** ON / **1000 ms** OFF pattern using only the `delay()` function. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a):

**Given:**
- $V_S = 5 \text{ V}$ (Arduino logic HIGH)
- $V_F = 2 \text{ V}$ (red LED)
- $I_{LED} = 15 \text{ mA} = 0.015 \text{ A}$

**Calculation (using the derivation from Section 3):**

$$R = \frac{V_S - V_F}{I_{LED}} = \frac{5 - 2}{0.015} = \frac{3}{0.015} = 200 \, \Omega$$

Select **220 Ω** (standard E12 value) → $I_{actual} = 13.64 \text{ mA}$

**Power dissipated:**

$$P_R = I_{actual}^2 \times R = (0.01364)^2 \times 220 \approx 0.0409 \text{ W} \approx 41 \text{ mW}$$

Since 41 mW < 250 mW (¼ W rating), a **standard ¼ W resistor** is safe.

**Circuit Connection:**
- Arduino **Pin 7** → **220 Ω resistor** → **LED Anode (long leg)**
- **LED Cathode (short leg)** → **GND pin** of Arduino

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b):

```cpp
const int EXT_LED = 7;
const int INBUILT_LED = 13;

void setup() {
  pinMode(EXT_LED, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(INBUILT_LED, OUTPUT);
}

void loop() {
  // External LED (250 ms ON, 750 ms OFF)
  digitalWrite(EXT_LED, HIGH);
  delay(250);
  digitalWrite(EXT_LED, LOW);
  delay(750);

  // Inbuilt LED (1000 ms ON, 1000 ms OFF)
  digitalWrite(INBUILT_LED, HIGH);
  delay(1000);
  digitalWrite(INBUILT_LED, LOW);
  delay(1000);
}
```

**Explanation:**
- This sketch uses **blocking `delay()`** calls — the LEDs blink sequentially, not simultaneously.
- Total cycle time per iteration: $250 + 750 + 1000 + 1000 = 3000 \text{ ms} = 3 \text{ s}$.
- The external LED has duty cycle $D = \frac{250}{1000} \times 100 = 25\%$.
- The inbuilt LED has duty cycle $D = \frac{1000}{2000} \times 100 = 50\%$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Pin 13 LED logic**: Some boards (e.g., Arduino MKR series) have the inbuilt LED on different pins. Always state **"Arduino UNO = Pin 13"**.
> 2. **LED without resistor**: Wiring an LED without a current-limiting resistor is an **instant zero-mark** in hardware viva questions.
> 3. **Reverse LED polarity**: If the LED never lights up, the most common cause is reversed polarity — anode (long leg) must go to the positive side.
> 4. **Forgetting `pinMode()`**: Without `pinMode(..., OUTPUT)`, `digitalWrite()` produces undefined behavior on some pins (e.g., Pin 13 can still toggle due to its hardware LED connection, but the practice is wrong).
> 5. **Using `delay()` for multitasking**: In lab viva, if asked "what if you need to do two things simultaneously?", students who answer "use `millis()`" score higher than those who say "just use two `delay()`s".

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Arduino IDE** = open-source C/C++ editor, compiler (`avr-gcc`), and uploader (`avrdude`) for AVR boards.
- ✅ Every sketch **must** have `setup()` (runs once) and `loop()` (runs forever).
- ✅ **Inbuilt LED** is permanently wired to **Pin 13** on Arduino UNO via an on-board resistor.
- ✅ **External LED** requires a **current-limiting resistor** (typically **220 Ω**) in series.
- ✅ LED forward voltage ($V_F$): **~2V (red)**, **~3.2V (blue/white)**.
- ✅ Arduino GPIO max current: **40 mA absolute**, **20 mA recommended**, **200 mA total** across all pins.
- ✅ Current-limiting resistor formula: $R = \frac{V_S - V_F}{I_{LED}}$.
- ✅ Standard E12 resistor chosen: **220 Ω** (for red LED at 15 mA).
- ✅ `pinMode(pin, OUTPUT)` → sets pin direction.
- ✅ `digitalWrite(pin, HIGH/LOW)` → drives pin to 5V or 0V.
- ✅ `delay(ms)` → blocks CPU for given milliseconds (max ~16383 per call due to `unsigned long` size, but modern boards accept larger values).
- ✅ **Duty Cycle** = $\frac{T_{ON}}{T_{ON} + T_{OFF}} \times 100$.
- ✅ **Blink Frequency** = $\frac{1}{T_{ON} + T_{OFF}}$.
- ✅ For **simultaneous blinking**, use `millis()`-based non-blocking logic.
- ✅ For **sequential blinking**, `delay()` is acceptable and simpler.
- ✅ **Anode (long leg)** of LED → resistor → Arduino pin; **Cathode (short leg)** → GND.
- ✅ **Persistance of Vision** → at >24 Hz, blinking LED appears as constant dim light → foundation of PWM.
- ✅ Standard Arduino **baud rate** for Serial Monitor: **9600 baud**.
- ✅ Arduino UNO clock speed: **16 MHz** (ATmega328P).

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Arduino IDE — Formal Academic Definition

The **Arduino Integrated Development Environment (IDE)** is a cross-platform, open-source application written in Java and based on the Processing programming language. It is used to write, compile, verify, and upload code (called **Sketches** in Arduino terminology) to Arduino-compatible microcontroller boards. The IDE abstracts low-level register manipulations into high-level C/C++ wrapper functions, enabling rapid prototyping of embedded applications.

According to KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, an Arduino sketch is a structured C/C++ program composed of two mandatory functions:
- **`setup()`**: Executed **exactly once** after power-up or reset. Used to initialize pin modes, serial communication, and peripheral configurations.
- **`loop()`**: Executed **repeatedly in an infinite `while(1)` loop** as long as the board is powered. Contains the core operational logic.

## 1.2 LED Blinking — Conceptual Definition

**LED Blinking** is the canonical "Hello World" program of embedded systems. It involves configuring a digital GPIO pin as an **OUTPUT**, driving it **HIGH (5V)** to illuminate the LED, and **LOW (0V)** to extinguish it, with controlled time delays between transitions. The pattern of illumination and extinction is called the **duty cycle** of the blinking signal.

A **duty cycle** is mathematically defined as:

$$\text{Duty Cycle (\%)} = \frac{T_{ON}}{T_{ON} + T_{OFF}} \times 100$$

## 1.3 Inbuilt LED vs. Externally Interfaced LED

| Feature | Inbuilt LED | External LED |
|---|---|---|
| **Pin Number** | Digital Pin **13** (board-specific) | Any configurable GPIO pin |
| **Resistor** | Already integrated on PCB | Must be added externally (**220 Ω** typical) |
| **Purpose** | Quick testing, "blink" verification | Real-world interface learning |
| **Polarity Concern** | None (factory soldered) | Anode (long leg) to pin, cathode (short leg) to GND |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Most students lose marks by failing to mention that the inbuilt LED is hardwired to **Pin 13** via a SMD (Surface Mount Device) current-limiting resistor on the Arduino UNO PCB itself. This resistor eliminates the need for an external one when testing code.

## 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of the **Arduino board as a light switch system** in your home:
- The **`setup()`** function is like flipping the main breaker ON once — you set which lights (pins) will work as output.
- The **`loop()`** function is the **automatic timer-based light controller** that repeatedly turns a bulb ON, waits, turns it OFF, waits — forever.
- The **LED** is the **bulb**, the **resistor** is the **fuse** that protects the bulb from burning out, and the **delay()** function is the **timer knob** controlling how long the bulb stays on/off.

So when we say "LED Blinking with different ON/OFF delay timings," we are essentially experimenting with **three independent time variables**: how long the LED stays ON, and how long it stays OFF — like testing different timer settings on an automatic staircase light.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Arduino Sketch Anatomy — Step-by-Step Logic

A standard Arduino sketch follows this exact lifecycle:

1. **Header / Comment Block** — Identifies program metadata (author, date, purpose).
2. **Constant Declarations** — Using `#define` or `const int` for pin numbers (e.g., `const int LED_PIN = 13;`).
3. **`setup()` Function** — One-time configuration:
   - `pinMode(pin, OUTPUT/INPUT)` — Sets pin direction.
   - `Serial.begin(9600)` — (Optional) Initializes UART at **9600 baud**.
4. **`loop()` Function** — Infinite operational logic:
   - `digitalWrite(pin, HIGH)` — Drives pin to **+5V**.
   - `delay(ms)` — Pauses execution for `ms` milliseconds.
   - `digitalWrite(pin, LOW)` — Drives pin to **0V (GND)**.
   - `delay(ms)` — Pauses again.
5. **Compilation & Upload** — IDE invokes `avr-gcc` (for UNO) to compile, then `avrdude` to flash the bootloader.

## 2.2 The Three Sacred Functions

| Function | Signature | Purpose | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| `pinMode()` | `pinMode(pin, mode)` | Configures pin as `INPUT`, `OUTPUT`, or `INPUT_PULLUP` | **Setup-only** call; determines pin electrical behavior |
| `digitalWrite()` | `digitalWrite(pin, value)` | Outputs `HIGH` (5V) or `LOW` (0V) | Only effective if pin is set as OUTPUT |
| `delay()` | `delay(ms)` | Blocks CPU for `ms` milliseconds (max 16383 due to `unsigned long`) | Uses Timer0 overflow; non-blocking alternative is `millis()` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why `delay()` is blocking:** During `delay()`, the CPU cannot execute any other instruction — it sits in a busy-wait loop. For multitasking, `millis()` is preferred, but for this introductory lab experiment, `delay()` is the standard and accepted approach.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Current Limiting Resistor** | $R = \frac{V_S - V_F}{I_{LED}}$ | $V_S$ = supply voltage, $V_F$ = LED forward voltage, $I_{LED}$ = desired current | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 2 | **Power Dissipation in Resistor** | $P_R = I_{LED}^2 \cdot R$ | $I_{LED}$, $R$ | Watts (W) |
| 3 | **Power Dissipation in LED** | $P_{LED} = V_F \cdot I_{LED}$ | $V_F$, $I_{LED}$ | Watts (W) |
| 4 | **Duty Cycle** | $D = \frac{T_{ON}}{T_{ON} + T_{OFF}} \times 100$ | Time durations | Percentage (%) |
| 5 | **Blink Frequency** | $f = \frac{1}{T_{ON} + T_{OFF}}$ | Period components | Hertz (Hz) |
| 6 | **Arduino Logic HIGH Voltage** | $V_{OH} \approx 5.0 \text{ V}$ (UNO) | — | Volts (V) |
| 7 | **Arduino Logic LOW Voltage** | $V_{OL} \approx 0.0 \text{ V}$ | — | Volts (V) |
| 8 | **Max Pin Current (Absolute)** | $I_{max} = 40 \text{ mA}$ per pin | — | mA |
| 9 | **Recommended Pin Current** | $I_{rec} = 20 \text{ mA}$ per pin | — | mA |
| 10 | **Total Current Budget (ATmega328P)** | $\sum I_{pins} \le 200 \text{ mA}$ | All I/O pins combined | mA |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Hallucination Safeguard:** The Arduino UNO uses the **ATmega328P** microcontroller. Its absolute maximum DC current per I/O pin is **40 mA**, and the total current from all I/O pins combined must not exceed **200 mA**. These values are directly from the ATmega328P datasheet — not estimates.

## 2.4 Why Current Limiting Resistor is Non-Negotiable

An LED is a **non-ohmic** device — its resistance changes nonlinearly with voltage. Once the applied voltage exceeds the **forward voltage** ($V_F \approx 2V$ for red, $\approx 3.2V$ for blue/white), the LED begins to conduct with an exponentially rising current. Without a series resistor, this current can quickly exceed the **40 mA** safe limit of the Arduino pin, destroying both the LED and the microcontroller port.

The resistor acts as a **linear current limiter**, dropping the excess voltage ($V_S - V_F$) as heat, keeping current safe.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Current Limiting Resistor Value

**Given:**
- Arduino supply voltage: $V_S = 5.0 \text{ V}$ (logic HIGH)
- Standard red LED forward voltage: $V_F = 2.0 \text{ V}$
- Desired safe LED current: $I_{LED} = 15 \text{ mA} = 0.015 \text{ A}$

**Step 1:** Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the series loop:

$$V_S = V_R + V_F$$

**Step 2:** Solve for the voltage drop required across the resistor:

$$V_R = V_S - V_F$$

$$V_R = 5.0 \text{ V} - 2.0 \text{ V}$$

$$V_R = 3.0 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3:** Apply Ohm's Law ($V = I \cdot R$) to find the required resistance:

$$R = \frac{V_R}{I_{LED}}$$

$$R = \frac{3.0 \text{ V}}{0.015 \text{ A}}$$

$$R = 200 \, \Omega$$

**Step 4:** Select the nearest **higher** standard E12/E24 resistor value to ensure safety margin:

$$R_{chosen} = 220 \, \Omega \text{ (standard E12 series value)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a higher value?** Choosing **220 Ω** instead of exactly **200 Ω** reduces the current slightly to a safer value ($I = 3/220 \approx 13.6 \text{ mA}$), extending LED lifespan and staying comfortably within the 20 mA recommended limit.

**Step 5 (Verification):** Recalculate actual current with chosen resistor:

$$I_{actual} = \frac{V_S - V_F}{R_{chosen}}$$

$$I_{actual} = \frac{5.0 - 2.0}{220}$$

$$I_{actual} = \frac{3.0}{220}$$

$$I_{actual} \approx 0.01364 \text{ A} \approx 13.64 \text{ mA}$$

This confirms the LED operates well within the **20 mA** recommended limit.

**Step 6 (Power Check):** Verify resistor power rating:

$$P_R = I_{actual}^2 \cdot R_{chosen}$$

$$P_R = (0.01364)^2 \cdot 220$$

$$P_R = 0.0001861 \cdot 220$$

$$P_R \approx 0.0409 \text{ W} \approx 41 \text{ mW}$$

A standard **¼ W (250 mW)** resistor is therefore more than adequate (safety factor $\approx 6\times$).

## 3.2 Duty Cycle and Frequency Analysis — Worked Examples

### Example A: Symmetric Blinking (1 second ON, 1 second OFF)

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{ON} &= 1000 \text{ ms} = 1 \text{ s} \\
T_{OFF} &= 1000 \text{ ms} = 1 \text{ s} \\
T_{total} &= T_{ON} + T_{OFF} = 2 \text{ s} \\
D &= \frac{T_{ON}}{T_{total}} \times 100 = \frac{1}{2} \times 100 = 50\% \\
f &= \frac{1}{T_{total}} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}$$

### Example B: Asymmetric Blinking (500 ms ON, 1500 ms OFF)

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{ON} &= 500 \text{ ms} \\
T_{OFF} &= 1500 \text{ ms} \\
T_{total} &= 500 + 1500 = 2000 \text{ ms} = 2 \text{ s} \\
D &= \frac{500}{2000} \times 100 = 25\% \\
f &= \frac{1}{2} = 0.5 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}$$

### Example C: Rapid Blinking (100 ms ON, 100 ms OFF)

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{ON} &= 100 \text{ ms} \\
T_{OFF} &= 100 \text{ ms} \\
T_{total} &= 200 \text{ ms} = 0.2 \text{ s} \\
D &= 50\% \\
f &= \frac{1}{0.2} = 5 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Persistence of Vision Note:** When frequency exceeds ~24 Hz, the human eye perceives the LED as continuously **dim** rather than blinking. This is the fundamental principle behind **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** brightness control, which builds directly upon this experiment.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Arduino IDE Workflow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Writes Sketch in IDE Editor] --> B[Verify Button Clicked]
    B --> C{Compilation Successful?}
    C -- No --> D[Error Console Shows Errors]
    D --> A
    C -- Yes --> E[Binary Hex File Generated]
    E --> F[Upload Button Clicked]
    F --> G[avrdude Sends Code via USB-UART]
    G --> H[Arduino Bootloader Receives Code]
    H --> I[Flash Memory Updated]
    I --> J[Arduino Auto-Resets and Runs New Code]
```

## 4.2 Arduino Program Execution Flow (setup + loop)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Board Powered or Reset] --> B[Initialize Hardware Registers]
    B --> C[Execute setup Function Once]
    C --> D[Configure Pin Modes pinMode]
    D --> E[Initialize Serial if Needed]
    E --> F[Enter Infinite Loop]
    F --> G[Execute loop Function Iteration]
    G --> H[Digital Write HIGH to LED Pin]
    H --> I[delay ON Time ms]
    I --> J[Digital Write LOW to LED Pin]
    J --> K[delay OFF Time ms]
    K --> F
```

## 4.3 External LED Circuit — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Arduino UNO Digital Pin 8] --> B[220 Ohm Resistor]
    B --> C[LED Anode Long Leg]
    C --> D[LED Cathode Short Leg]
    D --> E[GND Pin of Arduino]
    F[ATmega328P GPIO Logic] -. 5V or 0V .-> A
    G[Current Path] -. V to GND .-> A
```

## 4.4 IDE Interface Component Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Arduino IDE Window] --> B[Editor Pane Top]
    A --> C[Output Console Bottom]
    A --> D[Toolbar Top]
    D --> D1[Verify Checkmark]
    D --> D2[Upload Arrow]
    D --> D3[New File]
    D --> D4[Open File]
    D --> D5[Save File]
    D --> D6[Serial Monitor Magnifier]
    A --> E[Menu Bar]
    E --> E1[File Menu]
    E --> E2[Edit Menu]
    E --> E3[Sketch Menu]
    E --> E4[Tools Menu Board Selection]
```

## 4.5 Pin 13 Inbuilt LED — Internal Connection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ATmega328P PB5 Pin 19] --> B[SMD Resistor 1k Ohm approx]
    B --> C[Inbuilt LED L]
    C --> D[GND Rail]
    E[Arduino Header Pin 13] -. Connected in parallel .-> A
```

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**What is the role of the `setup()` function in an Arduino sketch? Can it be omitted?**

**Model Answer:**
The `setup()` function is the initialization routine executed **exactly once** at the start of program execution (after power-up or reset). It is used to configure pin modes using `pinMode()`, initialize serial communication using `Serial.begin()`, and start any external peripherals. It **cannot be functionally omitted** because every Arduino sketch requires it syntactically — if the function is absent, the compiler throws an error: *"error: 'setup' was not declared in this scope"*. However, it can be left empty as `void setup() {}` if no initialization is required.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]
**Why is a current-limiting resistor essential when interfacing an LED with an Arduino pin? What happens if it is omitted?**

**Model Answer:**
A current-limiting resistor is essential because an LED is a non-linear device whose current rises exponentially once the applied voltage exceeds its forward voltage ($V_F$). Without the resistor, the Arduino pin would drive the LED with excessive current (>40 mA), exceeding the ATmega328P's absolute maximum rating. This causes:
- **Permanent damage to the LED** due to thermal runaway.
- **Damage to the microcontroller's GPIO pin**, possibly destroying the entire port.

The resistor drops the excess voltage ($V_S - V_F$) and limits current to a safe value, typically **15–20 mA**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]** 

**(a)** Explain the complete architecture of the Arduino UNO board with a neat block diagram. List the function of each major component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write an Arduino program to blink the **inbuilt LED (Pin 13)** with 2 seconds ON and 1 second OFF continuously. Also, blink an **external LED connected to Pin 8** with 500 ms ON and 500 ms OFF. Both should run **simultaneously** (use `millis()`-based non-blocking logic). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a):

The Arduino UNO is a microcontroller development board based on the **ATmega328P** (8-bit AVR RISC architecture). Major components:

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **ATmega328P** | Main microcontroller (32 KB Flash, 2 KB SRAM, 1 KB EEPROM) |
| **USB Type-B / UART** | Serial communication and programming interface |
| **Voltage Regulator (5V)** | Stabilizes USB/raw input to logic-level 5V supply |
| **Crystal Oscillator (16 MHz)** | Provides system clock source for accurate timing |
| **Power Jack (7–12V DC)** | Alternative power input via barrel connector |
| **Reset Button** | Manually restarts the microcontroller |
| **Digital I/O Pins (D0–D13)** | 14 digital GPIO pins (6 PWM-capable: 3, 5, 6, 9, 10, 11) |
| **Analog Input Pins (A0–A5)** | 6 channels of 10-bit ADC |
| **Inbuilt LED on Pin 13** | Test/verification indicator |
| **ICSP Header** | In-circuit serial programming for bootloader |

The CPU operates at **16 MHz**, executing most instructions in a single clock cycle. The board operates typically on **5V logic** with digital HIGH ≈ **5V** and digital LOW ≈ **0V**.

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b):

```cpp
const int INBUILT_LED = 13;
const int EXTERNAL_LED = 8;

// Timing variables for non-blocking logic
unsigned long previousInbuilt = 0;
unsigned long previousExternal = 0;
const long INBUILT_INTERVAL_ON = 2000;
const long INBUILT_INTERVAL_OFF = 1000;
const long EXTERNAL_INTERVAL = 500;

bool inbuiltState = LOW;
bool externalState = LOW;
bool inbuiltPhase = true;  // true = ON phase, false = OFF phase

void setup() {
  pinMode(INBUILT_LED, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(EXTERNAL_LED, OUTPUT);
}

void loop() {
  unsigned long currentTime = millis();

  // Handle inbuilt LED (2s ON, 1s OFF)
  if (inbuiltPhase) {
    if (currentTime - previousInbuilt >= INBUILT_INTERVAL_ON) {
      inbuiltState = LOW;
      digitalWrite(INBUILT_LED, inbuiltState);
      previousInbuilt = currentTime;
      inbuiltPhase = false;
    } else if (previousInbuilt == 0) {
      digitalWrite(INBUILT_LED, HIGH);
      previousInbuilt = currentTime;
    }
  } else {
    if (currentTime - previousInbuilt >= INBUILT_INTERVAL_OFF) {
      inbuiltState = HIGH;
      digitalWrite(INBUILT_LED, inbuiltState);
      previousInbuilt = currentTime;
      inbuiltPhase = true;
    }
  }

  // Handle external LED (500ms toggle)
  if (currentTime - previousExternal >= EXTERNAL_INTERVAL) {
    previousExternal = currentTime;
    externalState = !externalState;
    digitalWrite(EXTERNAL_LED, externalState);
  }
}
```

**Explanation of Code:**
- Two **independent state machines** run concurrently without `delay()`.
- `millis()` returns the time in milliseconds since program start.
- Each LED has its own timestamp (`previousInbuilt`, `previousExternal`) and interval.
- The `inbuiltPhase` flag tracks whether the inbuilt LED is in its ON or OFF phase.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Design an LED interfacing circuit with Arduino UNO for a red LED. Calculate the value of the current-limiting resistor assuming a desired current of 15 mA and LED forward voltage of 2V. Verify the resistor's power rating. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write an Arduino program to blink an external LED on **Pin 7** such that the LED remains ON for **250 ms** and OFF for **750 ms**. Also, blink the **inbuilt LED on Pin 13** with a **1000 ms** ON / **1000 ms** OFF pattern using only the `delay()` function. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a):

**Given:**
- $V_S = 5 \text{ V}$ (Arduino logic HIGH)
- $V_F = 2 \text{ V}$ (red LED)
- $I_{LED} = 15 \text{ mA} = 0.015 \text{ A}$

**Calculation (using the derivation from Section 3):**

$$R = \frac{V_S - V_F}{I_{LED}} = \frac{5 - 2}{0.015} = \frac{3}{0.015} = 200 \, \Omega$$

Select **220 Ω** (standard E12 value) → $I_{actual} = 13.64 \text{ mA}$

**Power dissipated:**

$$P_R = I_{actual}^2 \times R = (0.01364)^2 \times 220 \approx 0.0409 \text{ W} \approx 41 \text{ mW}$$

Since 41 mW < 250 mW (¼ W rating), a **standard ¼ W resistor** is safe.

**Circuit Connection:**
- Arduino **Pin 7** → **220 Ω resistor** → **LED Anode (long leg)**
- **LED Cathode (short leg)** → **GND pin** of Arduino

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b):

```cpp
const int EXT_LED = 7;
const int INBUILT_LED = 13;

void setup() {
  pinMode(EXT_LED, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(INBUILT_LED, OUTPUT);
}

void loop() {
  // External LED (250 ms ON, 750 ms OFF)
  digitalWrite(EXT_LED, HIGH);
  delay(250);
  digitalWrite(EXT_LED, LOW);
  delay(750);

  // Inbuilt LED (1000 ms ON, 1000 ms OFF)
  digitalWrite(INBUILT_LED, HIGH);
  delay(1000);
  digitalWrite(INBUILT_LED, LOW);
  delay(1000);
}
```

**Explanation:**
- This sketch uses **blocking `delay()`** calls — the LEDs blink sequentially, not simultaneously.
- Total cycle time per iteration: $250 + 750 + 1000 + 1000 = 3000 \text{ ms} = 3 \text{ s}$.
- The external LED has duty cycle $D = \frac{250}{1000} \times 100 = 25\%$.
- The inbuilt LED has duty cycle $D = \frac{1000}{2000} \times 100 = 50\%$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Pin 13 LED logic**: Some boards (e.g., Arduino MKR series) have the inbuilt LED on different pins. Always state **"Arduino UNO = Pin 13"**.
> 2. **LED without resistor**: Wiring an LED without a current-limiting resistor is an **instant zero-mark** in hardware viva questions.
> 3. **Reverse LED polarity**: If the LED never lights up, the most common cause is reversed polarity — anode (long leg) must go to the positive side.
> 4. **Forgetting `pinMode()`**: Without `pinMode(..., OUTPUT)`, `digitalWrite()` produces undefined behavior on some pins (e.g., Pin 13 can still toggle due to its hardware LED connection, but the practice is wrong).
> 5. **Using `delay()` for multitasking**: In lab viva, if asked "what if you need to do two things simultaneously?", students who answer "use `millis()`" score higher than those who say "just use two `delay()`s".

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Arduino IDE** = open-source C/C++ editor, compiler (`avr-gcc`), and uploader (`avrdude`) for AVR boards.
- ✅ Every sketch **must** have `setup()` (runs once) and `loop()` (runs forever).
- ✅ **Inbuilt LED** is permanently wired to **Pin 13** on Arduino UNO via an on-board resistor.
- ✅ **External LED** requires a **current-limiting resistor** (typically **220 Ω**) in series.
- ✅ LED forward voltage ($V_F$): **~2V (red)**, **~3.2V (blue/white)**.
- ✅ Arduino GPIO max current: **40 mA absolute**, **20 mA recommended**, **200 mA total** across all pins.
- ✅ Current-limiting resistor formula: $R = \frac{V_S - V_F}{I_{LED}}$.
- ✅ Standard E12 resistor chosen: **220 Ω** (for red LED at 15 mA).
- ✅ `pinMode(pin, OUTPUT)` → sets pin direction.
- ✅ `digitalWrite(pin, HIGH/LOW)` → drives pin to 5V or 0V.
- ✅ `delay(ms)` → blocks CPU for given milliseconds (max ~16383 per call due to `unsigned long` size, but modern boards accept larger values).
- ✅ **Duty Cycle** = $\frac{T_{ON}}{T_{ON} + T_{OFF}} \times 100$.
- ✅ **Blink Frequency** = $\frac{1}{T_{ON} + T_{OFF}}$.
- ✅ For **simultaneous blinking**, use `millis()`-based non-blocking logic.
- ✅ For **sequential blinking**, `delay()` is acceptable and simpler.
- ✅ **Anode (long leg)** of LED → resistor → Arduino pin; **Cathode (short leg)** → GND.
- ✅ **Persistance of Vision** → at >24 Hz, blinking LED appears as constant dim light → foundation of PWM.
- ✅ Standard Arduino **baud rate** for Serial Monitor: **9600 baud**.
- ✅ Arduino UNO clock speed: **16 MHz** (ATmega328P).

---

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Lab Tip:** Always bring a **220 Ω resistor**, a **red LED**, **jumper wires**, and your **USB cable** to the lab. Before connecting the hardware, get the program verified by your instructor. A common KTU lab evaluation rubric awards 50% marks for **working code demonstration** and 50% for **viva + circuit understanding**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Arduino Based Voltage Measurement of 12V Solar PV Module/12V Battery and Displaying the Measured Value Using I2C LCD Display
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL508 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Arduino Based Voltage Measurement of 12V Solar PV Module/12V Battery and Displaying the Measured Value Using I2C LCD Display

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Voltage Measurement in Embedded Systems** is the process of acquiring an analog potential difference from a real-world source (such as a 12V Solar Photovoltaic (PV) Module or a 12V Lead-Acid Battery), conditioning it to a safe, measurable range, sampling it through an Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC), and rendering the digitized result on a Human-Machine Interface (HMI) such as an I2C-driven Liquid Crystal Display (LCD).

In the context of the **Arduino Uno (ATmega328P)** platform, this experiment integrates three core embedded subsystems:

1. **Analog Front-End (AFE):** A **resistive voltage divider network** used to scale the high-voltage DC source down to the microcontroller's safe ADC input range of **0 V – 5 V**.
2. **Data Acquisition Subsystem:** The internal **10-bit Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC** of the ATmega328P, which maps the scaled analog voltage to an integer value in the range **0 to 1023**.
3. **Display Subsystem:** A **16x2 Alphanumeric LCD** driven over the **Inter-Integrated Circuit (I2C)** two-wire serial bus, communicating via the **PCF8574 I/O Expander** at a typical address of **0x27**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCEOL508 - Module 2):**
> Students must be able to (i) interface analog sensors/sources with Arduino using a voltage divider, (ii) program the `analogRead()` API, (iii) convert raw ADC counts to actual engineering units (volts), and (iv) display real-time data on an I2C LCD using the `LiquidCrystal_I2C` library.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **giant water tank (the 12V battery)** that you want to monitor. The tank is so tall that if you tried to pour all its water into a small cup, the cup would overflow. To solve this, you use a **siphon tube with a specific diameter ratio** (the voltage divider) that draws off only a proportional, safe sample of the total water. You then pour this small sample into a **measuring beaker with 1023 fine markings** (the 10-bit ADC). Finally, you **write the reading on a sticky note** (the I2C LCD) so anyone can read it instantly.

**The Three Laws of This Experiment (in plain English):**
- ⚡ **Law 1 — The Big Voltage Problem:** Arduino cannot taste voltages above **5V**; it will burn out. We must shrink the signal first.
- ⚡ **Law 2 — The Digital Ruler:** Arduino's ruler has exactly **1024 marks** (0 to 1023), so each mark = **5V / 1023 ≈ 4.88 mV** in ADC resolution.
- ⚡ **Law 3 — The Language of LCDs:** Instead of using 16 messy wires, I2C uses only **2 wires (SDA & SCL)** to send characters — like a polite two-person telephone call.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not connect the 12V directly?**
> The absolute maximum rating on any ATmega328P I/O pin is **Vcc + 0.5V**. Applying 12V directly destroys the microcontroller instantly. The voltage divider is not optional — it is a **mandatory protective interface**.

## 1.3 Standard Physical & Electrical Constants

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|----------|--------|-------|------|
| ADC Reference Voltage (Default) | $V_{ref}$ | **5.0** | V |
| ADC Resolution | $n$ | **10** | bits |
| Total ADC Steps | $2^{10}$ | **1024** (0 to 1023) | counts |
| I2C Clock Frequency (Standard Mode) | $f_{SCL}$ | **100** | kHz |
| Typical LCD I2C Address | $A_{LCD}$ | **0x27** (or 0x3F) | hex |
| Solar PV Open Circuit Voltage (12V panel) | $V_{OC}$ | ≈ **21 – 22** | V |
| Battery Full Charge Voltage (12V Lead-Aid) | $V_{full}$ | ≈ **13.8 – 14.4** | V |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Voltage Divider Theorem — The Heart of the Experiment

A **voltage divider** is a passive linear circuit consisting of two resistors ($R_1$ and $R_2$) connected in series across a voltage source ($V_{IN}$). The output voltage ($V_{OUT}$) is tapped from the junction between the two resistors.

### Step-by-Step Operating Logic

1. **Current Flow:** A small current $I$ flows through $R_1$ and $R_2$ in series.
2. **Ohm's Law Application:** The current is governed by the total resistance: $I = V_{IN} / (R_1 + R_2)$.
3. **Voltage Drop Across $R_2$:** The voltage at the divider's midpoint is the drop across $R_2$ only.
4. **Proportional Scaling:** $V_{OUT}$ is always a fixed *fraction* of $V_{IN}$, independent of load current (assuming negligible ADC input current).
5. **Scaling Ratio:** The ratio $R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$ is called the **divider ratio** or **attenuation factor (k)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for Solar/Battery:** A 12V battery can spike up to **14.4V** during charging. A 12V solar panel can reach **22V** at open circuit. We must design the divider so that the **maximum expected voltage** maps to **≤ 5.0V** at the Arduino pin.

## 2.2 Choosing the Divider Resistors

The KTU-standard choice for this experiment is:

- **$R_1$ = 30 kΩ** (top resistor, connected to the positive terminal of the source)
- **$R_2$ = 10 kΩ** (bottom resistor, connected to GND)

This gives a divider ratio of:
$$k = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{10\text{k}}{30\text{k} + 10\text{k}} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25$$

**Maximum measurable input voltage:** $V_{IN,max} = 5.0\text{V} / 0.25 = 20\text{V}$

This comfortably covers the 12V battery range (10V – 14.4V) and most of the solar panel range (open-circuit spikes of 18-20V).

> [!WARNING]
> **Power Dissipation Trap:** The current through the divider at 22V is $I = 22 / 40\text{k} = 0.55$ mA. Total power dissipated is $P = V^2/R = 22^2/40\text{k} = 12.1$ mW. Always use **¼ W resistors** at minimum; ⅛ W resistors will overheat over time.

## 2.3 The Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC) Pipeline

The ATmega328P uses a **Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC** with the following conversion chain:

| Stage | Operation | Range |
|-------|-----------|-------|
| 1. Sensing | $V_{IN}$ enters ADC pin (A0) | 0V – 5V |
| 2. Sampling | Sample-and-hold capacitor charges | ~ 1.5 ADC clock cycles |
| 3. Quantization | Binary search through 10 bits | $2^{10} = 1024$ levels |
| 4. Output | Integer stored in `ADC` register | 0 – 1023 |

The fundamental ADC equation:
$$V_{measured} = \frac{ADC_{value} \times V_{ref}}{1023}$$

Since the divider attenuates the input by factor $k$, the **actual source voltage** is:
$$V_{actual} = \frac{ADC_{value} \times V_{ref}}{1023 \times k}$$

## 2.4 I2C Communication Protocol — The Two-Wire Bus

**Inter-Integrated Circuit (I2C)** is a synchronous, multi-master, multi-slave serial communication bus invented by **Philips Semiconductor (now NXP)** in 1982.

| Signal | Full Name | Function | Arduino Uno Pin |
|--------|-----------|----------|-----------------|
| **SDA** | Serial Data | Bidirectional data line | **A4** |
| **SCL** | Serial Clock | Master-generated clock | **A5** |
| GND | Ground | Common reference | GND |
| VCC | Power | +5V supply to LCD backpack | 5V |

**Key I2C Protocol Mechanics:**
- **START condition:** SDA falls while SCL is HIGH.
- **Address frame:** Master sends the 7-bit slave address + 1 R/W bit.
- **ACK/NACK:** The receiver pulls SDA LOW to acknowledge each byte.
- **Data frame:** 8 bits per byte, MSB first.
- **STOP condition:** SDA rises while SCL is HIGH.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why pull-up resistors are mandatory:** I2C uses **open-drain** outputs. Both SDA and SCL lines must be pulled HIGH to VCC through resistors (typically **4.7 kΩ**). The PCF8574 backpack on most I2C LCDs has these resistors **soldered onboard**, so no external pull-ups are needed in this experiment.

## 2.5 The PCF8574 I/O Expander — Bridge Between I2C and LCD

The LCD's parallel interface requires **6 GPIO pins** (RS, EN, D4, D5, D6, D7). Arduino's I2C pins (A4, A5) cannot drive this directly. The **PCF8574** chip acts as a **serial-to-parallel converter**:

1. Arduino sends commands/characters over I2C (2 wires).
2. PCF8574 receives the byte and latches it on its 8 output pins.
3. The LCD reads the parallel data and displays the character.

The `LiquidCrystal_I2C` library handles this transparently — the programmer never manually toggles the PCF8574.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Unit / Notes |
|---|--------------|----------|--------------|
| 1 | Voltage Divider Output | $V_{OUT} = V_{IN} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}$ | Volts (V) |
| 2 | Attenuation Factor (k) | $k = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | Actual Source Voltage | $V_{IN} = \dfrac{V_{OUT}}{k} = V_{OUT} \cdot \dfrac{R_1+R_2}{R_2}$ | Volts (V) |
| 4 | ADC Voltage Conversion | $V_{ADC} = \dfrac{ADC \times V_{ref}}{1023}$ | Volts (V) |
| 5 | Combined End-to-End | $V_{IN} = \dfrac{ADC \times V_{ref} \times (R_1+R_2)}{1023 \cdot R_2}$ | Volts (V) |
| 6 | ADC Resolution | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{n}-1} = \dfrac{5.0}{1023}$ | ≈ 4.888 mV/step |
| 7 | Max Measurable Input | $V_{IN,max} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{k}$ | Limited by k |
| 8 | Divider Current (Quiescent) | $I_{div} = \dfrac{V_{IN}}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Amperes (A) |
| 9 | Power in $R_1$ | $P_{R_1} = I_{div}^2 \cdot R_1$ | Watts (W) |
| 10 | I2C Clock Period (100 kHz) | $T_{SCL} = \dfrac{1}{f_{SCL}} = 10\ \mu s$ | Microseconds |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

This exact architecture is the foundation of every modern **Battery Management System (BMS)**, **Solar Charge Controller**, and **Uninterruptible Power Supply (UPS) monitor**. Industrial applications include:

- ☀️ **Off-grid solar installations** — remote panel voltage telemetry.
- 🚗 **Electric Vehicle (EV) Battery Packs** — cell voltage monitoring (BMS).
- 🏭 **Telecom Tower Backup** — 48V battery string health monitoring.
- 🏥 **Medical Equipment** — portable defibrillator battery status.
- 🤖 **Robotics** — lead-acid battery state-of-charge estimation.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From ADC Count to Real-World Voltage

We will derive the complete end-to-end formula that converts the raw 10-bit integer from `analogRead()` to the actual source voltage, with **every algebraic step explicitly shown**.

### Step 1 — The Raw ADC Reading
The Arduino API `analogRead(A0)` returns an integer $D$ in the closed interval $[0, 1023]$. This represents the discretized version of the analog voltage present at pin A0.

$$D = \lfloor 1023 \cdot \frac{V_{ADC}}{V_{ref}} + 0.5 \rfloor \approx \frac{V_{ADC}}{V_{ref}} \cdot 1023$$

**Conversion logic:** The ADC normalizes the input voltage by the reference voltage to get a fractional number between 0 and 1, then scales it to the integer range 0–1023.

### Step 2 — Inverting the ADC Equation
We solve the previous relation for $V_{ADC}$ (the voltage at the Arduino pin):

$$V_{ADC} = D \cdot \frac{V_{ref}}{1023}$$

**Conversion logic:** Multiply both sides by $V_{ref}/1023$ to isolate $V_{ADC}$.

### Step 3 — Applying the Voltage Divider Forward Equation
The voltage divider (forward direction) tells us what the scaled-down voltage is:

$$V_{ADC} = V_{IN} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = V_{IN} \cdot k$$

**Conversion logic:** The voltage at the divider midpoint is the input voltage multiplied by the divider ratio k.

### Step 4 — Inverting the Divider Equation
We solve for the original source voltage $V_{IN}$:

$$V_{IN} = \frac{V_{ADC}}{k} = V_{ADC} \cdot \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_2}$$

**Conversion logic:** Since the divider shrinks the signal, we must multiply (not divide) by the inverse of k to recover the original.

### Step 5 — Substituting Step 2 into Step 4
This combines the ADC and divider relations into a single end-to-end formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{IN} &= \left( D \cdot \frac{V_{ref}}{1023} \right) \cdot \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_2} \\[6pt]
V_{IN} &= D \cdot \frac{V_{ref} \cdot (R_1 + R_2)}{1023 \cdot R_2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion logic:** We replace $V_{ADC}$ with its ADC-derived expression, yielding a single equation that takes the raw ADC count and outputs the actual source voltage.

### Step 6 — Plugging in the Standard Resistor Values
For $R_1 = 30\text{ k}\Omega$ and $R_2 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_2} &= \frac{30\text{k} + 10\text{k}}{10\text{k}} = \frac{40}{10} = 4
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion logic:** The factor (R1+R2)/R2 is the inverse of the attenuation ratio. With our 30k/10k divider, this factor is exactly 4.

### Step 7 — The Final Working Formula

$$\boxed{\,V_{IN} = D \cdot \frac{V_{ref}}{1023} \cdot 4 = D \cdot \frac{5.0 \times 4}{1023} = D \cdot 0.01955\,}$$

**Conversion logic:** Numerically, each ADC count corresponds to ~19.55 mV of actual source voltage. The constant `0.01955` (or its float equivalent) is what gets multiplied in the Arduino sketch.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (For Lab Record)

**Given:**
- $R_1 = 30\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$
- $V_{ref} = 5.0$ V
- Solar panel open-circuit voltage $V_{IN} = 18.0$ V (assumed true value)

**Step A — Predict the ADC reading:**
$$D = V_{IN} \cdot \frac{1023}{V_{ref}} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} = 18.0 \cdot \frac{1023}{5.0} \cdot \frac{10}{40}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
D &= 18.0 \cdot 204.6 \cdot 0.25 \\
D &= 18.0 \cdot 51.15 \\
D &= 920.7 \approx 921
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion logic:** We invert the end-to-end formula to predict what ADC count we should observe if the source really is 18V.

**Step B — Verify by running the formula forward:**
$$V_{IN,recovered} = 921 \cdot \frac{5.0 \times 40}{1023 \times 10} = 921 \cdot 0.01955 = 18.005 \text{ V}$$

**Conversion logic:** Going forward with the same formula should reproduce the original voltage (within rounding error). The match confirms the formula is correct.

## 3.3 Average-Based Filtering Derivative

To reduce noise (ADC readings fluctuate by ±2-3 counts due to switching power supply ripple and sensor noise), we take the **mean of N samples**:

$$\bar{D} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} D_i$$

The standard deviation of the mean decreases as:
$$\sigma_{\bar{D}} = \frac{\sigma_D}{\sqrt{N}}$$

For $N = 50$ samples, the noise is reduced by a factor of $\sqrt{50} \approx 7.07$. This is implemented in software as a simple `for` loop that sums 50 readings and divides by 50.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Divider Resistor Selection

| Target Max $V_{IN}$ | Recommended $R_1$ | Recommended $R_2$ | Ratio $(R_1+R_2)/R_2$ | Notes |
|---------------------|-------------------|-------------------|------------------------|-------|
| 5.0 V (no divider) | 0 Ω | ∞ | 1.0 | Direct connection (unsafe above 5V) |
| 10.0 V | 10 kΩ | 10 kΩ | 2.0 | 1:1 divider |
| **20.0 V** | **30 kΩ** | **10 kΩ** | **4.0** | **KTU standard for 12V systems** |
| 25.0 V | 40 kΩ | 10 kΩ | 5.0 | 24V battery systems |
| 50.0 V | 90 kΩ | 10 kΩ | 10.0 | 48V telecom battery |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Complete Circuit Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PV[12V Solar PV Module\nor 12V Battery] --> R1[R1 = 30kΩ]
    R1 --> MID((Analog Node\nA0 Junction))
    MID --> R2[R2 = 10kΩ]
    R2 --> GND1[Arduino GND]
    MID --> A0[Arduino Pin A0\nADC Input]
    GND1 --> AGND[Common Ground]
    A0 --> ADC[ATmega328P\n10-bit SAR ADC]
    ADC --> MCU[MCU Firmware\nanalogRead and math]
    MCU --> I2C1[I2C Bus SDA Pin A4]
    MCU --> I2C2[I2C Bus SCL Pin A5]
    I2C1 --> PCF[PCF8574\nI/O Expander]
    I2C2 --> PCF
    PCF --> LCD[16x2 LCD Display\nHD44780 Compatible]
    VCC[Arduino 5V] --> LCDVCC[LCD VCC]
    GND1 --> LCDGND[LCD GND]
    PV --> SRCGND[Source Negative]
    SRCGND --> GND1
```

**Reading this diagram:**
- The **left side** is the high-voltage analog world (battery/solar panel).
- The **middle** is the signal conditioning layer (resistor divider).
- The **right side** is the digital processing and display layer.

## 4.2 Software / Firmware Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INIT([Power ON / Reset]) --> SETUP[setup: Initialize\nLCD and Serial]
    SETUP --> LOOP([loop: Forever])
    LOOP --> READ[analogRead A0\nReturn D in 0-1023]
    READ --> SUM[Add D to running sum\nRepeat 50 times]
    SUM --> DECISION{Done 50\nsamples?}
    DECISION -->|No| READ
    DECISION -->|Yes| AVERAGE[Compute mean D_avg]
    AVERAGE --> CONVERT[V_in = D_avg * 5.0\ntimes 4 / 1023]
    CONVERT --> PRINT_LCD[LCD setCursor 0,0\nprint Voltage string]
    PRINT_LCD --> PRINT_SERIAL[Serial.println\ndebug output]
    PRINT_SERIAL --> DELAY[delay 1000 ms]
    DELAY --> LOOP
```

## 4.3 I2C Communication Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant ARD as Arduino Master
    participant PCF as PCF8574 Slave 0x27
    participant LCD as HD44780 LCD
    Note over ARD,LCD: I2C address 0x27, SDA=A4, SCL=A5
    ARD->>PCF: START condition
    ARD->>PCF: Address 0x27 + Write bit
    PCF-->>ARD: ACK
    ARD->>PCF: Control byte EN=1, RS=0 (Command mode)
    PCF-->>ARD: ACK
    ARD->>PCF: High nibble of command (e.g., 0x30)
    PCF-->>ARD: ACK
    ARD->>PCF: EN=0 to latch command
    PCF->>LCD: Parallel write to LCD
    ARD->>PCF: STOP condition
    Note over ARD,LCD: Repeat for each character
    ARD->>PCF: START, Address 0x27, Write
    ARD->>PCF: Data byte ASCII 'V' = 0x56
    PCF->>LCD: Display 'V' on LCD
    ARD->>PCF: STOP
```

## 4.4 Complete Arduino Sketch (Lab-Ready Code)

```cpp
/*-------------------------------------------------------------
 KTU 2024 Scheme - PCEOL508
 Experiment 2: Arduino Voltage Measurement with I2C LCD
---------------------------------------------------------------*/
#include <Wire.h>
#include <LiquidCrystal_I2C.h>

// I2C address 0x27, 16 columns, 2 rows
LiquidCrystal_I2C lcd(0x27, 16, 2);

const int SENSOR_PIN = A0;          // Voltage divider midpoint
const float V_REF    = 5.0;         // Arduino ADC reference
const float DIVIDER_RATIO = 4.0;   // (R1+R2)/R2 with 30k/10k
const int   NSAMPLES  = 50;        // Averaging window

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(9600);
  lcd.init();
  lcd.backlight();
  lcd.setCursor(0, 0);
  lcd.print("Voltage Monitor");
  delay(2000);
  lcd.clear();
}

void loop() {
  long sum = 0;
  for (int i = 0; i < NSAMPLES; i++) {
    sum += analogRead(SENSOR_PIN);
    delay(2);
  }
  float avgADC  = (float)sum / NSAMPLES;
  float voltage = avgADC * (V_REF / 1023.0) * DIVIDER_RATIO;

  // ---- Display on I2C LCD ----
  lcd.setCursor(0, 0);
  lcd.print("Voltage:");
  lcd.setCursor(0, 1);
  lcd.print(voltage, 2);
  lcd.print(" V    ");

  // ---- Debug to Serial Monitor ----
  Serial.print("ADC = ");
  Serial.print(avgADC);
  Serial.print("  V = ");
  Serial.println(voltage, 3);

  delay(1000);
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Line-by-Line Insight:**
> - `lcd.init()` — Initializes the LCD by sending the 4-bit mode command sequence.
> - `lcd.backlight()` — Turns ON the blue/green LED backlight of the LCD.
> - `voltage = avgADC * (5.0/1023.0) * 4.0` — Implements the derived formula $V_{IN} = D \cdot \frac{V_{ref}}{1023} \cdot \frac{R_1+R_2}{R_2}$.
> - `delay(2)` inside the averaging loop gives the ADC ~2 ms to settle between samples (much greater than the 13 ADC clock cycles ≈ 1.6 µs needed).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 (Model)]**
**What is a voltage divider? Why is it necessary when measuring a 12V source with an Arduino Uno?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Allocation: 1 + 2):**

A voltage divider is a series combination of two resistors ($R_1$ and $R_2$) across a voltage source, producing an output voltage $V_{OUT} = V_{IN} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}$ that is a fraction of the input. **(1 mark)**

It is necessary because the ATmega328P microcontroller's ADC input pins have an absolute maximum rating of **Vcc + 0.5V = 5.5V**. A 12V source connected directly would permanently damage the chip. The divider scales the input to a safe 0–5V range, enabling accurate measurement using the internal 10-bit ADC. **(2 marks)**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model)]**
**Explain the role of the PCF8574 chip in an I2C-based LCD module. State the typical I2C address used.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Allocation: 2 + 1):**

The **PCF8574** is an **8-bit I2C-bus I/O expander** manufactured by NXP. It acts as a bridge between the Arduino's 2-wire I2C bus and the LCD's 6-wire parallel interface (RS, EN, D4, D5, D6, D7). It receives serial data from the Arduino and latches it onto its parallel output pins, freeing up Arduino GPIO pins. **(2 marks)**

The typical I2C address is **0x27** (sometimes 0x3F for PCF8574A variant). **(1 mark)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 (Model)]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the design of a voltage divider to measure a 12V battery using an Arduino Uno. Choose appropriate resistor values and justify. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the mathematical relationship between the raw ADC value and the actual battery voltage. What is the maximum battery voltage that can be measured with your chosen divider? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Circuit Diagram (3 Marks):**

```
+12V Battery+ ---+---[ R1 = 30kΩ ]---+--- Arduino A0
                                    |
                                  [ R2 = 10kΩ ]
                                    |
                                  GND ---- Arduino GND --- Battery-
```

**Resistor Selection Logic (2 Marks):**
- The Arduino ADC accepts a maximum of **5.0V**.
- The divider ratio must satisfy: $V_{IN,max} \times \frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} \leq 5.0$V
- For 12V systems that may spike to **14.4V** during charging, we choose $R_1 = 30\text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 10\text{k}\Omega$.
- This gives $k = 0.25$, so a 20V input produces exactly 5V output — safe margin.

**Justification (2 Marks):**
- High resistor values (40kΩ total) limit quiescent current to ~0.3 mA at 12V, preserving battery life.
- Standard 1% tolerance resistors (E12 series) provide adequate accuracy for educational purposes.
- The 30kΩ/10kΩ combination is a 4:1 ratio, simplifying software calculations.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Derivation (5 Marks):**

**Step 1:** The ADC output equation:
$$D = \frac{V_{ADC}}{V_{ref}} \times 1023 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{ADC} = D \times \frac{V_{ref}}{1023}$$

**Step 2:** The voltage divider forward equation:
$$V_{ADC} = V_{IN} \times \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 3:** Equating and solving for $V_{IN}$:
$$V_{IN} = D \times \frac{V_{ref}}{1023} \times \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_2}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting $R_1 = 30\text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 10\text{k}\Omega$, $V_{ref} = 5.0$V:
$$V_{IN} = D \times \frac{5.0}{1023} \times \frac{40}{10} = D \times 0.01955 \text{ V}$$

**Maximum Measurable Voltage (2 Marks):**
$$V_{IN,max} = \frac{V_{ref}}{k} = \frac{5.0}{0.25} = 20.0 \text{ V}$$

This covers the full 12V battery range (10V–14.4V) with a 5.6V safety margin.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model)]**

**(a)** Explain the I2C communication protocol. List the functions of SDA, SCL, and the PCF8574 in the context of interfacing a 16x2 LCD with Arduino. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write the complete Arduino program (with comments) to measure a solar panel voltage through a voltage divider and display it on an I2C LCD with the format "Voltage: XX.XX V". Use a moving average of 50 samples. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**I2C Protocol (3 Marks):**
I2C (Inter-Integrated Circuit) is a **synchronous, half-duplex, multi-master, multi-slave** serial bus developed by Philips (1982). It uses **two bidirectional lines**:
- **SDA (Serial Data):** Carries address and data bits.
- **SCL (Serial Clock):** Generated by the master; synchronizes all transactions.

**Key signals:** START (SDA↓ while SCL=HIGH), STOP (SDA↑ while SCL=HIGH), ACK (receiver pulls SDA LOW).

**SDA, SCL & PCF8574 Roles (4 Marks):**
| Element | Function |
|---------|----------|
| **SDA (Arduino A4)** | Transmits 7-bit slave address + R/W bit, then data bytes |
| **SCL (Arduino A5)** | Provides 100 kHz clock pulses; each bit is sampled on SCL's rising edge |
| **PCF8574** | Receives serial I2C data and converts to 8-bit parallel output for the LCD; eliminates need for 6 Arduino GPIO pins |

The typical slave address is **0x27** (or 0x3F). Pull-up resistors (4.7 kΩ to 5V) on SDA and SCL are required — usually pre-soldered on the LCD backpack module.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

```cpp
#include <Wire.h>
#include <LiquidCrystal_I2C.h>

LiquidCrystal_I2C lcd(0x27, 16, 2);   // 1 mark for includes & object
const int SENSOR_PIN = A0;
const float V_REF = 5.0;
const float DIVIDER_RATIO = 4.0;       // 30k/10k divider
const int NSAMPLES = 50;                // 1 mark for moving average

void setup() {                          // 1 mark for setup
  Serial.begin(9600);
  lcd.init();
  lcd.backlight();
  lcd.setCursor(0, 0);
  lcd.print("Solar Monitor");
  delay(1500);
  lcd.clear();
}

void loop() {                           // 4 marks for loop
  long sum = 0;
  for (int i = 0; i < NSAMPLES; i++) {  // Averaging loop
    sum += analogRead(SENSOR_PIN);
    delay(2);
  }
  float avgADC  = (float)sum / NSAMPLES;
  float voltage = avgADC * (V_REF / 1023.0) * DIVIDER_RATIO;  // 1 mark for formula

  lcd.setCursor(0, 0);                  // 1 mark for LCD print
  lcd.print("Voltage: ");
  lcd.print(voltage, 2);
  lcd.print(" V   ");

  Serial.print("V = ");                 // Optional debug
  Serial.println(voltage, 2);
  delay(1000);
}
```

**Mark Allocation Breakdown:**
- Correct library inclusion & LCD initialization: **1 Mark**
- Pin definitions and constants: **1 Mark**
- Setup function with LCD backlight: **1 Mark**
- 50-sample moving average loop: **2 Marks**
- Voltage conversion formula `D * 5.0/1023 * 4`: **1 Mark**
- LCD print with proper format `XX.XX V`: **1 Mark**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - ⚠️ **Forgetting the factor of 4:** Students often compute only $V_{ADC}$ and display that as the "voltage", losing **3-4 marks** instantly. The end-to-end formula *must* include the divider inversion $(R_1+R_2)/R_2$.
> - ⚠️ **Using 1024 instead of 1023:** The 10-bit ADC has 1024 *levels* (0 to 1023). The denominator is 1023, not 1024. Off-by-one errors cost marks.
> - ⚠️ **Wrong I2C address:** The address is **0x27** for PCF8574 and **0x3F** for PCF8574A. If the LCD shows blocks but no text, use the `I2C Scanner` sketch first.
> - ⚠️ **No power supply common ground:** The Arduino GND and the battery/solar negative terminal **MUST** be connected together. Floating grounds give garbage readings.
> - ⚠️ **No current limiting in divider:** Don't use 1Ω resistors — current will be huge and the resistors will burn. Use at least 10kΩ total.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

✅ **Three mandatory hardware components:** Voltage divider (R1, R2), Arduino Uno, I2C LCD (with PCF8574 backpack).

✅ **Voltage divider formula:** $V_{OUT} = V_{IN} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}$. For 12V systems use **30kΩ + 10kΩ** (ratio = 4).

✅ **ADC fundamentals:** ATmega328P has a **10-bit SAR ADC** with **1024 levels** (0–1023) and **5.0V default reference**. Each step = **4.888 mV**.

✅ **End-to-end voltage formula (MEMORIZE):**
$$V_{IN} = D \times \frac{V_{ref}}{1023} \times \frac{R_1+R_2}{R_2}$$

✅ **For 30k/10k divider:** the multiplier simplifies to **0.01955 V per ADC count**.

✅ **I2C pins on Arduino Uno:** **SDA = A4**, **SCL = A5** (hardware-defined, cannot be changed on Uno).

✅ **I2C LCD address:** typically **0x27** (PCF8574) or **0x3F** (PCF8574A). Always run an I2C scanner if unsure.

✅ **I2C clock speed (standard mode):** **100 kHz**, period = **10 µs**. Fast mode is 400 kHz.

✅ **Required Arduino libraries:** `Wire.h` (built-in) and `LiquidCrystal_I2C.h` (third-party, install via Library Manager).

✅ **Key LiquidCrystal_I2C functions:** `lcd.init()`, `lcd.backlight()`, `lcd.setCursor(col, row)`, `lcd.print("text")`, `lcd.clear()`.

✅ **Software filtering:** Use **moving average of 50 samples** to reduce ADC noise. Standard deviation reduces by factor $\sqrt{N}$.

✅ **Common ground is NON-NEGOTIABLE** — Arduino GND must tie to the source's negative terminal.

✅ **Maximum input voltage** for a 4:1 divider = **20V** (5V × 4). Anything above this saturates the ADC at 1023.

✅ **Solar panel safety:** A 12V panel can reach **22V at open circuit** — never disconnect it under load, and never connect it to Arduino without the divider.

✅ **Battery charging spike:** A 12V lead-acid battery can reach **14.4V** during absorption charging — your divider must handle this safely.

✅ **Lab Viva Quick-Fire Questions:**
- *Q: Why 1023 and not 1024?* A: 10 bits = $2^{10}$ levels = 0 to **1023** (count starts at zero).
- *Q: What happens if R2 is 0?* A: Output is shorted to ground — ADC reads 0 — and $R_1$ burns.
- *Q: Why use I2C instead of parallel LCD?* A: Saves 4 Arduino pins (only 2 used); allows multiple devices on the same bus.
- *Q: What is the resolution in mV?* A: $5/1023 \times 4 = 19.55$ mV per ADC step (smallest detectable voltage change).
- *Q: Can we measure AC voltage with this circuit?* A: No, the ADC reads only positive DC (0–5V). For AC, we need rectification and biasing.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Demo Experiments on Arduino/Raspberry Pi to Upload/Retrieve Temperature and Humidity Data to ThingSpeak Cloud
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL508 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Demo Experiments on Arduino/Raspberry Pi to Upload/Retrieve Temperature and Humidity Data to ThingSpeak Cloud

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**ThingSpeak** is an **Internet of Things (IoT) analytics platform service** hosted in the cloud that allows embedded systems (such as Arduino, ESP8266/ESP32, and Raspberry Pi) to **aggregate, visualize, and analyze live data streams** using RESTful API calls over HTTP/HTTPS or MQTT protocols. In the context of the KTU PCEOL508 lab syllabus, the experiment demonstrates a **sensor-to-cloud data pipeline** where temperature and humidity readings from a **DHT11/DHT22 digital sensor** are pushed to ThingSpeak fields and later retrieved for graphical plotting and remote monitoring.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (Module Experiment 2):**
> The experiment tests the following Course Outcomes (COs):
> - **CO2:** Interface sensors with microcontroller/SoC platforms.
> - **CO3:** Implement IoT communication protocols for cloud data exchange.
> - **CO4:** Develop end-to-end embedded solutions with cloud integration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Canonical Definitions for Board Examination:**
> - **IoT (Internet of Things):** A network of physical objects ("things") embedded with sensors, software, and connectivity that enables them to collect and exchange data over the internet.
> - **Cloud Computing (in IoT context):** On-demand availability of compute, storage, and analytics resources over the internet without direct active management by the user.
> - **REST API (Representational State Transfer):** An architectural style that uses HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) for stateless communication between client and server.
> - **DHT11/DHT22:** Low-cost digital sensors that measure both temperature and relative humidity using a thermistor and capacitive humidity sensor.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Postbox" Model

Think of ThingSpeak as a **high-tech digital postbox floating in the sky**:

- 📬 **The Postbox (ThingSpeak Channel):** Each user gets a private numbered channel (like a P.O. Box). Inside it are 8 small slots (Field 1 through Field 8) where letters (numeric data) can be dropped.
- 🏠 **Your House (Arduino/Raspberry Pi):** Lives far away, has a thermometer and hygrometer (DHT sensor) that constantly record weather.
- ✉️ **The Postman (HTTP POST Request):** Carries your weather readings from the house to the postbox every 20 seconds.
- 📊 **The Dashboard (ThingSpeak Web App):** Anyone with the read API key can open the postbox's display window and see a beautiful graph of the temperature and humidity over time.

> [!TIP]
> **Why Cloud + IoT?** Imagine you want to monitor the climate of a greenhouse in Munnar from your hostel in Thiruvananthapuram. Without the cloud, you'd need a long physical wire. With ThingSpeak, the Arduino in Munnar simply "calls" a server over WiFi, and you view the live chart from anywhere on Earth.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

| Metric | Value | Context |
|---|---|---|
| **DHT11 Temperature Range** | **0 °C to 50 °C** | Standard operational range |
| **DHT11 Humidity Range** | **20% to 90% RH** | Relative Humidity |
| **DHT11 Sampling Rate** | **1 Hz (1 sample/second)** | Minimum 2-second interval between reads |
| **DHT11 Accuracy (Temp)** | **±2 °C** | At 25 °C ambient |
| **DHT11 Accuracy (Humidity)** | **±5% RH** | At 25 °C ambient |
| **DHT11 Operating Voltage** | **3 V to 5.5 V DC** | Compatible with Arduino 5V logic |
| **ThingSpeak Free Update Interval** | **15 seconds minimum** | Enforced by server |
| **HTTP POST Payload Limit** | **~8 KB per request** | Sufficient for sensor data |
| **WiFi Standard (ESP8266)** | **IEEE 802.11 b/g/n** | 2.4 GHz band only |
| **MQTT Default Port** | **1883 (TCP)** | 8883 for TLS (secure) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations of the Sensor–Cloud Pipeline

The data flow in this experiment follows a **4-layer IoT architecture** recognized by KTU and IEEE:

1. **Perception Layer (Sensor):** Physical world → DHT11 digitizes analog temperature/humidity.
2. **Network Layer (WiFi Module):** Microcontroller → ESP8266/NodeMCU → TCP/IP stack → Internet.
3. **Processing Layer (Cloud):** ThingSpeak server receives HTTP POST, stores in MySQL backend, timestamps each entry.
4. **Application Layer (Visualization):** MATLAB-powered ThingSpeak web app renders live line graphs, gauges, and computes statistics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why ESP8266/NodeMCU for KTU Labs?** The KTU 2024 syllabus recommends **NodeMCU (ESP8266-based)** or **Raspberry Pi** because they bundle WiFi directly. A standard Arduino Uno needs an external ESP-01 or Ethernet shield, which complicates the lab.

## 2.2 Operational Breakdown — Step-by-Step Logic

### **Stage A: Sensor Acquisition**
- The DHT11 uses a **single-wire bidirectional protocol** (not I²C, not SPI).
- The microcontroller sends a **start signal** by pulling the data line LOW for **at least 18 ms**.
- The DHT11 then transmits a **40-bit data packet**:
  - **8 bits** integer part of humidity
  - **8 bits** decimal part of humidity
  - **8 bits** integer part of temperature
  - **8 bits** decimal part of temperature
  - **8 bits** parity checksum (sum of first 4 bytes)
- The library (e.g., `DHT.h` by Adafruit) handles all the microsecond-level timing.

### **Stage B: WiFi Connection**
- The ESP8266 joins the local WiFi Access Point using **WPA2-PSK** credentials.
- It obtains a **DHCP-assigned IP address** automatically.
- It opens a **TCP socket on port 80** to communicate with `api.thingspeak.com`.

### **Stage C: ThingSpeak API Call (Write Operation)**
- The HTTP POST URL is constructed as:
  `http://api.thingspeak.com/update?api_key=XXXXX&field1=TEMP&field2=HUM`
- The server responds with the **entry number** (a positive integer) on success, or `0` on failure.
- **Rate limiting:** ThingSpeak enforces a **15-second minimum** between writes for free accounts.

### **Stage D: Data Retrieval (Read Operation)**
- Two methods exist:
  - **Method 1:** Visit the public/private channel URL in a browser.
  - **Method 2:** Use HTTP GET with the **Read API Key**:
    `https://api.thingspeak.com/channels/CHANNEL_ID/feeds.json?api_key=READ_KEY&results=10`
- The response is a **JSON document** containing the last N entries.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all key equations and protocols** a KTU examiner may test. Memorize the structure, not the exact numbers.

| # | Concept | Formula / Protocol | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Relative Humidity (RH) | $RH = \frac{P_{water}}{P_{saturation}} \times 100$ | **%** | Ratio of partial vapor pressures |
| 2 | Dew Point (approximation) | $T_d = T - \frac{100 - RH}{5}$ | **°C** | Magnus-Tetens simplified form |
| 3 | Heat Index (apparent temp) | $HI = -42.379 + 2.049 T + 10.143 RH - 0.225 T \cdot RH$ | **°F** | Used by NWS; convert to °C if needed |
| 4 | HTTP POST URL length | $L = L_{base} + L_{key} + L_{fields}$ | **bytes** | Must be < 2048 for safety |
| 5 | Update Frequency | $f_{upd} = \frac{1}{T_{delay}}$ | **Hz** | ThingSpeak $\geq 0.067$ Hz (15 s) |
| 6 | Daily Message Quota (Free) | $N_{max} = \frac{86400}{15} = 5760$ | **msgs/day** | Hard server limit |
| 7 | Data Throughput | $\Theta = N_{fields} \times S_{field} \times f_{upd}$ | **bytes/sec** | $S_{field} \approx 8$ bytes (float) |
| 8 | DHT11 Bit Time Slot | $\tau = 50 \text{ μs (0)} \text{ vs } 70 \text{ μs (1)}$ | **μs** | Decoded by MCU via pulse-width |
| 9 | Start Signal Duration | $t_{start} \geq 18 \text{ ms}$ | **ms** | MCU pulls data line LOW |
| 10 | WiFi TX Power (ESP8266) | $P_{tx} = 0 \text{ to } 20.5 \text{ dBm}$ | **dBm** | Configurable via `WiFi.setSleepMode()` |
| 11 | JSON Field Mapping | $F_1 \rightarrow T, \ F_2 \rightarrow RH$ | **—** | Configurable in channel settings |
| 12 | DHT11 Checksum | $CS = (B_1 + B_2 + B_3 + B_4) \pmod{256}$ | **byte** | Last 8 bits must equal $B_5$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** In the 14-mark questions, students who draw the **data flow diagram** AND write the **HTTP URL structure** usually score 4-5 extra marks vs those who only write code.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Precision Agriculture:** Greenhouses in Wayanad monitor humidity to trigger automatic misters.
- **Cold Chain Logistics:** Pharmaceutical companies track vaccine temperatures during transit.
- **Smart Home HVAC:** Air conditioners auto-adjust based on cloud-received humidity.
- **Industrial Predictive Maintenance:** Server rooms alert staff when humidity crosses **60% RH** (condensation risk).
- **Weather Stations:** Low-cost citizen-science stations push data to public ThingSpeak channels.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# ⚙️ SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Code Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** This is a **LAB course** (PCEOL508), so the question paper focuses on **circuit diagrams, code analysis, output interpretation, and debugging scenarios** — not just theory. Memorize the code flow!

## 3.1 Hardware Setup and Pin Configuration

**Component List:**
1. NodeMCU (ESP8266) **OR** Arduino Uno + ESP-01 **OR** Raspberry Pi 3/4
2. DHT11 or DHT22 sensor module
3. Breadboard and jumper wires
4. USB cable for programming
5. 10 kΩ pull-up resistor (only for raw DHT11, not the module)

**Pin Connection Matrix:**

| DHT11 Pin | NodeMCU Pin | Arduino Uno Pin | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **VCC (+)** | **3V3** | **5V** | Power supply |
| **DATA (OUT)** | **D4 (GPIO2)** | **D2** | Bidirectional data line |
| **GND (-)** | **GND** | **GND** | Common ground |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Viva Question Alert:** *"Why do we need a 10 kΩ pull-up resistor on the DHT11 data line?"*
> **Answer:** The DHT11 uses an open-drain output. The pull-up resistor ensures the line returns to HIGH (5V/3.3V) when no device is driving it. Without it, the line **floats**, leading to corrupted readings and intermittent `NaN` errors.

## 3.2 Complete Arduino/NodeMCU Code (Step-by-Step Annotated)

The following is the **canonical, KTU-approved code** for this experiment. Each block is annotated for examination purposes.

```cpp
/*============================================================
 * Experiment 2: DHT11 to ThingSpeak Cloud
 * Platform: NodeMCU (ESP8266) + Arduino IDE
 * Library: DHT sensor library by Adafruit
 *============================================================*/

#include <ESP8266WiFi.h>          // Built-in WiFi library for NodeMCU
#include <ThingSpeak.h>           // ThingSpeak communication library
#include <DHT.h>                  // Adafruit DHT sensor library

// --------- Step 1: Define sensor and WiFi credentials ---------
#define DHTPIN    D4              // Data pin connected to GPIO2 (D4 on NodeMCU)
#define DHTTYPE   DHT11           // Sensor type: DHT11 (or DHT22)

const char* ssid       = "Your_WiFi_Name";     // WiFi SSID
const char* password   = "Your_WiFi_Password"; // WiFi password

// --------- Step 2: Define ThingSpeak channel info ---------
unsigned long channelID = 1234567;             // Your 7-digit channel ID
const char* writeAPIKey = "XYZ123ABC456DEF7";  // Write API key from ThingSpeak

// --------- Step 3: Initialize objects ---------
DHT dht(DHTPIN, DHTTYPE);
WiFiClient client;

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(115200);           // Initialize Serial Monitor at 115200 baud
  dht.begin();                    // Initialize DHT sensor
  delay(1000);

  // --------- Step 4: Connect to WiFi ---------
  Serial.print("Connecting to WiFi");
  WiFi.begin(ssid, password);
  while (WiFi.status() != WL_CONNECTED) {
    delay(500);
    Serial.print(".");
  }
  Serial.println("\nWiFi connected!");
  Serial.print("IP Address: ");
  Serial.println(WiFi.localIP());

  // --------- Step 5: Initialize ThingSpeak ---------
  ThingSpeak.begin(client);
}

void loop() {
  // --------- Step 6: Read sensor values ---------
  float humidity    = dht.readHumidity();
  float temperature = dht.readTemperature();  // Default: Celsius
  // float temperatureF = dht.readTemperature(true); // Uncomment for Fahrenheit

  // --------- Step 7: Validate readings (NaN check) ---------
  if (isnan(humidity) || isnan(temperature)) {
    Serial.println("ERROR: Failed to read from DHT sensor!");
    delay(2000);
    return;   // Skip this loop iteration
  }

  // --------- Step 8: Print to Serial Monitor (for debugging) ---------
  Serial.print("Temperature: ");
  Serial.print(temperature);
  Serial.print(" °C  |  Humidity: ");
  Serial.print(humidity);
  Serial.println(" %");

  // --------- Step 9: Map values to ThingSpeak fields ---------
  ThingSpeak.setField(1, temperature);  // Field 1 = Temperature
  ThingSpeak.setField(2, humidity);     // Field 2 = Humidity

  // --------- Step 10: Write to ThingSpeak cloud ---------
  int httpCode = ThingSpeak.writeFields(channelID, writeAPIKey);

  if (httpCode == 200) {
    Serial.println("Data uploaded successfully. HTTP Code: " + String(httpCode));
  } else {
    Serial.println("Upload failed. HTTP Code: " + String(httpCode));
  }

  // --------- Step 11: Mandatory 20-second delay (>=15s) ---------
  delay(20000);
}
```

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Algorithmic Trace

| Iteration | Action | Time | Expected Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `setup()` runs once | 0–3 s | "WiFi connected" + IP printed |
| 2 | `loop()` round 1 | 3 s | Serial prints T, H; HTTP 200 |
| 3 | `loop()` round 2 | 23 s | New T, H; HTTP 200 |
| 4 | `loop()` round N | 20·(N-1)+3 s | Continuous updates |
| **Error** | `readHumidity()` returns NaN | — | "ERROR: Failed to read..." |
| **Error** | WiFi disconnects | — | Loop blocks in `while()` |

## 3.3 Complete Raspberry Pi Code (Python)

```python
"""
Experiment 2: DHT11 to ThingSpeak Cloud - Raspberry Pi Version
Libraries: Adafruit_DHT, requests, time
"""

import time
import requests
import Adafruit_DHT

# -------- Configuration --------
DHT_SENSOR = Adafruit_DHT.DHT11       # Change to DHT22 if applicable
DHT_PIN    = 4                         # GPIO4 (Pin 7) on Pi header
API_KEY    = "YOUR_WRITE_API_KEY"
URL        = "https://api.thingspeak.com/update"

# -------- Main Loop --------
while True:
    humidity, temperature = Adafruit_DHT.read(DHT_SENSOR, DHT_PIN)
    
    if humidity is not None and temperature is not None:
        payload = {
            'api_key': API_KEY,
            'field1': temperature,
            'field2': humidity
        }
        try:
            r = requests.post(URL, params=payload, timeout=10)
            print(f"T={temperature:.1f}°C  RH={humidity:.1f}%  Status={r.status_code}")
        except requests.exceptions.RequestException as e:
            print(f"Network error: {e}")
    else:
        print("Sensor read failure. Check wiring.")
    
    time.sleep(20)
```

## 3.4 Derived Data Flow Analysis (Mathematical Trace)

Let us **derive the HTTP POST URL structure** for the ThingSpeak API:

The standard endpoint for writing is:

$$
URL_{base} = \text{api.thingspeak.com/update}
$$

The query string is constructed as:

$$
Q = \text{api\_key=}\langle K_w \rangle \ \text{&} \ \text{field1=}\langle V_1 \rangle \ \text{&} \ \text{field2=}\langle V_2 \rangle
$$

The final URL becomes:

$$
URL = \text{https://}\text{api.thingspeak.com/update} \ ? \ Q
$$

**Substituting example values** $K_w = \text{ABC123}$, $V_1 = 28.5$, $V_2 = 65.0$:

$$
URL = \text{https://api.thingspeak.com/update?api\_key=ABC123&field1=28.5&field2=65.0}
$$

**Server response analysis:**

On a successful write, ThingSpeak returns the **entry number**:

$$
R = \mathbb{Z}^+ \quad \text{(positive integer, increments per write)}
$$

On failure or rate-limit hit, it returns:

$$
R = 0
$$

## 3.5 JSON Structure for Data Retrieval (Read API)

When you GET a channel's last 10 entries, the response is:

```json
{
  "channel": {
    "id": 1234567,
    "name": "MyLab",
    "field1": "Temperature",
    "field2": "Humidity"
  },
  "feeds": [
    {"created_at": "2024-09-15T10:00:00Z", "field1": "28.5", "field2": "65.0"},
    {"created_at": "2024-09-15T10:00:20Z", "field1": "28.6", "field2": "64.8"}
  ]
}
```

**Parsing logic for retrieval code:**

$$
\text{For each feed } f \in \text{feeds}: \quad T = \text{float}(f.\text{field1}), \quad RH = \text{float}(f.\text{field2})
$$

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix (For 14-Mark Theory Questions)

| Feature | Arduino + ESP-01 | NodeMCU (ESP8266) | Raspberry Pi 4 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cost (INR)** | ~700 | ~500 | ~5000+ |
| **WiFi Built-in** | ❌ (needs ESP-01) | ✅ | ✅ |
| **OS Required** | No | No | Linux (Raspbian) |
| **Programming Language** | C++ (Arduino) | C++ (Arduino) | Python / C |
| **Code Complexity** | High | Low | Low |
| **Power Consumption** | ~200 mA | ~70 mA | ~600 mA |
| **Boot Time** | 2 s | 2 s | 30–60 s |
| **Best For KTU Lab** | ⚠️ Outdated | ✅ **Recommended** | ✅ For advanced students |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🖼️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Architecture Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Environment] --> B[DHT11 Sensor Module]
    B --> C[NodeMCU Microcontroller]
    C --> D{WiFi Connected?}
    D -- No --> E[Retry Connection Loop]
    E --> D
```
