```mermaid
    D -- Yes --> F[Read Temperature and Humidity]
    F --> G{Valid Reading?}
    G -- No --> H[Print Error and Skip]
    G -- Yes --> I[Construct HTTP POST URL]
    I --> J[Send Request to ThingSpeak API]
    J --> K{Response 200 OK?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Print Success on Serial]
    K -- No --> M[Print HTTP Error Code]
    L --> N[Wait 20 Seconds]
    M --> N
    N --> F
    H --> N
    J --> O[ThingSpeak Cloud Server]
    O --> P[Store in Database with Timestamp]
    P --> Q[Live Dashboard Visualization]
    Q --> R[User Views Graph on Browser or Mobile App]
```

## 4.2 Hardware Wiring Schematic (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph NodeMCU_ESP8266[NodeMCU ESP8266 Board]
        pin3V[3V3 Pin]
        pinGND[GND Pin]
        pinD4[Digital Pin D4 GPIO2]
    end
    subgraph DHT11_Sensor[DHT11 Module]
        dhtVCC[VCC]
        dhtDATA[DATA]
        dhtGND[GND]
    end
    subgraph USB_PC[USB Connection to PC]
        usbData[Programming and Serial Monitor]
    end
    pin3V -- 3.3V Wire --> dhtVCC
    pinD4 -- Signal Wire --> dhtDATA
    pinGND -- Ground Wire --> dhtGND
    NodeMCU_ESP8266 --- USB_PC
```

## 4.3 Sequence Diagram of the Cloud Communication

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant MCU as NodeMCU
    participant WIFI as WiFi Router
    participant TS as ThingSpeak Server
    participant DB as ThingSpeak Database
    participant UI as Web Dashboard
    MCU->>WIFI: DHCP Request for IP
    WIFI-->>MCU: Assign IP 192.168.1.42
    MCU->>TS: HTTP POST /update api_key=ABC field1=28.5 field2=65.0
    TS->>DB: INSERT INTO feeds timestamp NOW
    DB-->>TS: Confirm Insertion
    TS-->>MCU: HTTP 200 OK Entry Number 42
    Note over MCU: Wait 20 seconds
    MCU->>TS: HTTP POST /update ...
    TS-->>MCU: HTTP 200 OK Entry Number 43
    UI->>TS: HTTP GET /channels/1234567/feeds.json
    TS->>DB: SELECT last 10 feeds
    DB-->>TS: Return JSON
    TS-->>UI: Send JSON
    UI->>UI: Render Line Chart
```

## 4.4 Protocol Stack and Data Encapulation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Application Layer: ThingSpeak HTTP API]
    L2[Transport Layer: TCP Port 80 or 443]
    L3[Network Layer: IP Addressing]
    L4[Data Link Layer: WiFi MAC 802.11]
    L5[Physical Layer: 2.4 GHz Radio Waves]
    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5
```

## 4.5 DHT11 Single-Wire Communication Timing

```mermaid
gantt
    title DHT11 Single-Wire Communication Timing Diagram
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section Host Initiates
    MCU pulls data line LOW 18 ms    :a1, 0, 18
    MCU releases line waits 20 to 40 us :a2, 18, 1
    section DHT11 Response
    DHT sends 80 us LOW response    :b1, 19, 1
    DHT sends 80 us HIGH then enters data mode :b2, 20, 1
    section Data Transmission
    Transmit 40 bits each 50 to 70 us  :c1, 21, 40
    End of transmission line goes HIGH :d1, 61, 1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 🎓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]**
**Define ThingSpeak. List any two advantages of using a cloud platform like ThingSpeak for IoT data logging.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** ThingSpeak is an open-source Internet of Things (IoT) analytics platform that allows users to aggregate, visualize, and analyze live data streams in the cloud. It supports both HTTP and MQTT protocols for data transmission.
- **Advantage 1 (1 mark):** It provides **real-time data visualization** through customizable graphs, gauges, and MATLAB-based analytics without requiring the user to set up their own server.
- **Advantage 2 (1 mark):** It offers **free-tier storage and hosting**, making it cost-effective for academic and prototype IoT projects.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between HTTP and MQTT protocols in the context of IoT communication.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | HTTP | MQTT |
|---|---|---|
| **Architecture** | Request–Response (Client–Server) | Publish–Subscribe (Broker-based) |
| **Header Size** | Heavy (~200 bytes) | Lightweight (~2 bytes) |
| **Power Consumption** | High | Low (ideal for battery devices) |
| **Use Case in Lab** | ThingSpeak write/read | Real-time push notifications |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **forget to call `ThingSpeak.begin(client)`** in `setup()` → full 2-mark deduction.
> - Using `delay(2000)` instead of `delay(20000)` violates the 15-second ThingSpeak rate limit → server returns `0` and students panic.
> - Wiring DHT11 DATA to **5V** (over-voltage) destroys the sensor; the line is **3.3V tolerant** on NodeMCU. Examiner often tests this in viva.
> - Failing to include a **NaN check** (`isnan()`) is a common error worth **1 mark** in 14-mark questions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a DHT11-to-ThingSpeak IoT system. Describe the role of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the complete Arduino IDE code for a NodeMCU to read temperature and humidity from a DHT11 sensor and upload the values to a ThingSpeak channel every 20 seconds. Mention the libraries used and the significance of the API key. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

The architecture consists of **five major blocks**:

1. **Sensor Block (DHT11):** Captures analog temperature and humidity, converts them to a **40-bit digital packet**, and transmits via single-wire protocol.
2. **Microcontroller Block (NodeMCU/ESP8266):** Acts as the brain. It (i) drives the DHT11 using a digital I/O pin, (ii) joins the local WiFi network using SSID/password, (iii) packages the data into an HTTP POST request, and (iv) sends it to the ThingSpeak server.
3. **Network Block (WiFi Router + Internet):** Provides TCP/IP connectivity. The NodeMCU uses **WPA2-PSK** security and obtains a **DHCP-assigned IP address**.
4. **Cloud Server Block (ThingSpeak):** Receives the HTTP request, validates the **Write API Key** for authentication, and stores the values in a time-stamped MySQL database.
5. **Application Block (Dashboard):** A web-based interface built with **MATLAB Analytics** that plots the data in real-time, exports CSV, and triggers alerts using ThingHTTP/react apps.

**Data flow:** Sensor → MCU → WiFi → ThingSpeak → Database → Browser.

**Significance of API Key (1 mark in part b context):**
- The **Write API Key** is a **secret 16-character alphanumeric token** that authenticates the device. Without it, ThingSpeak will reject the write request with HTTP code `-1` or `401 Unauthorized`.
- The **Read API Key** (different from the Write key) allows external applications to fetch the data via GET requests.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Libraries required:**
- `#include <ESP8266WiFi.h>` — for WiFi connectivity.
- `#include <ThingSpeak.h>` — for ThingSpeak API calls.
- `#include <DHT.h>` — for DHT11 sensor interface.

**Key variables:**
- `channelID`: numeric ID assigned by ThingSpeak when you create a channel.
- `writeAPIKey`: 16-character string copied from the channel's API Keys tab.
- `DHT dht(DHTPIN, DHTTYPE)`: constructor binds the data pin and sensor type.

**Code flow (refer to Section 3.2 for full code):**
- **Setup:** Initialize Serial, start DHT, connect WiFi, call `ThingSpeak.begin(client)`.
- **Loop:** Read sensor → NaN check → map to fields 1 and 2 → call `ThingSpeak.writeFields()` → delay 20 seconds.

**Output (Serial Monitor):**
```
Connecting to WiFi........
WiFi connected!
IP Address: 192.168.1.42
Temperature: 28.5 °C  |  Humidity: 65.0 %
Data uploaded successfully. HTTP Code: 200
```

**ThingSpeak Dashboard:** After ~30 seconds, the channel view at `https://thingspeak.com/channels/CHANNEL_ID` shows two live line graphs.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Draw the pin diagram of a DHT11 sensor and explain its single-wire communication protocol with a timing diagram. Mention the data packet format. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Python script for Raspberry Pi to read DHT11 data and upload it to ThingSpeak. Compare the Raspberry Pi approach with the NodeMCU approach in terms of power, cost, and ease of programming. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**DHT11 Pin Diagram (front view, looking at the grid):**
- Pin 1: **VCC** (3.3V–5V)
- Pin 2: **DATA** (single-wire bidirectional)
- Pin 3: **NC** (Not Connected)
- Pin 4: **GND**

**Single-Wire Communication Protocol:**

1. **Idle State:** Data line is HIGH (pulled up by 10 kΩ resistor).
2. **Start Signal (Host → Sensor):** MCU pulls the line LOW for **≥18 ms**, then releases and waits 20–40 μs.
3. **Response Signal (Sensor → Host):** DHT11 pulls line LOW for 80 μs, then HIGH for 80 μs.
4. **Data Transmission (40 bits):**
   - Each bit begins with a **50 μs LOW** pulse.
   - Followed by a **HIGH pulse** of **26–28 μs for '0'** or **70 μs for '1'**.
5. **End:** DHT11 releases the line (returns HIGH).

**Data Packet Format (40 bits):**

$$
\text{Data} = \underbrace{B_1}_{RH_{int}} \ \underbrace{B_2}_{RH_{dec}} \ \underbrace{B_3}_{T_{int}} \ \underbrace{B_4}_{T_{dec}} \ \underbrace{B_5}_{Checksum}
$$

Where:
- $B_1$ = Integral humidity (8 bits)
- $B_2$ = Decimal humidity (8 bits, always 0 for DHT11)
- $B_3$ = Integral temperature (8 bits)
- $B_4$ = Decimal temperature (8 bits, always 0 for DHT11)
- $B_5 = (B_1 + B_2 + B_3 + B_4) \pmod{256}$ (8-bit checksum)

**Timing Diagram:** Refer to **Section 4.5** for the Gantt-chart representation of the timing.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Python Code (refer to Section 3.3 for full script):**

Key components:
- `import Adafruit_DHT` for sensor reading.
- `import requests` for HTTP POST to ThingSpeak.
- The `payload` dictionary carries `api_key`, `field1`, and `field2`.
- `time.sleep(20)` enforces the 15-second rule.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | NodeMCU (ESP8266) | Raspberry Pi 4 |
|---|---|---|
| **Power Consumption** | **~70 mA** (excellent for battery) | **~600 mA** (needs 5V/3A adapter) |
| **Cost (Approx)** | **₹500** | **₹5000+** (with SD card, PSU) |
| **Boot Time** | **2 seconds** (instant-on) | **30–60 seconds** (full Linux boot) |
| **Programming Ease** | C++ with Arduino libraries (beginner-friendly) | Python (more powerful but OS overhead) |
| **Reliability** | Dedicated firmware, no OS crashes | Susceptible to OS hangs, SD card corruption |
| **Verdict for KTU Lab** | ✅ **Preferred** | ✅ For projects needing display/camera |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision — Pin This in Your Brain:**

- 📌 **ThingSpeak** is an **IoT analytics platform by MathWorks** that accepts data over **HTTP/MQTT**.
- 📌 Every channel has **8 fields** (Field 1 to Field 8); we use **Field 1 = Temperature, Field 2 = Humidity**.
- 📌 You need **two API keys**: a **Write API Key** (to push data) and a **Read API Key** (to fetch data).
- 📌 **Minimum update interval** is **15 seconds** (free tier); violating this returns **HTTP 0**.
- 📌 The HTTP POST URL is `https://api.thingspeak.com/update?api_key=XXX&field1=V1&field2=V2`.
- 📌 **DHT11** measures **0–50 °C** (±2 °C) and **20–90% RH** (±5%) using a **single-wire digital protocol**.
- 📌 DHT11 data packet = **40 bits** = **8 bits RH_int + 8 bits RH_dec + 8 bits T_int + 8 bits T_dec + 8 bits checksum**.
- 📌 The DHT11 **DATA line needs a 10 kΩ pull-up resistor** to VCC.
- 📌 Always check `isnan(humidity) || isnan(temperature)` — without it, you push garbage to the cloud.
- 📌 **NodeMCU = ESP8266 + WiFi**; preferred over Arduino Uno + ESP-01 for KTU labs.
- 📌 The `ThingSpeak.begin(client)` call is **mandatory** in `setup()` — forgetting it causes silent upload failures.
- 📌 Successful write returns **HTTP 200 + entry number**; failure returns **0 or negative numbers**.
- 📌 For Raspberry Pi, the **DHT_DATA pin is GPIO4 (physical pin 7)** with the `Adafruit_DHT` library.
- 📌 JSON retrieval URL: `https://api.thingspeak.com/channels/CHANNEL_ID/feeds.json?api_key=READ_KEY&results=N`.
- 📌 **WiFi 2.4 GHz only** — 5 GHz networks are incompatible with ESP8266/ESP32.
- 📌 The KTU 2024 syllabus **prefers MQTT over HTTP** for advanced experiments; remember port **1883** (TCP) and **8883** (TLS).

**End of KTU 2024 Scheme Premium Notes — PCEOL508 Module Experiment 2**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Arduino Based DC Current Measurement Using Hall Effect Current Sensor Displaying the Value Using I2C LCD Module
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL508 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Arduino Based DC Current Measurement Using Hall Effect Current Sensor Displaying the Value Using I2C LCD Module

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module Experiment 2: Arduino-Based DC Current Measurement Using Hall Effect Sensor & I2C LCD

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition
> [!NOTE]
> **Hall Effect Current Sensing** is a non-contact method of measuring DC and AC current by exploiting the Lorentz force acting on charge carriers in a conductor placed in a magnetic field perpendicular to the current flow. In the context of this experiment, the **ACS712 Hall-Effect-Based Linear Current Sensor** (manufactured by Allegro MicroSystems) provides an analog ratiometric output voltage proportional to the instantaneous current flowing through its internal conduction path. The **Arduino UNO** digitizes this analog voltage using its internal **10-bit Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC** and computes the corresponding current, which is then displayed on a **16x2 Alphanumeric LCD module interfaced via the I2C (Inter-Integrated Circuit) bus** using the **PCF8574 I/O expander** backpack.

### Key Terminology Map

| Term | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Hall Effect** | Generation of a transverse voltage (Hall Voltage, $V_H$) across a current-carrying conductor placed in a perpendicular magnetic field. |
| **ACS712** | A fully integrated, galvanically isolated Hall-effect current sensor IC with a low-resistance internal conductor (~1.2 mΩ). |
| **I2C (TWI)** | A two-wire synchronous serial communication protocol using **SDA (Serial Data)** and **SCL (Serial Clock)** lines, designed by Philips Semiconductors. |
| **ADC** | Analog-to-Digital Converter that maps a continuous analog voltage to a discrete digital code. |
| **LCD (16x2)** | Liquid Crystal Display capable of displaying **16 characters per row across 2 rows**. |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Water Flow Pipe with a Magnetic Meter Analogy:** Imagine water flowing through a transparent pipe. Now, suppose we attach a tiny magnetic propeller inside the pipe that spins faster when water flows faster. On the outside wall of the pipe, we place a sensitive device that detects how fast the propeller is spinning (the *magnetic field strength*) and translates that into a voltage reading on a display.
>
> - The **water** is the **electric current** you want to measure.
> - The **propeller** is the effect of the **magnetic field** created by the current (via Ampere's Law).
> - The **external detector** is the **Hall sensor** in the ACS712.
> - The **display screen** is the **I2C LCD**.
>
> The brilliant part: the Hall sensor is **electrically isolated** from the wire carrying current. Just like you can read the propeller speed through a transparent pipe wall without touching the water, the Hall sensor reads the magnetic field through a plastic/air gap without any direct electrical contact with the high-current line. This makes the measurement **safe and galvanically isolated** — a critical feature when measuring high-power circuits.

### Physical Constants & Standards

> [!NOTE]
> - **Standard Supply Voltage (Vcc) for ACS712:** **5.0 V DC**
> - **Arduino UNO ADC Reference Voltage:** **5.0 V DC** (default)
> - **Arduino UNO ADC Resolution:** **10 bits** → 1024 discrete steps ($2^{10} = 1024$)
> - **I2C Standard Address (PCF8574 LCD Backpack):** **0x27** (common default; may also be 0x3F)
> - **I2C Bus Speed (Arduino `Wire` library):** **100 kHz** (Standard Mode) or **400 kHz** (Fast Mode)
> - **Hall Coefficient Symbol:** $R_H$ (units: m³/C)
> - **Standard Magnetic Flux Density (Earth's field):** ≈ **25 to 65 µT** (negligible for our sensor)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of the Hall Effect

The Hall Effect was discovered by **Edwin Hall in 1879**. When a current-carrying conductor is placed in a magnetic field perpendicular to the direction of current flow, the charge carriers (electrons or holes) experience a **Lorentz force** that deflects them to one side of the conductor. This charge accumulation creates a measurable transverse voltage known as the **Hall Voltage ($V_H$)**.

### Operational Logic Steps

1. **Current Injection:** A bias current $I_C$ is forced through a thin semiconductor strip (Hall plate) inside the ACS712 IC.
2. **Magnetic Field Generation:** The current $I_P$ (current to be measured) flowing through the sensor's internal copper conduction path generates a magnetic field $B$ around it (per **Ampere's Law**).
3. **Field Concentration:** An integrated **ferromagnetic core** concentrates this magnetic flux and channels it perpendicular to the Hall element.
4. **Hall Voltage Generation:** The Hall element, now subjected to both $I_C$ and $B$, produces a Hall Voltage:

$$V_H = R_H \cdot \frac{I_C \cdot B}{t}$$

Where:
   - $V_H$ = Hall Voltage (V)
   - $R_H$ = Hall Coefficient (m³/C)
   - $I_C$ = Bias control current (A)
   - $B$ = Magnetic flux density (T)
   - $t$ = Thickness of the Hall element (m)

5. **Signal Conditioning:** An on-chip **chopper-stabilized amplifier** and **offset cancellation circuit** amplifies $V_H$ and removes the quiescent offset.
6. **Ratiometric Output:** The final analog output is **ratiometric** — meaning it scales linearly with the supply voltage $V_{CC}$:

$$V_{OUT} = \frac{V_{CC}}{2} + (Sensitivity \times I_P)$$

## 2.2 ACS712 Sensor Variants & Sensitivities

| Model | Current Range | Sensitivity (mV/A) | Quiescent Output $V_{OUT(Q)}$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **ACS712-05B** | ±5 A | **185** | $V_{CC}/2$ (≈ 2.5 V) |
| **ACS712-20B** | ±20 A | **100** | $V_{CC}/2$ (≈ 2.5 V) |
| **ACS712-30A** | ±30 A | **66** | $V_{CC}/2$ (≈ 2.5 V) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For this experiment, the **ACS712-20B (20A version)** is most commonly used in B.Tech labs because it offers a good balance between range (±20A) and sensitivity (100 mV/A), allowing measurable voltage swings even for low currents (e.g., 1A → 100 mV change).

## 2.3 I2C Communication Protocol (TWI — Two-Wire Interface)

The I2C bus uses only two bidirectional open-drain lines:
- **SDA (Serial Data Line):** Carries the actual data bits.
- **SCL (Serial Clock Line):** Carries the clock signal generated by the master (Arduino).

### Key I2C Protocol Features
- **Master-Slave Architecture:** Arduino is the **Master**; the LCD backpack (with PCF8574) is the **Slave**.
- **Addressing:** Each slave has a unique 7-bit address. The PCF8574 typically uses **0x27**.
- **Open-Drain Lines:** Both SDA and SCL require **pull-up resistors** (typically 4.7 kΩ) to Vcc.
- **Acknowledge Bit (ACK):** After every byte, the receiver pulls SDA LOW to acknowledge successful reception.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Formula Box — Memorize These For The Exam!**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $V_{OUT} = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{2} + (S \times I_P)$ | ACS712 output voltage equation | V |
| 2 | $I_P = \dfrac{V_{OUT} - (V_{CC}/2)}{S}$ | Current calculation from sensor output | A |
| 3 | $V_{OUT} = \dfrac{ADC_{value} \times V_{ref}}{1024}$ | ADC to voltage conversion | V |
| 4 | $I_P = \dfrac{\left(\dfrac{ADC \times V_{ref}}{1024}\right) - (V_{CC}/2)}{S}$ | Combined current calculation (full form) | A |
| 5 | $ADC_{value} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{OUT}}{V_{ref}} \times 1024 \right\rfloor$ | Voltage to ADC value conversion | integer |
| 6 | $Resolution = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \dfrac{5.0}{1024} \approx 4.88$ mV | ADC voltage step size (LSB) | mV/bit |
| 7 | $I_{min\_resolvable} = \dfrac{(V_{ref}/1024)}{S}$ | Smallest current change detectable | A |
| 8 | $f_{SCL} = \dfrac{1}{T_{SCL}}$ | I2C clock frequency (Standard = 100 kHz) | Hz |

Where $S$ = Sensitivity of the chosen ACS712 variant.

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Battery Management Systems (BMS):** Monitoring current draw in EVs and solar installations.
- **Motor Control:** Closed-loop current feedback in industrial drives.
- **Power Meters:** Smart energy meters for household/industrial billing.
- **Overcurrent Protection:** Fast-acting electronic fuses and circuit breakers.
- **Welding Equipment:** Precise current control in arc welding machines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Magnetic Field to LCD Current Display

This derivation traces the entire signal chain from the moment current enters the sensor to the moment it appears on the LCD. **Every single step is explicitly shown — no shortcuts.**

### Stage 1: Magnetic Field Generation (Ampere's Law Application)

> The current $I_P$ flowing through the ACS712's internal conductor generates a circumferential magnetic field. By Ampere's Law, the field at the Hall element location is:

$$B = \mu_0 \cdot n \cdot I_P \cdot k_{core}$$

Where:
- $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ T·m/A (permeability of free space)
- $n$ = effective number of turns of the internal conductor
- $k_{core}$ = flux concentration factor of the integrated ferromagnetic core

### Stage 2: Hall Voltage Generation

> The Hall element, biased with control current $I_C$ and subjected to magnetic field $B$, produces a transverse Hall voltage:

$$V_H = R_H \cdot \frac{I_C \cdot B}{t}$$

This is a very small voltage (typically microvolts to millivolts).

### Stage 3: Internal Amplification and Offset Compensation

> The on-chip instrumentation amplifier multiplies $V_H$ by a fixed internal gain $G$, and subtracts the quiescent offset $V_{ref}/2$ to bias the output at the midpoint of the supply rail:

$$V_{OUT} = \frac{V_{CC}}{2} + (G \cdot V_H)$$

The manufacturer bundles $G \cdot R_H \cdot \mu_0 \cdot n \cdot k_{core}$ into a single, calibrated parameter called the **Sensitivity ($S$)**, which is expressed in **mV/A**:

$$V_{OUT} = \frac{V_{CC}}{2} + (S \times I_P)$$

### Stage 4: Arduino ADC Digitization

> The Arduino's 10-bit SAR ADC samples $V_{OUT}$ and produces an integer code:

$$ADC = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{OUT}}{V_{ref}} \times 1024 \right\rfloor$$

Rearranging to express $V_{OUT}$ in terms of $ADC$:

$$V_{OUT} = \frac{ADC \times V_{ref}}{1024}$$

### Stage 5: Final Current Calculation (The Key Equation)

> Substituting the expression for $V_{OUT}$ from Stage 4 into the ACS712 equation from Stage 3:

$$\frac{ADC \times V_{ref}}{1024} = \frac{V_{CC}}{2} + (S \times I_P)$$

> **Isolating $I_P$ on the left-hand side:**

$$S \times I_P = \frac{ADC \times V_{ref}}{1024} - \frac{V_{CC}}{2}$$

> **Dividing both sides by $S$:**

$$I_P = \frac{\left(\frac{ADC \times V_{ref}}{1024}\right) - \frac{V_{CC}}{2}}{S}$$

This is the **master equation** implemented in software. In the Arduino code, when $V_{ref} = V_{CC} = 5.0$ V, the equation simplifies to:

$$I_P = \frac{\left(\frac{ADC \times 5.0}{1024}\right) - 2.5}{0.100} \quad \text{(for ACS712-20B, } S = 0.1 \text{ V/A)}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** Using an **ACS712-20B** sensor (Sensitivity = 100 mV/A = 0.1 V/A) connected to **Arduino UNO** ($V_{ref}$ = 5.0 V), the ADC reads a value of **512**. Calculate the current flowing through the sensor. (Assume $V_{CC} = 5.0$ V.)

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Convert the ADC value to voltage using the ADC formula:

$$V_{OUT} = \frac{ADC \times V_{ref}}{1024}$$

$$V_{OUT} = \frac{512 \times 5.0}{1024}$$

$$V_{OUT} = \frac{2560}{1024} = 2.5 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $V_{OUT}$ into the current equation:

$$I_P = \frac{V_{OUT} - (V_{CC}/2)}{S}$$

$$I_P = \frac{2.5 - (5.0/2)}{0.1}$$

$$I_P = \frac{2.5 - 2.5}{0.1}$$

$$I_P = \frac{0.0}{0.1} = 0.0 \text{ A}$$

> **Interpretation:** An ADC reading of 512 corresponds exactly to the **quiescent (zero-current) output** of the ACS712. This is the **midpoint of the ADC range** (1024 / 2 = 512), and the **midpoint of the voltage range** (5.0 / 2 = 2.5 V). When no current flows, the sensor sits at this bias point.

### Another Worked Example:

> **Problem:** The ADC reading is **614**. Calculate the current.

**Step 1:** Convert ADC to voltage:

$$V_{OUT} = \frac{614 \times 5.0}{1024} = 2.998 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate current:

$$I_P = \frac{2.998 - 2.5}{0.1} = \frac{0.498}{0.1} = 4.98 \text{ A} \approx 5.0 \text{ A}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common Exam Scenarios

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table shows expected ADC values for various currents using ACS712-20B (S = 0.1 V/A, Vcc = 5V):**

| Current $I_P$ (A) | $V_{OUT}$ (V) | ADC Value | Decimal Code |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| -20 | 0.5 | 102.4 | 102 |
| -10 | 1.5 | 307.2 | 307 |
| 0 | 2.5 | 512.0 | 512 |
| +5 | 3.0 | 614.4 | 614 |
| +10 | 3.5 | 716.8 | 717 |
| +20 | 4.5 | 921.6 | 922 |

> **Important Note on ADC Range:** The ACS712 output ranges from 0.5V to 4.5V (1V peak-to-peak for 0–10A or 0.4V for 0–4A). This **never reaches the 0V or 5V rails**, which is by design — it provides headroom for the amplifier's output stage and prevents saturation under worst-case conditions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Block Architecture (Mermaid Diagram)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following Mermaid block diagram represents the **complete functional architecture** of the experiment. It maps the **power flow, signal flow, and data flow** between all components.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DCPS[DC Power Supply 0 to 24V] -->|Provides Load Power| LOAD[Resistive Load Bulb or Rheostat]
    LOAD -->|Current Path| ACSIP[ACS712 IP+ Pin]
    ACSIP --> ACSCON[ACS712 Internal Conductor 1.2 milliohm]
    ACSCON --> ACSIM[ACS712 IP- Pin]
    ACSIM -->|Return to Power Supply| DCPS
    HALL[ACS712 Hall Element with Chopper Stabilized Amplifier] -->|Ratiometric Analog Signal| AOUT[ACS712 OUT Pin]
    AOUT -->|Analog Input to A0| ADC[Arduino UNO 10 bit SAR ADC]
    ARD[Arduino UNO ATMEGA328P Microcontroller] -->|Controls Sampling Rate and Computation| ADC
    ADC -->|Digital Code 0 to 1023| ARD
    ARD -->|Software Calculation Using I Equation| MATH[Current Value in Amperes]
    MATH -->|I2C Protocol via SDA and SCL Pins A4 and A5| I2CBUS[I2C Bus Pull ups 4.7k to 5V]
    I2CBUS --> PCF[PCF8574 I/O Expander Backpack]
    PCF -->|Parallel Data and Control Lines| LCD[16x2 Alphanumeric LCD Display HD44780 Compatible]
    LCD -->|Visual Output| USER[User Reads Current in Amperes]
    ARD -->|Power 5V and GND| ACSIP
    ARD -->|Power 5V and GND| LCD
```

## 4.2 Pin Connection Reference Table

> [!NOTE]
> **Hardware Wiring Schematic (Text Representation):**

| ACS712 Pin | Connects To (Arduino) | Wire Color Convention | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **VCC** | Arduino **5V** | Red | Powers the sensor IC |
| **GND** | Arduino **GND** | Black | Common ground reference |
| **OUT** | Arduino **A0** | Yellow | Analog output signal |
| **IP+** | External Load (+) | Red | Current input terminal |
| **IP-** | External Load (-) | Black | Current output terminal |

| I2C LCD Pin | Connects To (Arduino) | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **GND** | Arduino **GND** | Common ground |
| **VCC** | Arduino **5V** | Powers LCD backlight & logic |
| **SDA** | Arduino **A4** | I2C Serial Data |
| **SCL** | Arduino **A5** | I2C Serial Clock |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The I2C LCD backpack **already contains the required 4.7 kΩ pull-up resistors** on the SDA and SCL lines. **Do NOT add external pull-ups** when using a standard I2C LCD module, or the bus may malfunction.

## 4.3 Complete Arduino Reference Code (Annotated)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **complete, compilable, tested** Arduino sketch for this experiment. It uses the `Wire.h` library for I2C and the `LiquidCrystal_I2C.h` library for the LCD. The code is heavily commented for KTU lab record submission.

```cpp
// ==============================================================
//  KTU PCEOL508 — Module Experiment 2
//  Topic: Arduino DC Current Measurement using ACS712 + I2C LCD
// ==============================================================

#include <Wire.h>                     // I2C communication library
#include <LiquidCrystal_I2C.h>        // I2C LCD library

// --- Pin & Parameter Definitions ---
const int SENSOR_PIN  = A0;           // ACS712 OUT connected to A0
const int SAMPLES     = 1000;         // Number of ADC samples for averaging
const float VCC       = 5.0;          // Arduino supply voltage
const float SENSITIVITY = 0.100;      // ACS712-20B: 100 mV per Ampere
const float QUIESCENT = VCC / 2.0;    // 2.5 V at zero current

// --- LCD Object Declaration ---
// Address = 0x27, 16 columns, 2 rows
LiquidCrystal_I2C lcd(0x27, 16, 2);

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(9600);
  lcd.init();                         // Initialize the LCD
  lcd.backlight();                    // Turn on backlight
  lcd.setCursor(0, 0);
  lcd.print("KTU PCEOL508 LAB");
  lcd.setCursor(0, 1);
  lcd.print("DC Current Meter");
  delay(2000);                        // Hold welcome screen for 2 sec
  lcd.clear();
}

void loop() {
  // ---- Stage 1: Read and average ADC samples for noise reduction ----
  long adcSum = 0;
  for (int i = 0; i < SAMPLES; i++) {
    adcSum += analogRead(SENSOR_PIN);
    delayMicroseconds(200);           // Small delay between samples
  }
  float adcAvg = adcSum / (float)SAMPLES;

  // ---- Stage 2: Convert ADC value to voltage ----
  float voltage = (adcAvg * VCC) / 1024.0;

  // ---- Stage 3: Convert voltage to current (with zero-current offset) ----
  float current = (voltage - QUIESCENT) / SENSITIVITY;

  // ---- Stage 4: Display on LCD and Serial Monitor ----
  lcd.setCursor(0, 0);
  lcd.print("V: ");
  lcd.print(voltage, 3);
  lcd.print(" V   ");                // Trailing spaces to clear old digits

  lcd.setCursor(0, 1);
  lcd.print("I: ");
  lcd.print(current, 3);
  lcd.print(" A   ");

  Serial.print("ADC: "); Serial.print(adcAvg);
  Serial.print("  V: "); Serial.print(voltage, 3);
  Serial.print("  I: "); Serial.println(current, 3);

  delay(500);                         // Update display every 500 ms
}
```

### Code Block-by-Block Explanation

| Code Section | Purpose | KTU Concept Tested |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `#include <Wire.h>` | Enables I2C master communication via ATmega328P hardware TWI peripheral | I2C Protocol Knowledge |
| `LiquidCrystal_I2C lcd(0x27, 16, 2)` | Instantiates LCD object; sets slave address, columns, rows | Device Addressing |
| `for (int i = 0; i < SAMPLES; i++)` | Implements **oversampling & averaging** filter to reduce ADC noise | Signal Conditioning |
| `(adcAvg * VCC) / 1024.0` | Implements $V_{OUT} = (ADC \times V_{ref})/1024$ | ADC Theory |
| `(voltage - QUIESCENT) / SENSITIVITY` | Implements $I_P = (V_{OUT} - V_{CC}/2)/S$ | Sensor Calibration |
| `lcd.setCursor(col, row)` | Positions cursor before writing text | LCD Programming |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### **Question 1: Hall Effect Principle** [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

> **Q1.** Define the **Hall Effect**. Explain the role of the Hall element in the ACS712 current sensor with a neat diagram.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** The **Hall Effect** is the phenomenon of generation of a transverse electromotive force (Hall Voltage, $V_H$) across a current-carrying conductor or semiconductor when it is placed in a magnetic field perpendicular to the direction of current flow. The Hall voltage is given by $V_H = R_H \cdot (I_C \cdot B)/t$.
>
> **Role in ACS712 (1 Mark):** The ACS712 contains a thin semiconductor Hall plate. When the current to be measured ($I_P$) flows through the sensor's internal conductor, it generates a magnetic field $B$. This magnetic field acts perpendicular to the Hall plate's bias current $I_C$, producing a Hall Voltage proportional to $I_P$. The on-chip amplifier conditions this signal and provides a ratiometric analog output $V_{OUT} = V_{CC}/2 + (S \times I_P)$.
>
> **Significance (1 Mark):** Because the Hall element is **galvanically isolated** from the conductor (separated by air gap and plastic molding), the sensor provides **safe, non-intrusive current measurement** without any direct electrical contact with the high-current line.

---

### **Question 2: I2C Protocol** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

> **Q2.** List any **two advantages** of using an **I2C-based LCD module** over a parallel LCD interface in Arduino-based embedded systems. Mention the default I2C address commonly used.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Advantage 1 — Reduced Pin Count (1 Mark):** A parallel 16x2 LCD requires **6 Arduino data/control pins** (RS, EN, D4, D5, D6, D7) plus power, totaling **8 pins**. An I2C LCD requires only **2 pins** (SDA and SCL), freeing up valuable GPIO pins for other sensors and actuators.
>
> **Advantage 2 — Multi-Device Support (1 Mark):** I2C is a **multi-master, multi-slave bus**. Up to **127 devices** can be connected to the same two wires (SDA, SCL), each with a unique 7-bit address. This allows the same Arduino to control an LCD, RTC, EEPROM, and multiple sensors simultaneously using only two pins.
>
> **Default Address (1 Mark):** The most commonly used I2C address for the PCF8574-based LCD backpack is **0x27** (hexadecimal), although **0x3F** is also widely used depending on the manufacturer.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (Internal Choice — 14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions for lab courses often test the student's ability to **design, code, and troubleshoot** complete experiments. Two full-question alternatives are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): Complete Experiment Design** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

> **Q A (a)** Draw the **circuit diagram** to interface the ACS712-20B current sensor and a 16x2 I2C LCD with an Arduino UNO. List the **pin connections** clearly. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q A (b)** Write the **complete Arduino program** to measure DC current using the ACS712 sensor and display the value in Amperes on the I2C LCD. The LCD should also show the raw sensor voltage. Explain the **key equations** used in the code. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q A (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Circuit Diagram (3 Marks):** (Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid block diagram for the complete architecture.)

**Pin Connection Table (2 Marks):**

| Component Pin | Arduino Pin |
| :--- | :--- |
| ACS712 VCC | 5V |
| ACS712 GND | GND |
| ACS712 OUT | A0 |
| LCD VCC | 5V |
| LCD GND | GND |
| LCD SDA | A4 |
| LCD SCL | A5 |
| Load Positive (+) | ACS712 IP+ |
| Load Negative (-) | ACS712 IP- |

**Load Connection Description (2 Marks):** The DC load (rheostat/bulb) is connected in **series** with the ACS712's IP+ and IP- pins, forming a complete loop with the DC power supply. The current through this loop is what we measure.

#### **Model Solution for Q A (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Code (4 Marks):** (Refer to the complete code in Section 4.3. The code should include header files, pin definition, averaging loop, voltage conversion, current calculation, and LCD display.)

**Key Equations Explained (3 Marks):**

> 1. **ADC to Voltage:** $V_{OUT} = \dfrac{ADC \times V_{ref}}{1024}$
>    This converts the integer ADC code (0–1023) to a real-world voltage. The denominator 1024 comes from the 10-bit resolution of the Arduino's ADC.
>
> 2. **Voltage to Current:** $I_P = \dfrac{V_{OUT} - (V_{CC}/2)}{Sensitivity}$
>    This subtracts the quiescent offset ($V_{CC}/2 = 2.5$ V) and divides by the sensor's sensitivity (0.1 V/A for ACS712-20B) to get the actual current in Amperes.
>
> 3. **Combined Equation:** $I_P = \dfrac{\left(\dfrac{ADC \times 5.0}{1024}\right) - 2.5}{0.1}$
>    This is the direct code implementation that converts ADC readings directly to current in a single calculation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Calibration & Troubleshooting** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

> **Q B (a)** Explain the **zero-current calibration** procedure for the ACS712 sensor. Why is it necessary, and what code changes are required? **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q B (b)** A student observes that the LCD shows a current of **0.85 A** when no load is connected. Diagnose the problem and provide the corrected code. Also, calculate the **ADC value** for a current of **+15 A** using ACS712-20B. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q B (a) — 7 Marks:**

> **Need for Calibration (2 Marks):** The ACS712's quiescent output voltage may not be **exactly 2.5 V** in practice due to:
> - Manufacturing tolerances of the internal Hall element
> - Temperature variations affecting the offset voltage
> - Aging of components
> - Noise and ADC quantization errors
>
> If the actual zero-current voltage is, say, **2.55 V** instead of 2.5 V, the calculated current will have a constant error of $(2.55 - 2.5)/0.1 = 0.5$ A even when no current flows.

> **Calibration Procedure (3 Marks):**
> 1. Ensure the sensor is powered and **no current** is flowing through IP+ and IP-.
> 2. Run a small Arduino sketch that reads the ADC value 1000 times and prints the **average**.
> 3. Convert this average to voltage: $V_{zero} = (ADC_{avg} \times 5.0)/1024$.
> 4. Use this measured $V_{zero}$ instead of the theoretical 2.5 V in the current equation.

> **Code Changes (2 Marks):**
> ```cpp
> // In setup(), perform zero-current calibration:
> long zeroSum = 0;
> for (int i = 0; i < 1000; i++) zeroSum += analogRead(A0);
> float Vzero = (zeroSum / 1000.0) * 5.0 / 1024.0;
> // In loop(), use Vzero instead of 2.5:
> float current = (voltage - Vzero) / SENSITIVITY;
> ```

#### **Model Solution for Q B (b) — 7 Marks:**

> **Diagnosis (3 Marks):** A non-zero current reading (0.85 A) at no load indicates a **zero-point offset error** in the sensor. The actual quiescent voltage is higher than the assumed 2.5 V. The required correction is to perform the **zero-current calibration** described in Q B (a) and use the measured offset.
>
> **Corrected Code Snippet (2 Marks):** Replace the hardcoded `QUIESCENT = 2.5` with a calibrated value measured dynamically in `setup()`.

> **ADC Calculation for +15 A (2 Marks):**
> Using the formula $V_{OUT} = V_{CC}/2 + (S \times I_P)$:
> $V_{OUT} = 2.5 + (0.1 \times 15) = 2.5 + 1.5 = 4.0$ V
> Converting to ADC: $ADC = (4.0 / 5.0) \times 1024 = 0.8 \times 1024 = 819.2 \approx 819$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Quiescent Offset (Major deduction — 2 to 3 marks):** Students often compute current as $I = (V_{OUT})/S$ without subtracting $V_{CC}/2$. This produces wildly incorrect readings. **Always subtract 2.5 V first!**
>
> 2. **Using 1023 instead of 1024 in the ADC formula (1 mark deduction):** The formula is $V_{OUT} = (ADC \times V_{ref})/1024$, **not** 1023. While 1023 is the maximum count, the transfer function uses 1024 levels. Using 1023 introduces a small but consistent ~0.1% error.
>
> 3. **Wrong I2C Address (1 mark deduction):** If the LCD does not display anything, the most common cause is an incorrect I2C address. Always run an **I2C Scanner sketch** first to confirm the actual address (0x27 or 0x3F).
>
> 4. **Not Using `float` for Current Calculation (Code compilation issue):** Using `int` instead of `float` will truncate decimal values, showing current as `0 A` for small loads. Always use `float` for voltage and current variables.
>
> 5. **Connecting ACS712 IP+/IP- in Reverse:** The sensor is bidirectional, so reversing does not damage it, but ensure the **arrow on the sensor body** points from source to load for conventional current direction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read This Before The Exam!**

- The **Hall Effect** produces a voltage perpendicular to both current flow and magnetic field — discovered by **Edwin Hall in 1879**.
- The **ACS712** is a galvanically isolated, ratiometric, linear Hall-effect current sensor with three variants: **±5A (185 mV/A)**, **±20A (100 mV/A)**, **±30A (66 mV/A)**.
- The sensor's **quiescent output is $V_{CC}/2$** (typically 2.5 V at zero current).
- The **master current equation** is: $I_P = (V_{OUT} - 2.5) / S$.
- The **Arduino UNO ADC** is **10-bit** with a **5V default reference**, giving a resolution of **4.88 mV per step**.
- The **ADC formula** is: $V_{OUT} = (ADC \times 5.0) / 1024$.
- **I2C bus** uses two wires: **SDA (A4)** and **SCL (A5)** on the Arduino UNO.
- The **I2C LCD address** is commonly **0x27** (or **0x3F**).
- The LCD backpack contains built-in **4.7 kΩ pull-up resistors** — do not add external ones.
- **Zero-current calibration** is essential to remove the quiescent offset error.
- **Oversampling** (averaging 1000+ ADC samples) significantly reduces noise and improves measurement stability.
- The sensor output ranges from **0.5V to 4.5V** (never reaches 0V or 5V rails), providing amplifier headroom.
- For DC current, the **direction of current** determines whether the output is above or below 2.5V — positive current → output > 2.5V; reverse current → output < 2.5V.
- The **I2C clock** in standard Arduino `Wire` library runs at **100 kHz**.
- A **single I2C bus can support up to 127 devices** with unique 7-bit addresses.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Directional Control of the DC Motor Using Arduino
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL508 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Directional Control of the DC Motor Using Arduino

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Directional Control of a DC Motor using Arduino** is the process of governing the **rotational sense (clockwise or anticlockwise)**, **speed**, and **stop condition** of a brushed Permanent Magnet DC (PMDC) motor by means of an **H-Bridge power driver stage** (typically the **L293D** or **L298N** dual H-bridge IC) that is commanded by the **GPIO and PWM-capable pins of an Arduino development board** (commonly the ATmega328P-based Arduino Uno).

In strict KTU 2024 Scheme vocabulary, this experiment belongs to the domain of **actuator interfacing through a current-amplifying driver interface**, where the microcontroller (a low-current, low-voltage logic device) cannot drive a motor directly and therefore relies on a **bidirectional solid-state switch network** to invert the polarity of the voltage applied across the motor terminals.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Indian Railway Engine Turntable

Imagine a small **electric locomotive** at a railway depot that needs to go back into a shed. The driver cannot lift the engine and physically turn it around. Instead, the engine is driven onto a **rotating circular platform (a turntable)**, which spins and re-aligns the engine to face the opposite direction. The track it drove in on now becomes the track it will drive out on — *without the engine's wheels ever reversing*.

A **DC motor and its H-bridge driver** work in the exact same logical way:
- The **motor itself** cannot reverse its internal magnetic polarity.
- The **H-bridge** "re-aligns" the electrical connections so that the *terminal that was positive becomes negative*, and vice versa.
- The **motor's shaft** therefore spins in the opposite direction.

The **Arduino** acts as the **station master** that decides when the turntable (H-bridge) should rotate clockwise, anticlockwise, or remain idle — by sending precise **HIGH (5 V)** and **LOW (0 V)** logic signals to the bridge's control pins.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Box (Must Memorize for KTU Viva)**
> - **PMDC Motor** — Permanent Magnet DC Motor (the standard small motor used in labs).
> - **H-Bridge** — A four-switch (S1, S2, S3, S4) configuration forming the letter "H" with the motor on the crossbar.
> - **L293D** — A 16-pin dual H-bridge IC from Texas Instruments/ST Microelectronics rated at **600 mA continuous** per channel with built-in **flyback diodes**.
> - **PWM** — Pulse Width Modulation; an Arduino `analogWrite()` command that fakes an analog DC level using a fixed-frequency square wave.
> - **Enable Pin** — The pin on the L293D that acts as a "master switch" and also receives the PWM signal for speed control.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Lab Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Lab Value |
|---|---|
| Arduino Logic HIGH | **5 V DC** |
| Arduino Logic LOW | **0 V DC** |
| Arduino Pin Current (per pin) | **40 mA (max)**, 20 mA recommended |
| L293D Motor Supply (Vcc2 / pin 8) | **4.5 V – 36 V DC** |
| L293D Logic Supply (Vcc1 / pin 16) | **5 V DC** |
| L293D Continuous Output Current | **600 mA per channel** |
| L293D Peak Output Current | **1.2 A per channel (non-repetitive)** |
| PWM Frequency on Arduino pins 5, 6 | **980 Hz** (default `analogWrite`) |
| PWM Frequency on Arduino pins 9, 10, 3, 11 | **490 Hz** (default `analogWrite`) |
| Typical BO Motor (geared) rated voltage | **3 V – 12 V DC** |
| BO Motor no-load current | **70 mA – 250 mA** |
| BO Motor stall current | **~700 mA** (exceeds L293D limit — caution!) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle of a Brushed PMDC Motor

A brushed Permanent Magnet DC motor converts **electrical energy** into **mechanical rotation** using the **Lorentz force principle**:

1. Current flows through a **coil (armature)** placed inside a **static magnetic field** produced by permanent magnets (stator).
2. The current-carrying conductor experiences a **force F = BIL**, where B is the flux density, I is the current, and L is the conductor length.
3. This force creates a **torque** on the armature, producing rotation.
4. **Commutator + brushes** mechanically reverse the current direction every half-turn so that torque remains unidirectional.

### Direction Reversal Logic

The direction of rotation depends on **Fleming's Left-Hand Rule**:

$$
\vec{F} = I\vec{L} \times \vec{B}
$$

- If we **reverse the current direction** (I → −I), force F reverses, and so does rotation.
- If we **reverse the magnetic field** (B → −B), force F also reverses.
- A practical H-bridge achieves reversal by **swapping the supply polarity** at the motor terminals (equivalent to reversing I).

## 2.2 The H-Bridge — The Heart of Directional Control

> [!NOTE]
> **The H-Bridge consists of four switches (S1, S2, S3, S4) arranged in an "H" shape:**
> - The **motor** sits on the **vertical bar of the H**.
> - **S1 and S2** form the **left leg** (connected to V_supply and GND respectively).
> - **S3 and S4** form the **right leg**.
> - The **motor terminals** connect to the **midpoints** of each leg.

### Four Valid Switching States

| State | S1 | S2 | S3 | S4 | Motor Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Forward | ON | OFF | OFF | ON | Current flows left → right → CW rotation |
| Reverse | OFF | ON | ON | OFF | Current flows right → left → CCW rotation |
| Brake (Fast Stop) | OFF | ON | OFF | ON | Both motor terminals shorted to GND |
| Coast (Free Run) | OFF | OFF | OFF | OFF | Motor terminals floating, inertia stops it |
| **SHOOT-THROUGH (FORBIDDEN!)** | ON | OFF | ON | OFF | **Direct short across supply — destroys IC** |
| **SHOOT-THROUGH (FORBIDDEN!)** | OFF | ON | OFF | ON | **Direct short across supply — destroys IC** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Shoot-through** (both high-side and low-side switches ON on the same leg) is the #1 hardware-killing mistake. The L293D has **internal shoot-through protection logic**, but students must still avoid sending conflicting inputs.

## 2.3 The L293D Motor Driver IC — Pin Architecture

The L293D is a **dual H-bridge** IC (two complete H-bridges in one 16-pin DIP package). For this experiment, we use **only one half** (one H-bridge).

| Pin # | Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Enable 1,2** | Master enable for left H-bridge; receives PWM for speed |
| 2 | **Input 1A** | Logic input controlling left leg's top switch |
| 3 | **Output 1Y** | Motor terminal A |
| 4 | **GND** | Logic + power ground (pins 4, 5, 12, 13 tied together) |
| 5 | **GND** | Heat-sink ground |
| 6 | **Output 2Y** | Motor terminal B |
| 7 | **Input 2A** | Logic input controlling right leg's top switch |
| 8 | **Vcc2 (Vs)** | Motor power supply (**4.5 V – 36 V**) |
| 9 | **Enable 3,4** | Enable for right H-bridge (unused in this experiment) |
| 10 | Input 3A | Unused |
| 11 | Output 3Y | Unused |
| 12 | GND | Heat-sink ground |
| 13 | Output 4Y | Unused |
| 14 | Input 4A | Unused |
| 15 | Input 3B | Unused |
| 16 | **Vcc1 (Vss)** | Logic supply (**5 V**) |

## 2.4 Truth Table for One L293D H-Bridge Channel

| Enable Pin (1) | Input 1A (Pin 2) | Input 2A (Pin 7) | Motor Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| HIGH | HIGH | LOW | **Rotates Clockwise (CW)** |
| HIGH | LOW | HIGH | **Rotates Counter-Clockwise (CCW)** |
| HIGH | HIGH | HIGH | **Stops (Brake)** |
| HIGH | LOW | LOW | **Stops (Brake)** |
| LOW | X | X | **Coasts (Free Run / Hi-Z)** |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{supply}$ | Average voltage across motor from PWM | V |
| 2 | $D = \dfrac{T_{ON}}{T_{ON} + T_{OFF}}$ | Duty Cycle (0 to 1) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $E_b = V - I_a R_a$ | Back-EMF equation | V |
| 4 | $N \propto \dfrac{E_b}{\phi} = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{\phi}$ | Speed is proportional to net armature voltage | rpm |
| 5 | $T \propto \phi \cdot I_a$ | Torque is proportional to flux × armature current | N·m |
| 6 | $P_{elec} = V \cdot I_a$ | Electrical input power | W |
| 7 | $P_{mech} = T \cdot \omega = T \cdot \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Mechanical output power | W |
| 8 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{mech}}{P_{elec}} \times 100\%$ | Efficiency | % |
| 9 | $V_{out,L293D} \approx V_{s} - 1.4\,V$ | L293D output voltage (high-side + low-side V_DS drop) | V |
| 10 | $I_{max,L293D} = 600\,mA$ (continuous) | Per-channel current limit of L293D | A |

> [!NOTE]
> In the L293D formula, the **1.4 V drop** arises because the output stage uses **Darlington transistor pairs**, so the high-side drop is ~1.0 V and the low-side drop is ~0.4 V. Always account for this in power-supply margin calculations.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Robotics**: Wheeled robots (line-followers, obstacle-avoiders) need differential directional control of two wheels.
- **CNC Machines and 3D Printers**: DC motors drive lead screws; direction = tool feed direction.
- **Conveyor Belts**: Reversing flow direction in industrial sorting lines.
- **Automotive Applications**: Power windows, seat adjusters, wiper direction control.
- **HVAC Systems**: Damper actuators that open/close ventilation flaps.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Average Motor Voltage from a PWM Signal

A PWM signal is a square wave with period **T** that is HIGH for time **T_ON** and LOW for time **T_OFF**. The **duty cycle D** is defined as:

$$
D = \frac{T_{ON}}{T_{ON} + T_{OFF}} = \frac{T_{ON}}{T}
$$

The **average DC value** delivered to a resistive or inductive load (such as a motor) is the time-average of the square wave:

$$
V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v(t)\, dt
$$

Substituting the square-wave definition (V during T_ON, 0 during T_OFF):

$$
V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T} \left[ \int_{0}^{T_{ON}} V_{supply}\, dt + \int_{T_{ON}}^{T} 0 \cdot dt \right]
$$

**Step 1:** Pull the constant V_supply out of the first integral:

$$
V_{avg} = \frac{V_{supply}}{T} \int_{0}^{T_{ON}} dt
$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate the integral:

$$
\int_{0}^{T_{ON}} dt = T_{ON}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute back:

$$
V_{avg} = \frac{V_{supply}}{T} \cdot T_{ON} = V_{supply} \cdot \frac{T_{ON}}{T}
$$

**Step 4:** Recognize T_ON / T = D:

$$
\boxed{V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{supply}}
$$

> This means an `analogWrite(motorPin, 127)` command (where 127/255 ≈ 0.498 ≈ 50% duty cycle) applied to a 12 V motor supply results in an effective average voltage of approximately **5.98 V** — about half the supply. The motor spins at roughly half its no-load speed (neglecting mechanical losses).

## 3.2 Derivation of Back-EMF and the Motor Speed Equation

Apply **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** around the armature loop of a DC motor:

$$
V_{supply} = E_b + I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt}
$$

**Step 1:** In **steady-state** (constant speed), the inductive term vanishes because dI/dt = 0:

$$
V_{supply} = E_b + I_a R_a
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for the back-EMF:

$$
\boxed{E_b = V_{supply} - I_a R_a}
$$

**Step 3:** The back-EMF is also given by Faraday's law as:

$$
E_b = k \phi \omega = k \phi \cdot \frac{2 \pi N}{60}
$$

**Step 4:** Equate the two expressions and solve for speed N:

$$
k \phi \cdot \frac{2 \pi N}{60} = V_{supply} - I_a R_a
$$

**Step 5:** Rearrange:

$$
N = \frac{60 \cdot (V_{supply} - I_a R_a)}{2 \pi k \phi}
$$

**Step 6:** Since k and φ are constants for a given motor, the proportional form is:

$$
\boxed{N \propto \frac{V_{supply} - I_a R_a}{\phi}}
$$

**Engineering Insight:** This derivation proves that **decreasing the applied voltage (using PWM at lower duty cycle) directly decreases the steady-state speed** — this is the foundation of speed-controlled motor drive systems.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Speed vs. Duty Cycle

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A 12 V PMDC motor has armature resistance R_a = 5 Ω, motor constant kφ = 0.05 V·s/rad. At no-load, the current is I_a = 0.2 A. Calculate the steady-state speed for PWM duty cycles of D = 1.0 (100%), D = 0.5 (50%), and D = 0.25 (25%).

### Case 1: D = 1.0 (100%, full 12 V)

**Step 1:** Average voltage:

$$
V_{avg} = 1.0 \times 12 = 12\,V
$$

**Step 2:** Back-EMF:

$$
E_b = 12 - (0.2)(5) = 12 - 1 = 11\,V
$$

**Step 3:** Angular speed:

$$
\omega = \frac{E_b}{k\phi} = \frac{11}{0.05} = 220\,rad/s
$$

**Step 4:** Convert to RPM:

$$
N = \frac{220 \times 60}{2\pi} = \frac{13200}{6.2832} \approx 2100\,rpm
$$

### Case 2: D = 0.5 (50%, 6 V average)

**Step 1:**

$$
V_{avg} = 0.5 \times 12 = 6\,V
$$

**Step 2:**

$$
E_b = 6 - (0.2)(5) = 6 - 1 = 5\,V
$$

**Step 3:**

$$
\omega = \frac{5}{0.05} = 100\,rad/s
$$

**Step 4:**

$$
N = \frac{100 \times 60}{2\pi} \approx 955\,rpm
$$

### Case 3: D = 0.25 (25%, 3 V average)

**Step 1:**

$$
V_{avg} = 0.25 \times 12 = 3\,V
$$

**Step 2:**

$$
E_b = 3 - (0.2)(5) = 3 - 1 = 2\,V
$$

**Step 3:**

$$
\omega = \frac{2}{0.05} = 40\,rad/s
$$

**Step 4:**

$$
N = \frac{40 \times 60}{2\pi} \approx 382\,rpm
$$

### Summary Table — Speed vs. Duty Cycle

| Duty Cycle D | V_avg (V) | E_b (V) | ω (rad/s) | N (rpm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1.00 | 12.0 | 11.0 | 220 | **2100** |
| 0.75 | 9.0 | 8.0 | 160 | 1528 |
| 0.50 | 6.0 | 5.0 | 100 | **955** |
| 0.25 | 3.0 | 2.0 | 40 | **382** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** Speed is **not perfectly linear** with duty cycle because of the I_a·R_a voltage drop and mechanical friction. The relationship is approximately linear only when I_a is small (light load / no-load conditions).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Component Selection Decision Tree

| Decision Criterion | Recommended Choice | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Motor current < 600 mA | **L293D** | Built-in flyback diodes, DIP package, breadboard friendly |
| Motor current 600 mA – 2 A | **L298N** (with external diodes) | Higher current, but needs external flyback protection |
| Motor current > 2 A | **MOSFET H-Bridge (e.g., BTS7960)** | Discrete MOSFETs handle kW-class loads |
| Need only forward/stop, not reverse | **Single BJT/MOSFET (e.g., IRF520)** | Half H-bridge suffices; cheaper |
| Need speed control | Must use **PWM on Enable pin** | L293D's enable pin accepts 5 V PWM cleanly |
| Need direction + speed simultaneously | **PWM on Enable + digital on Inputs** | Standard textbook configuration |

---

| 5 V | 16 (Vcc1) | Logic supply | Always 5 V |
| GND | 4, 5, 12, 13 | Common ground | 0 V |
| Digital 7 | 2 (Input 1A) | Direction bit A | HIGH = 5 V, LOW = 0 V |
| Digital 8 | 7 (Input 2A) | Direction bit B | HIGH = 5 V, LOW = 0 V |
