# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]
**Explain the role of an H-bridge in DC motor direction control. Why is it preferred over directly connecting the motor to the Arduino?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An H-bridge is a four-switch electronic circuit arranged in an 'H' topology that allows the **polarity of the voltage applied across a DC motor to be reversed** without mechanically rewiring the motor. By selectively activating diagonal pairs of switches, the current through the motor can be made to flow left-to-right (forward) or right-to-left (reverse), thereby reversing the direction of rotation.

It is preferred over direct Arduino-to-motor connection because of three critical reasons:

1. **Current Amplification:** Arduino GPIO pins source/sink a maximum of **40 mA**, whereas small DC motors require **200–700 mA**. The H-bridge (e.g., L293D) can deliver up to **600 mA continuous** per channel.
2. **Voltage Compatibility:** Arduino operates at **5 V**, while motors often need **6 V, 9 V, 12 V, or higher**. The H-bridge's motor supply (Vcc2) is independent of the logic supply.
3. **Bidirectional Control:** Arduino alone cannot reverse polarity; the H-bridge provides the four-switch topology needed for forward, reverse, brake, and coast states.

**Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for H-bridge definition, 1 mark for switch-pair concept, 1 mark for current/voltage justification.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**What is the function of the Enable pin of the L293D IC? How can it be used to control motor speed?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Enable pin (pin 1 or pin 9)** of the L293D acts as a **master switch** for the corresponding H-bridge channel. When the Enable pin is driven **LOW**, the outputs (1Y, 2Y or 3Y, 4Y) enter a **high-impedance state**, and the motor is effectively disconnected and free to coast. When the Enable pin is driven **HIGH**, the outputs become active and obey the logic inputs (Input 1A, Input 2A) to drive the motor in the commanded direction.

To control **motor speed**, a **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation) signal** is applied to the Enable pin. The L293D's internal logic responds to the duty cycle of this PWM signal, effectively modulating the **average voltage** delivered to the motor. On the Arduino, the function `analogWrite(enablePin, value)` where value ranges from **0 to 255** produces a duty cycle of `value/255`. A higher duty cycle corresponds to a higher average voltage and hence a higher motor speed, in accordance with the relation:

$$
V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{supply}
$$

**Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for Enable pin master switch role, 1 mark for HIGH/LOW behavior, 1 mark for PWM-speed linkage with formula.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Design and explain the interfacing circuit of a DC motor with an Arduino Uno using the **L293D motor driver IC**. Draw the complete circuit diagram, identify all pin connections, and state the motor supply voltage considerations. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** Write the complete Arduino sketch (program) to perform the following sequence of operations on the DC motor:
1. Run forward at 70% speed for 2 seconds.
2. Brake (fast stop) for 500 ms.
3. Run reverse at 50% speed for 2 seconds.
4. Coast (free run) for 1 second.
5. Repeat the sequence indefinitely. **\[7 marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution to Part A(a) — Circuit Design and Pin Mapping

**Step 1 — Identify the L293D channel to be used:**

We will use the **left H-bridge** of the L293D (controlled by pins 1, 2, 3, 6, 7).

**Step 2 — Power Supply Architecture:**

- The **logic side** of the L293D (pin 16, Vcc1) is powered by the **Arduino 5 V pin**. This is acceptable because the logic block draws only a few mA.
- The **motor side** (pin 8, Vcc2) is powered by an **external DC supply**. For a standard 5 V–12 V BO motor used in labs, a **9 V battery or 12 V adapter** is used. *Always choose Vcc2 ≥ the motor's rated voltage for full-speed operation, or lower for slower speed.*

**Step 3 — Common Ground:**

The Arduino GND, the L293D's logic GND (pin 4, 5), and the **negative terminal of the external motor supply** must all be tied together on a **common ground rail**. Without a common ground, the L293D's input logic levels will be undefined and the motor will behave erratically.

**Step 4 — Direction Control Inputs:**

- **Arduino Digital Pin 7 → L293D Pin 2 (Input 1A)**
- **Arduino Digital Pin 8 → L293D Pin 7 (Input 2A)**

**Step 5 — Speed Control (PWM) Input:**

- **Arduino PWM Pin 9 → L293D Pin 1 (Enable 1,2)**

**Step 6 — Motor Output Connections:**

- **Motor Wire 1 → L293D Pin 3 (Output 1Y)**
- **Motor Wire 2 → L293D Pin 6 (Output 2Y)**

**Step 7 — Flyback Diodes:**

The L293D has **internal flyback diodes** between each output and the Vcc2/GND rails. **No external diodes are required**, which is a major advantage over the L298N.

**Step 8 — Decoupling Capacitor (Best Practice):**

Place a **0.1 µF ceramic capacitor** between the Arduino 5 V pin and GND, close to the L293D, to suppress voltage spikes from the motor's inductive kick.

**Mark Allocation:** 2 marks circuit design, 2 marks pin mapping, 1 mark supply architecture, 1 mark common ground, 1 mark flyback diode knowledge.

---

#### Model Solution to Part A(b) — Arduino Sketch

```cpp
/*============================================================
 * Program: Directional Control of DC Motor using L293D
 * Board:   Arduino Uno (ATmega328P)
 * Pins:    D7 -> IN1A, D8 -> IN2A, D9 -> EN1,2
 * KTU Course: PCEOL508 - Microcontrollers Lab
 *============================================================*/

// ---------- Pin Definitions ----------
const int IN1A = 7;      // Direction control bit A
const int IN2A = 8;      // Direction control bit B
const int EN12 = 9;      // Enable / PWM pin for speed

// ---------- Speed Values (0-255) ----------
const int SPEED_FWD   = 178;   // 70% of 255 = 178.5 -> 178
const int SPEED_REV   = 128;   // 50% of 255 = 127.5 -> 128

// ---------- Time Delays (milliseconds) ----------
const int T_FWD       = 2000;  // 2 seconds forward
const int T_BRAKE     = 500;   // 500 ms brake
const int T_REV       = 2000;  // 2 seconds reverse
const int T_COAST     = 1000;  // 1 second coast

void setup() {
  // Configure control pins as OUTPUT
  pinMode(IN1A, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(IN2A, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(EN12, OUTPUT);

  // Initial state: motor stopped (coast)
  digitalWrite(IN1A, LOW);
  digitalWrite(IN2A, LOW);
  analogWrite(EN12, 0);
}

void loop() {
  // ---- Phase 1: Forward at 70% speed ----
  digitalWrite(IN1A, HIGH);     // IN1A = 1
  digitalWrite(IN2A, LOW);      // IN2A = 0
  analogWrite(EN12, SPEED_FWD); // PWM duty = 70%
  delay(T_FWD);                 // run for 2 s

  // ---- Phase 2: Brake (fast stop) ----
  digitalWrite(IN1A, LOW);      // both LOW -> brake
  digitalWrite(IN2A, LOW);
  analogWrite(EN12, 0);
  delay(T_BRAKE);

  // ---- Phase 3: Reverse at 50% speed ----
  digitalWrite(IN1A, LOW);      // IN1A = 0
  digitalWrite(IN2A, HIGH);     // IN2A = 1
  analogWrite(EN12, SPEED_REV); // PWM duty = 50%
  delay(T_REV);

  // ---- Phase 4: Coast (free run) ----
  digitalWrite(IN1A, LOW);
  digitalWrite(IN2A, LOW);
  analogWrite(EN12, 0);         // Enable LOW -> Hi-Z
  delay(T_COAST);
}
```

**Code Walkthrough for Valuation:**

1. **Pin Declarations (1 mark):** `const int` definitions establish readable, changeable pin mappings.
2. **setup() Function (2 marks):** `pinMode()` configures all three pins as outputs. Initial state ensures motor is OFF at startup.
3. **Forward Phase Logic (1 mark):** `IN1A=HIGH, IN2A=LOW, analogWrite(EN12, 178)` is the canonical CW command at 70% speed.
4. **Brake Phase Logic (1 mark):** `IN1A=LOW, IN2A=LOW` is the **brake** state (both motor terminals shorted to ground through low-side switches). For **coast**, `analogWrite(EN12, 0)` would be set to LOW.
5. **Reverse Phase Logic (1 mark):** `IN1A=LOW, IN2A=HIGH, analogWrite(EN12, 128)` is the canonical CCW command at 50% speed.
6. **Delay Sequence and Loop (1 mark):** Correct `delay()` values in milliseconds matching the problem statement; infinite `loop()`.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a)** Derive the relationship between the **average voltage** delivered to a DC motor and the **PWM duty cycle** applied at the L293D's Enable pin. A 12 V motor is being driven by a 50% duty cycle PWM. Calculate the **average voltage** and the **back-EMF** if the armature current is 0.3 A and the armature resistance is 4 Ω. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** Explain with a neat sketch the **four switching states** of an H-bridge. Why is **shoot-through** considered a catastrophic fault? What protection mechanism does the L293D provide against it? **\[7 marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution to Part B(a) — Derivation and Numerical Calculation

**Derivation of V_avg vs Duty Cycle (5 marks):**

Let T be the PWM period, T_ON the time the Enable pin is HIGH, and T_OFF the time it is LOW. The duty cycle D is:

$$
D = \frac{T_{ON}}{T_{ON} + T_{OFF}} = \frac{T_{ON}}{T}
$$

The instantaneous voltage across the motor is:

$$
v(t) = \begin{cases} V_{supply} & 0 \le t \le T_{ON} \\ 0 & T_{ON} < t \le T \end{cases}
$$

The average voltage is the time integral of v(t) over one period:

$$
V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v(t)\, dt
$$

$$
V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T}\left[\int_{0}^{T_{ON}} V_{supply}\, dt + \int_{T_{ON}}^{T} 0\, dt\right]
$$

$$
V_{avg} = \frac{V_{supply}}{T} \cdot T_{ON} = V_{supply} \cdot D
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{supply}}
$$

**Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**

Given: D = 0.5, V_supply = 12 V, I_a = 0.3 A, R_a = 4 Ω.

**Step 1:** Compute V_avg:

$$
V_{avg} = 0.5 \times 12 = 6\,V
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the back-EMF equation:

$$
E_b = V_{avg} - I_a R_a = 6 - (0.3)(4) = 6 - 1.2 = 4.8\,V
$$

**Final Answer:** V_avg = **6 V**, E_b = **4.8 V**.

**Mark Allocation:** 4 marks derivation (with all steps), 1 mark substitution, 1 mark final numerical answer, 1 mark unit.

---

#### Model Solution to Part B(b) — H-Bridge States and Shoot-Through

**H-Bridge Switching States (4 marks):**

A standard H-bridge consists of four switches labeled S1, S2, S3, S4 arranged such that the motor sits on the crossbar. The four valid states are:

| State | Active Switches | Motor Action |
|---|---|---|
| **Forward (CW)** | S1, S4 | Current V+ → S1 → Motor → S4 → GND; motor turns CW |
| **Reverse (CCW)** | S2, S3 | Current V+ → S3 → Motor → S2 → GND; motor turns CCW |
| **Brake (Fast Stop)** | S2, S4 | Both motor terminals shorted to GND; motor brakes |
| **Coast (Free Run)** | None (all OFF) | Motor terminals floating; motor coasts to a halt |

**Shoot-Through Explanation (2 marks):**

**Shoot-through** occurs when both the **high-side switch (S1) and the low-side switch (S2) on the same leg** are turned ON simultaneously. This creates a **direct short circuit from V_supply to GND** through the switch pair. The current through the switches rises to a value limited only by the on-resistance of the transistors and the internal resistance of the supply — typically **tens of amperes** in a fraction of a microsecond. The resulting I²R heating destroys the H-bridge IC within milliseconds.

**L293D Protection Mechanism (1 mark):**

The L293D's internal driver uses **Darlington transistor pairs** with **controlled switching transitions** and **base-drive time delays** that ensure the high-side switch fully turns OFF before the low-side switch begins to turn ON (and vice versa). This **built-in dead-time / anti-shoot-through logic** prevents the catastrophic cross-conduction fault that would otherwise occur from noisy or overlapping input signals.

**Mark Allocation:** 1 mark per state table entry (4 marks), 1.5 marks shoot-through explanation, 1 mark L293D protection, 0.5 mark for neatness and sketch.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (Read Carefully Before Exam)**
>
> 1. **Confusing Brake and Coast:** *Brake* = both inputs LOW, both motor terminals pulled to GND (regenerative damping). *Coast* = Enable pin LOW, motor terminals floating. Many students write `digitalWrite(IN1A, LOW); digitalWrite(IN2A, LOW);` and call it "coast" — it is actually **brake**!
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Common Ground:** If the Arduino GND and the L293D's external motor supply GND are not tied, the input logic levels become undefined. The motor will either not run or run at full speed in one direction regardless of code. **Always mention "common ground" in your circuit diagram.**
>
> 3. **Using Digital Pins Without PWM for Speed Control:** Trying to use `digitalWrite()` on a non-PWM pin and expecting speed variation will fail. Only pins **3, 5, 6, 9, 10, 11** support `analogWrite()` on the Arduino Uno. Pin 9 is recommended in this experiment.
>
> 4. **Stalling the Motor:** If the duty cycle is set too low, the motor cannot overcome static friction and **stalls**. The armature current rises to the **stall current** (often 5–10× the no-load current), which can exceed the L293D's 600 mA limit and **permanently damage the IC**. Always check the motor's stall current rating.
>
> 5. **Wrong Speed Percentage Mapping:** Many students write `analogWrite(EN12, 70)` thinking it means 70%. The range is **0–255**, so 70% is `0.70 × 255 ≈ 178`. Writing 70 directly gives only **27%** speed.
>
> 6. **Skipping Pin Initialization in setup():** If `pinMode()` is not called for the Enable pin, the `analogWrite()` may not work reliably. Always call `pinMode(EN12, OUTPUT)` first.
>
> 7. **Drawing the L293D with 4 outputs on one motor:** The L293D has **two separate H-bridges**. Each bridge can drive **one motor only**. A single L293D IC = max **2 DC motors**. Do not try to drive 3 or 4 motors from one L293D.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets — Read 5 Minutes Before Entering the Lab / Exam Hall**
>
> ☐ DC motor direction = polarity of voltage across its terminals. Reverse the polarity → reverse the direction.
>
> ☐ Arduino **cannot** drive a motor directly (40 mA limit vs. 200–700 mA motor demand). **Always use a driver IC.**
>
> ☐ L293D = **dual H-bridge + internal flyback diodes** + 600 mA continuous per channel. Pins: 1=EN, 2=IN1A, 3=OUT1Y, 6=OUT2Y, 7=IN2A, 8=Vcc2 (motor), 16=Vcc1 (5V logic).
>
> ☐ Forward = **IN1A=HIGH, IN2A=LOW, EN=HIGH**.
> ☐ Reverse = **IN1A=LOW, IN2A=HIGH, EN=HIGH**.
> ☐ Brake  = **IN1A=LOW, IN2A=LOW, EN=HIGH** (or both HIGH).
> ☐ Coast  = **EN=LOW** (regardless of IN1A, IN2A).
>
> ☐ PWM on Enable pin = speed control. `analogWrite(EN12, value)` with value = 0 to 255.
>
> ☐ Average motor voltage: $V_{avg} = D \times V_{supply}$.
>
> ☐ Back-EMF: $E_b = V_{avg} - I_a R_a$.
>
> ☐ Speed proportional to back-EMF: $N \propto E_b / \phi$.
>
> ☐ **Common ground** between Arduino, L293D logic, and external motor supply is **mandatory**.
>
> ☐ **Shoot-through** = both switches on the same leg ON → short circuit → IC destroyed. L293D has internal dead-time protection.
>
> ☐ L293D voltage drop = ~1.4 V (Darlington pair). Motor sees Vcc2 − 1.4 V, not Vcc2 directly.
>
> ☐ Stall current of a small DC motor can exceed the L293D's 600 mA limit → add current-limiting or use a stronger driver (L298N, BTS7960).
>
> ☐ L293D's 4 unused pins (10, 11, 14, 15 and pins 9, 3, 6, 13 for the second bridge) should be **left floating or tied to GND** to prevent floating-input noise.
>
> ☐ Always include a `delay()` after a state change to allow the motor to physically respond before the next command — debugging "the motor doesn't run" issues is often simply a missing delay.
>
> ☐ Motor supply (Vcc2) should ideally be slightly **higher** than the motor's rated voltage to compensate for the L293D's 1.4 V drop. Example: rated 9 V motor → use 11–12 V supply.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interfacing of the Relay with Arduino
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEOL508 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Interfacing of the Relay with Arduino # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** lab curriculum, **Interfacing of a Relay with Arduino** is defined as the structured hardware–software methodology by which a low-power digital output pin of the **ATmega328P microcontroller** (operating at **+5 V DC** and sourcing a maximum of **40 mA** per I/O pin) is used to energize the electromagnetic coil of an electromechanical relay, thereby enabling the microcontroller to switch high-voltage or high-current AC/DC loads (such as lamps, fans, or solenoids) that it cannot drive directly. The relay forms part of an **Isolated Switching Actuator Subsystem**, comprising: - A control circuit (Arduino + driver transistor) - A load circuit (Relay contacts + AC load) - A galvanic isolation boundary (air gap / optocoupler) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PCEOL508):** This experiment validates **CO2 — "Interface various input/output peripherals with the microcontroller"** and **CO4 — "Design and implement embedded applications using real-time hardware interfacing."** It is typically assessed in the **continuous evaluation viva** and the **end-semester practical record**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bouncer at a Club" Imagine the Arduino as a **manager** sitting in a small office. He is well-dressed and gives clear instructions (**logic HIGH/LOW signals**), but he is physically too weak to physically push open the massive iron gate of a factory by himself. The **transistor (BC547 / 2N2222)** is the **strong bouncer** standing at the door. When the manager (Arduino) whispers "Open it" (sends a small **5 V / ~1 mA** signal to the base), the bouncer flexes his muscles and slams the gate open, letting high power rush into the relay coil. The **relay itself** is that massive iron gate. Once the coil is energized, an internal mechanical lever (the armature) snaps shut, completing a *separate*, *physically isolated* high-voltage circuit — like a 230 V AC light bulb glowing, while the Arduino remains completely safe and untouched. > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Recap:** Arduino cannot touch high voltage. It uses a transistor as an amplifier, which energizes a relay's coil, which mechanically switches a high-power circuit. This is **indirect switching**. ## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Ratings | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Arduino Logic HIGH | $V_{OH}$ | **+5.0** | V DC | | Arduino Pin Max Current | $I_{OH(max)}$ | **40** | mA | | 5V Relay Coil Voltage | $V_{coil}$ | **5** | V DC | | Relay Coil Current | $I_{coil}$ | **70 – 100** | mA | | Relay Contact Rating (Resistive) | $I_{contact}$ | **10 A @ 250 V AC** | A / V | | BC547 DC Current Gain | $h_{FE}(\beta)$ | **200 – 800** | dimensionless | | Transistor $V_{BE(sat)}$ | $V_{BE}$ | **0.7** | V | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Internal Architecture of an Electromechanical Relay A relay is fundamentally a **single-pole double-throw (SPDT)** or **DPDT** switch whose wiper is mechanically coupled to a magnetic armature. Its operational phases are: 1. **De-energized state** — Spring holds the armature against the **Normally Closed (NC)** contact. 2. **Energized state** — Current through the coil generates a magnetic flux ($\Phi$) proportional to the **ampere-turns** ($N \cdot I_{coil}$), pulling the armature to the **Normally Open (NO)** contact. 3. **Hysteresis & bounce** — Mechanical contacts exhibit **contact bounce** (typically **1 – 5 ms**) which can be debounced in software. ## 2.2 Why a Driver Transistor is Mandatory The **ATmega328P datasheet** specifies an *absolute maximum* DC sink/source of **40 mA per pin** with a *recommended* operating current of **20 mA**. A 5 V relay coil typically demands **70 – 100 mA**, which exceeds the microcontroller's safe limit and will permanently damage the I/O pin without external buffering. The NPN transistor in **common-emitter configuration** acts as a **current amplifier**: $$I_c = \beta \cdot I_b$$ Where $I_c$ must exceed $I_{coil}$ to drive the relay into **saturation** (fully ON). ## 2.3 Flyback Diode (Freewheeling Diode) — The "Kick-Back" Protector When the relay coil is suddenly de-energized, the collapsing magnetic field induces a high-voltage reverse-EMF spike of several hundred volts (per **Lenz's Law**). This spike can destroy the transistor via reverse breakdown of the **collector–base junction**. A **1N4007 flyback diode** is connected in **reverse-bias** across the coil, providing a safe circulating path for the inductive kick. The energy stored in the inductor is dissipated as: $$E_{stored} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot L_{coil} \cdot I_{coil}^{2}$$ ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table is the **exam-ready formula sheet** for relay-driver circuit design. All values are mathematically verified against standard textbook practice. | # | Formula | Description | Typical Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $I_b = \dfrac{I_c}{\beta}$ | Base current required to saturate the transistor | Driver design | | 2 | $R_b = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{I_b}$ | Base resistor sizing equation | Arduino → Transistor base | | 3 | $I_{coil} = \dfrac{V_{coil}}{R_{coil}}$ | Ohm's law for relay coil | Load analysis | | 4 | $V_{EMF} = -L \dfrac{di}{dt}$ | Inductive kick-back voltage | Flyback protection | | 5 | $P_{coil} = V_{coil} \cdot I_{coil}$ | Power dissipation in coil | Thermal design | | 6 | $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2 \, \text{V}$ | Saturation voltage of BC547 | Transistor ON-state loss | | 7 | $f_{switch} \le \dfrac{1}{10 \cdot t_{bounce}}$ | Safe switching frequency | Mechanical contact reliability | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Relay interfacing underpins **Industrial Automation** (PLC discrete I/O), **Home Automation** (smart switches, IoT), **Automotive Electronics** (starter relay control), and **Power Electronics** (grid switching). The pattern learned here — *microcontroller → driver → isolator → high-power load* — is the foundational block of every **Embedded Switching System** on the market. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Base Resistor ($R_b$) Sizing We will rigorously derive the value of the base resistor $R_b$ such that the BC547 transistor is driven into **hard saturation** when the Arduino outputs HIGH. ### Given Data (from datasheets) - Arduino supply voltage: $V_{CC} = 5.0 \, \text{V}$ - Transistor $V_{BE(sat)} = 0.7 \, \text{V}$ - DC current gain (minimum guaranteed for BC547): $\beta_{min} = 100$ - Relay coil current: $I_{coil} = 70 \, \text{mA}$ - Safety overdrive factor: $k = 2$ (drives the transistor twice as hard as minimum requirement to ensure saturation regardless of $\beta$ variation) ### Step 1 — Define Collector Current Since the relay coil is the collector load, the required collector current equals the coil current: $$I_c = I_{coil} = 70 \, \text{mA}$$ ### Step 2 — Calculate the Minimum Base Current Applying the transistor current equation $I_c = \beta \cdot I_b$, we solve for $I_b$: $$ \begin{aligned} I_{b(min)} &= \frac{I_c}{\beta_{min}} \\ &= \frac{70 \times 10^{-3}}{100} \\ &= 0.7 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{A} \\ &= 0.7 \, \text{mA} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Apply the Safety Overdrive Factor To guarantee saturation even with a "weak" transistor ($\beta = 100$), we overdrive the base by a factor of 2: $$ \begin{aligned} I_{b(actual)} &= k \cdot I_{b(min)} \\ &= 2 \times 0.7 \, \text{mA} \\ &= 1.4 \, \text{mA} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) Around the Base Loop The base loop consists of the Arduino pin, the base resistor $R_b$, and the base–emitter junction: $$ \begin{aligned} V_{CC} - I_b \cdot R_b - V_{BE} &= 0 \\ I_b \cdot R_b &= V_{CC} - V_{BE} \\ R_b &= \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{I_b} \\ R_b &= \frac{5.0 - 0.7}{1.4 \times 10^{-3}} \\ R_b &= \frac{4.3}{1.4 \times 10^{-3}} \\ R_b &= 3071.4 \, \Omega \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5 — Select the Closest Standard E12 Resistor Value The nearest commercially available standard resistor in the **E12 series** is: $$R_b \approx 2.7 \, \text{k}\Omega \quad \text{(Standard value)}$$ A value of **1 kΩ** is also commonly used in KTU lab kits, providing even stronger overdrive and ensuring reliable switching even with aged or low-$\beta$ transistors. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Component-by-Component Justification > [!IMPORTANT] > The following matrix is the **lab-viva answer gold standard**. Memorize the *why* of each component for full marks. | Component | Value | Function | What happens if removed? | |---|---|---|---| | **Arduino Uno** | ATmega328P | Logic controller | No control signal source | | **Relay (5V SPDT)** | 5 V, 70 mA coil | Isolated switch | Load cannot be switched | | **BC547 NPN** | $\beta = 200\text{–}800$ | Current amplifier | Arduino pin burns out | | **Base Resistor ($R_b$)** | 1 kΩ | Limits base current | Transistor destroyed (over-$I_b$) | | **Flyback Diode (1N4007)** | 1 A, 1000 V | Suppresses inductive kick | Transistor CB junction punctures | | **Indicator LED** | Red, 2 V | Visual status | Cannot debug easily | | **Current Limiting R** | 220 Ω | Protects LED | LED burns out | | **Pull-down Resistor** | 10 kΩ | Holds base LOW on reset | Relay may flicker on startup | ## 3.3 Step-by-Step Working Logic of the Arduino Sketch 1. **Initialization phase** — `pinMode(relayPin, OUTPUT);` configures the GPIO pin as a push-pull output. 2. **OFF state** — `digitalWrite(relayPin, LOW);` → Base is at 0 V → Transistor OFF → Coil de-energized → Armature on **NC** contact. 3. **ON state** — `digitalWrite(relayPin, HIGH);` → Base receives ~1.4 mA → Transistor saturates → Coil energized → Magnetic flux pulls armature to **NO** contact → Load (bulb) glows. 4. **Debounce delay** — `delay(200);` provides mechanical settling time. 5. **Repeat / Toggling** — Toggles state every 1 second (or as required by application). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Complete Circuit Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram) The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **functional flow architecture** of the relay-driver interfacing subsystem, mapping signal, control, and power-flow interactions exactly as wired on the KTU lab breadboard. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Arduino Uno ATmega328P] -- GPIO Pin 7
5V 1.4mA --> B[Base Resistor 1kΩ] B -- Limited Base Current --> C[BC547 NPN Transistor] C -- Collector Current 70mA --> D[Relay Coil 5V] D -- Inductive Spike Path --> E[Flyback Diode 1N4007] C -- Emitter --> F[Ground Rail] D -- Magnetic Flux Pull --> G[Relay Armature SPDT] G -- NO Contact --> H[230V AC Bulb Load] G -- NC Contact --> I[Optional Standby Load] A -- Logic Indicator --> J[Red LED 220Ω] H -- Returns to --> K[AC Mains Neutral] ``` ## 4.2 Functional Layer Architecture The hardware is best understood in **three isolated functional layers** — this is a key KTU viva topic. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph L1["LAYER 1 CONTROL SIDE LOW POWER 5V DC"] direction LR A1[Arduino MCU] --> A2[Driver Transistor BC547] A2 --> A3[Relay Coil] A1 -.Status.-> A4[Indicator LED] end subgraph L2["LAYER 2 ISOLATION BOUNDARY Air Gap"] I1[Galvanic Barrier
No Electrical Connection] end subgraph L3["LAYER 3 LOAD SIDE HIGH POWER 230V AC"] direction LR B1[Relay COM Terminal] --> B2[NO Contact] B2 --> B3[230V AC Bulb] B3 --> B4[AC Neutral] end L1 --- I1 I1 --- L3 ``` ## 4.3 Working-State Transition Diagram ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> OFF_State OFF_State: Relay OFF
NC Contact Closed
LED LOW OFF_State --> ON_State: digitalWrite HIGH
Coil Energized ON_State: Relay ON
NO Contact Closed
LED HIGH
Bulb Glows ON_State --> OFF_State: digitalWrite LOW
Coil De-energized ON_State --> ON_State: delay 1 second OFF_State --> OFF_State: delay 1 second ``` ## 4.4 Pin-Connection Reference Table (Breadboard Wiring) > [!NOTE] > Memorize this wiring table — it is the **most frequently asked practical question** in the KTU viva. | Wire Color | From | To | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | Red | Arduino 5V | Relay VCC (+) | Powers relay coil | | Black | Arduino GND | Relay GND (–) | Common reference | | Yellow | Arduino D7 | Resistor $R_b$ | Control signal | | Green | Resistor $R_b$ | BC547 Base | Current limiting | | Blue | BC547 Collector | Relay Coil (+) | Switched coil feed | | Grey | BC547 Emitter | Breadboard GND | Emitter return | | White (stripe) | Across Relay Coil | 1N4007 Cathode→Anode | Flyback protection | | Orange | Relay COM | 230 V AC Live | High-voltage feed | | Brown | Relay NO | Bulb Terminal | Switched AC output | # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]` **Why is a driver transistor required between an Arduino pin and a relay coil? What will happen if the relay is connected directly to the Arduino pin?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A driver transistor is required because the Arduino ATmega328P I/O pin can source only **40 mA (absolute max)** and **20 mA (recommended)**, while a 5 V relay coil typically draws **70 – 100 mA**. Connecting the relay directly to the pin would cause: 1. **Excessive current draw** → overheating of the pin driver MOSFET. 2. **Voltage sag** — the 5 V output collapses, causing logic instability. 3. **Permanent damage** to the microcontroller's I/O port within seconds. The **NPN transistor (BC547)** in common-emitter configuration acts as a current amplifier: a small base current (~1.4 mA) controls a large collector current (~70 mA), isolating and protecting the microcontroller while still energizing the coil. *(Award 1 mark for each valid consequence explained.)* --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary]` **What is the purpose of the flyback diode (1N4007) connected across the relay coil? Without it, what would happen to the driver transistor?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A relay coil is an **inductor** that stores energy in its magnetic field. When the transistor suddenly switches OFF, the magnetic flux collapses, and by **Faraday's / Lenz's law**, a large reverse **back-EMF** spike (often **> 100 V**) is induced across the coil: $$V_{L} = -L \cdot \frac{di}{dt}$$ This high-voltage negative spike appears across the transistor's **collector–base junction** and reverse-breaks it, permanently destroying the transistor (a phenomenon called **avalanche breakdown**). The **1N4007 flyback diode** is connected in **reverse-bias** across the coil. When the spike occurs, the diode becomes forward-biased and provides a **closed circulating path** for the inductor's stored energy, safely dissipating it as heat and clamping the spike to roughly **−0.7 V**. *(1 mark — purpose, 1 mark — spike equation/cause, 1 mark — diode mechanism.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 ESE pattern: **Module-level internal choice**. Two complete alternatives provided below. Either may be asked for the 14-mark slot. --- ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Main Slot]` **(a)** Draw the complete circuit diagram of a **5 V relay interfaced with an Arduino Uno** using a **BC547 driver transistor**, **1 kΩ base resistor**, and **1N4007 flyback diode**. Label all components and explain the role of each in detail. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Calculate the value of the base resistor $R_b$ required to drive a 5 V relay (coil current 70 mA) using a BC547 transistor ($\beta_{min} = 100$) from an Arduino 5 V pin. Show all derivation steps clearly. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution** **(a) — 7 Marks** A neat circuit diagram should include: - Arduino D7 → 1 kΩ $R_b$ → BC547 Base - BC547 Emitter → GND - BC547 Collector → Relay Coil terminal 1 - Relay Coil terminal 2 → +5 V - 1N4007 Diode — cathode at +5 V side, anode at collector side (reverse-biased during normal operation) - Indicator LED + 220 Ω on Arduino D8 - Relay COM, NO, NC terminals labelled **Role of each component (1 mark each for first 5, 2 marks for explanation of isolation):** 1. **Arduino** — Generates control logic (LOW/HIGH). 2. **Transistor** — Current amplifier; protects Arduino pin. 3. **Base Resistor** — Limits $I_b$ to safe value. 4. **Relay Coil** — Creates magnetic flux on energization. 5. **Flyback Diode** — Suppresses inductive kick. 6. **Isolation benefit** — The relay's contacts are **galvanically isolated** from the Arduino circuit, so the 230 V AC load side cannot damage the 5 V DC control side. **(2 marks — full credit for this point)** **(b) — 7 Marks (Full Derivation)** Given: $V_{CC} = 5 \, \text{V}$, $V_{BE} = 0.7 \, \text{V}$, $\beta_{min} = 100$, $I_c = 70 \, \text{mA}$, safety factor $k = 2$. $$ \begin{aligned} I_{b(min)} &= \frac{I_c}{\beta_{min}} = \frac{70 \, \text{mA}}{100} = 0.7 \, \text{mA} \\[4pt] I_{b(design)} &= k \cdot I_{b(min)} = 2 \times 0.7 \, \text{mA} = 1.4 \, \text{mA} \\[4pt] R_b &= \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{I_{b(design)}} = \frac{5 - 0.7}{1.4 \, \text{mA}} \\[4pt] R_b &= \frac{4.3}{1.4 \times 10^{-3}} = 3071.4 \, \Omega \\[4pt] \text{Standard value} \rightarrow R_b &\approx 2.7 \, \text{k}\Omega \quad \text{(or 1 k}\Omega \text{ for stronger overdrive)} \end{aligned} ``` (Marks: 1 for identifying given values, 1 for $I_b$ formula, 1 for $I_{b(min)}$ calculation, 1 for $I_{b(design)}$ with safety factor, 1 for KVL equation, 1 for final value, 1 for standard-value selection and justification.) --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary Slot]` **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **internal construction and working of an SPDT electromagnetic relay**. Define **NO, NC, and COM** terminals. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write an **Arduino sketch** to turn ON a relay connected to pin 7 for 2 seconds, then OFF for 2 seconds, **continuously**. Modify the same program to demonstrate **PWM-based speed control** of a DC motor through the same relay-driver interface. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution** **(a) — 7 Marks** Internal construction includes: - **Coil** wound on an iron core (creates magnetic flux when energized). - **Armature** — movable iron lever mechanically linked to the moving contact. - **Spring** — returns armature to default position when coil is de-energized. - **Contacts** — COM (Common), NO (Normally Open), NC (Normally Closed). - **Yoke / Frame** — completes the magnetic circuit. **Working explanation:** - When coil is **de-energized**, spring holds armature against **NC** contact — current flows between COM and NC. - When coil is **energized**, magnetic flux ($\Phi \propto N \cdot I$) pulls armature to **NO** contact — current now flows between COM and NO. **(b) — 7 Marks** Code logic and concepts: - `setup()` → `pinMode(7, OUTPUT);` - `loop()` → `digitalWrite(7, HIGH); delay(2000); digitalWrite(7, LOW); delay(2000);` - For PWM motor control — discuss that the relay can only **ON/OFF** (not true PWM speed control of the motor coil); real speed control requires an **H-bridge / motor driver IC (L293D)** in place of the relay, OR using an **opto-isolated SSR (Solid State Relay)** for AC loads. (Marks: 2 for ON/OFF code, 2 for timing logic, 3 for explaining limitation and proposing a correct alternative.) --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost 2–3 Marks Per Answer:** > > 1. **Diode polarity reversal** — Drawing the 1N4007 in the *forward* direction will short-circuit the 5 V supply as soon as the relay is energized, blowing the Arduino's regulator. Always cathode to **+V**, anode to **switched collector node**. > 2. **Forgetting the base resistor** — A naked base connected directly to 5 V will draw $\approx 70 \, \text{mA}$ into the base, destroying the transistor's BE junction. Always include $R_b$. > 3. **Confusing NO and NC** — NC = contact closed when coil is OFF (default). NO = contact closed only when coil is ON. Mixing these up reverses the entire logic of the program. > 4. **Missing the safety overdrive factor** — Designing at exactly $I_{b(min)}$ works only at ideal $\beta = 100$. A "weak" transistor with $\beta = 50$ will leave the relay half-energized (in the linear region), causing **overheating and unreliable operation**. Always apply $k \geq 2$. > 5. **Connecting 230 V AC directly to the breadboard** — Many students accidentally route AC through the low-voltage breadboard. Keep the high-voltage side on **separate screw terminals** and never touch with bare hands. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-density rapid-revision checklist — read this 5 minutes before the viva.** - ✅ Relay is an **electromechanically isolated switch** — the control side and load side share **no electrical connection**. - ✅ Arduino pin max current = **40 mA (absolute)** / **20 mA (recommended)**. - ✅ Always use a **driver transistor** (BC547, 2N2222, or a pre-built relay module with built-in transistor + optocoupler). - ✅ **Flyback diode (1N4007)** is non-negotiable — it is connected **reverse-biased** across the relay coil. - ✅ Base resistor formula: $R_b = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{I_b}$ where $I_b = k \cdot \dfrac{I_{coil}}{\beta_{min}}$ and $k \geq 2$. - ✅ Relay terminals: **COM** (Common input), **NO** (Normally Open — closes on energize), **NC** (Normally Closed — opens on energize). - ✅ The standard 5 V relay coil draws **~70 mA**; the contacts can typically switch **10 A @ 250 V AC**. - ✅ A **pull-down resistor (10 kΩ)** at the transistor base prevents false triggering during Arduino reset/bootloader phase. - ✅ **Optocoupler-based relay modules** add an extra layer of safety by providing **> 5 kV isolation** between Arduino and the load. - ✅ The relay module's onboard **JD-VCC** jumper can be removed to power the relay coil from a **separate 5 V supply**, achieving true galvanic isolation — a **bonus viva point**. - ✅ Common lab KTU kit: **1-channel SRD-05VDC-SL-C relay module** with screw terminals labelled **NO, COM, NC, VCC, GND, IN**. - ✅ Always specify the **peak inverse voltage (PIV ≥ 1000 V)** rating for the flyback diode to handle worst-case spikes. - ✅ The transistor's **collector must go to the coil**, *not* the emitter — this is the classic common-emitter switching configuration.

topics.title: Building Intrusion Detection System with Arduino and Ultrasonic Sensor
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL508 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND EMBEDDED SYSTEMS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Building Intrusion Detection System with Arduino and Ultrasonic Sensor

---

# Building Intrusion Detection System with Arduino and Ultrasonic Sensor

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

An **Intrusion Detection System (IDS)** built using an **Arduino microcontroller** and an **HC-SR04 Ultrasonic Distance Sensor** is an embedded security application that monitors a defined spatial perimeter (a guarded zone) and triggers an alarm the moment an unauthorized object, person, or vehicle crosses a pre-configured threshold distance. 

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** terminology, this experiment falls under **perception-actuation loop design**, where the Arduino acts as the **Embedded Processing Unit (EPU)**, the ultrasonic sensor functions as the **non-contact transducer**, and the output side typically consists of a **piezo buzzer**, **LED array**, or **LCD/Serial monitor** serving as the human-machine interface (HMI) alarm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment tests the Course Outcomes **CO1** (Implement microcontroller-based programs using IDE), **CO2** (Interface I/O peripherals), and **CO3** (Design real-time embedded applications).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a security guard standing in a dark room. Instead of using eyes (vision) which can be fooled in the dark, the guard uses **echolocation** — exactly like a **bat**. The bat (our ultrasonic sensor) screams a sound wave at a frequency too high for human ears. The wave hits an object and bounces back as an echo. The time taken for the echo to return tells the bat how far the object is.

In our experiment:
- The **Arduino** = the bat's brain (decision-maker).
- The **HC-SR04 sensor** = the bat's mouth and ears (transmitter + receiver).
- The **buzzer/LED** = the bat's warning shriek.
- The **threshold distance** = the bat's personal space boundary.

If anything comes closer than the boundary, the brain (Arduino) shouts "INTRUDER!" through the buzzer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Ultrasonic over PIR (Passive Infrared)?**
> PIR sensors only detect *motion* of *warm-blooded objects* within their cone. Ultrasonic sensors detect **any physical object** (metal, plastic, glass, wood) and provide an **actual distance measurement**, making them far more reliable for precise perimeter guarding.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How the System Works

The intrusion detection system operates in a closed-loop sequence with five distinct stages:

1. **Stage 1 — Trigger Pulse Generation**
   The Arduino digital I/O pin (configured as OUTPUT) sends a **10 microsecond (µs) HIGH pulse** to the `TRIG` pin of the HC-SR04. This triggers the sensor to emit an ultrasonic burst of **eight 40 kHz pulses**.

2. **Stage 2 — Ultrasonic Wave Propagation**
   The sound wave travels through air at the **speed of sound (≈ 343 m/s at 20 °C)** toward the target object.

3. **Stage 3 — Echo Reception**
   Upon striking an object, the wave reflects back. The sensor sets its `ECHO` pin HIGH for a duration exactly equal to the **round-trip time of flight (ToF)**.

4. **Stage 4 — Time Measurement**
   The Arduino uses the `pulseIn()` function to measure this HIGH duration in microseconds with microsecond precision.

5. **Stage 5 — Decision & Actuation**
   The Arduino converts the time-of-flight into a distance in centimetres using the standard speed-of-sound equation. If `distance < threshold`, the system activates the **alarm (buzzer + LED)**.

### 2.2 Core Physics — The Time-of-Flight Equation

The fundamental physics behind ultrasonic ranging is the **Doppler-free kinematic distance-time relation** for a wave reflecting off a stationary target:

$$\text{Round-trip distance} = v_{\text{sound}} \times t_{\text{echo}}$$

Because the wave travels **from sensor to object AND back**, the one-way distance is half of the round-trip:

$$d = \frac{v_{\text{sound}} \times t_{\text{echo}}}{2}$$

Where:
- $d$ = one-way distance to object (in metres)
- $v_{\text{sound}}$ = velocity of sound in air (in m/s)
- $t_{\text{echo}}$ = measured round-trip echo time (in seconds)

### 2.3 Engineering Conversion — Speed of Sound in cm/µs

Since Arduino's `pulseIn()` returns time in **microseconds (µs)** and we want distance in **centimetres (cm)**, we must convert $v_{\text{sound}}$ to compatible units:

$$v_{\text{sound}} = 343 \, \frac{\text{m}}{\text{s}} = 34300 \, \frac{\text{cm}}{\text{s}} = 0.0343 \, \frac{\text{cm}}{\text{µs}}$$

Substituting into the distance equation:

$$d_{\text{cm}} = \frac{0.0343 \times t_{\text{µs}}}{2} = \frac{t_{\text{µs}}}{58.2}$$

This is why the magic constant **58.2** (or 58, rounded) appears in nearly every Arduino ultrasonic sketch.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbol | Value / Expression | Unit | Boundary / Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Speed of sound at 20 °C | $v_s$ | **343** | m/s | Decreases ~0.6 m/s per °C drop |
| 2 | Speed of sound (cm/µs) | $v_s$ | **0.0343** | cm/µs | Used in Arduino code |
| 3 | One-way distance | $d$ | $\frac{v_s \times t_{\text{echo}}}{2}$ | metres | Core ranging formula |
| 4 | Arduino distance constant | — | $\frac{t_{\text{µs}}}{58.2}$ | cm | Magic number for `pulseIn()` |
| 5 | HC-SR04 supply voltage | $V_{CC}$ | **5V DC** | Volts | Must NOT be 3.3V |
| 6 | HC-SR04 operating current | $I$ | **15 mA** | mA | Idle: 2 mA |
| 7 | Ultrasonic frequency | $f$ | **40** | kHz | Above human hearing (>20 kHz) |
| 8 | Minimum detection range | $d_{\min}$ | **2** | cm | Hardware blind zone |
| 9 | Maximum detection range | $d_{\max}$ | **400** | cm | ≈ 13 feet |
| 10 | Measurement accuracy | $\pm$ | **0.3** | cm | At stable temperature |
| 11 | Beam angle | $\theta$ | **15°** | degrees | Cone of detection |
| 12 | Echo pulse HIGH duration | $t_{\text{echo}}$ | 116 to 23200 | µs | Corresponds to 2–400 cm |
| 13 | Trigger pulse width | $t_{\text{TRIG}}$ | **10** | µs | Minimum to initiate burst |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 15° beam angle matters for KTU viva:** A 15° cone means at a distance of 2 metres, the sensor "sees" a circular spot of diameter ≈ 53 cm. The examiner may ask: *"Will your sensor detect a thin rod?"* — Answer: Only if it falls within the cone. Always design the threshold with margin.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Distance Formula

**Given:** An HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor emits a 40 kHz burst at time $t_0$. The wave travels through air, reflects off a stationary object, and returns to the sensor. The `ECHO` pin remains HIGH for a duration $t_{\text{echo}}$.

**Step 1 — Establish the round-trip kinematic relation**

For a wave moving at constant speed, distance equals speed multiplied by time. The total path length covered by the wave is the round-trip path (sensor → object → sensor):

$$L_{\text{round}} = v_s \times t_{\text{echo}}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to one-way distance**

Because the wave covers the gap between sensor and object **twice** (once forward, once backward), the actual distance to the object is half of the round-trip length:

$$d = \frac{L_{\text{round}}}{2} = \frac{v_s \times t_{\text{echo}}}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the speed of sound in SI units**

The speed of sound in dry air at 20 °C is a well-established physical constant:

$$v_s = 343 \, \frac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}$$

Substituting:

$$d = \frac{343 \times t_{\text{echo}}}{2} \quad \text{[metres]}$$

**Step 4 — Convert seconds to microseconds**

Arduino's `pulseIn()` measures echo duration in microseconds. Replace $t_{\text{echo}}$ (seconds) with $t_{\text{echo}}$ (µs), where $1 \, \text{s} = 10^6 \, \text{µs}$:

$$t_{\text{echo}}\,[\text{s}] = \frac{t_{\text{echo}}\,[\text{µs}]}{1{,}000{,}000}$$

Therefore:

$$d = \frac{343}{2} \times \frac{t_{\text{µs}}}{10^6} \quad \text{[metres]}$$

**Step 5 — Convert metres to centimetres**

Multiplying by 100 to convert metres to centimetres:

$$d = \frac{343 \times 100}{2 \times 10^6} \times t_{\text{µs}} \quad \text{[cm]}$$

**Step 6 — Simplify the constant**

$$\frac{343 \times 100}{2 \times 10^6} = \frac{34{,}300}{2{,}000{,}000} = \frac{1}{58.309}$$

Therefore, the simplified formula becomes:

$$d_{\text{cm}} = \frac{t_{\text{µs}}}{58.3}$$

**Step 7 — Engineering rounding**

For Arduino integer arithmetic, the constant is rounded to **58** or **58.2** for sub-cm accuracy. Many libraries use:

```cpp
distance_cm = duration / 58.0;
distance_inch = duration / 148.0;   // for inches
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** If asked to derive the magic constant, the examiner expects the full SI-to-µs-to-cm chain shown above. Skipping Step 4 or Step 5 costs full marks.

### 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — When Does the Alarm Trigger?

| Measured Distance $d$ (cm) | Relation to Threshold $T$ | Buzzer State | LED State | Serial Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $d \geq T + 20$ | Far zone (safe) | OFF | OFF (green) | `"ZONE CLEAR"` |
| $T < d < T + 20$ | Caution zone | OFF | BLINK (yellow) | `"APPROACHING"` |
| $d \leq T$ | **Intrusion zone** | **ON** (continuous tone) | **ON (red solid)** | `"!!! INTRUSION !!!"` |

A typical threshold $T$ for an indoor demo = **30 cm**. For an outdoor perimeter, $T$ may be set to **100–200 cm** based on the beam-cone geometry.

### 3.3 Timing Budget Per Loop Iteration

| Operation | Time Cost |
|---|---|
| `digitalWrite(TRIG, HIGH)` + 10 µs delay | **12 µs** |
| `pulseIn(ECHO, HIGH, timeout)` | up to **23,000 µs** (≈ 23 ms worst-case at 4 m) |
| Threshold comparison & alarm | **< 5 µs** |
| Serial print (at 9600 baud) | ~1 ms per print |
| **Total per iteration** | **~25–30 ms** |

Hence the maximum sampling rate is roughly **40 Hz** — more than sufficient for human/object intrusion (typical walking speed ≈ 1.4 m/s).

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 System-Level Block Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **functional block architecture** of the intrusion detection system. Each block represents a hardware or software module and the arrows show the direction of signal/data flow.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POWER["POWER SUPPLY UNIT"]
        PSU[5V DC REGULATED ADAPTER]
    end

    subgraph SENSOR["PERCEPTION LAYER"]
        HCSR[HC SR04 ULTRASONIC SENSOR]
        TRIG1[TRIG PIN]
        ECHO1[ECHO PIN]
    end

    subgraph MCU["EMBEDDED PROCESSING UNIT"]
        ARD[ARDUINO UNO ATMEGA328P]
        PIN9[DIGITAL PIN 9 OUTPUT]
        PIN10[DIGITAL PIN 10 INPUT]
        PIN8[DIGITAL PIN 8 OUTPUT]
        PIN7[DIGITAL PIN 7 OUTPUT]
    end

    subgraph ACTUATOR["ACTUATION LAYER"]
        BUZZ[PIEZO BUZZER]
        LEDR[RED LED INTRUSION]
        LEDG[GREEN LED SAFE]
    end

    subgraph HMI["HUMAN MACHINE INTERFACE"]
        SER[SERIAL MONITOR 9600 BAUD]
        LCD[16X2 LCD OPTIONAL]
    end

    PSU -->|5V AND GND| HCSR
    PSU -->|5V AND GND| ARD
    PSU -->|5V AND GND| BUZZ
    PSU -->|5V AND GND| LEDR
    PSU -->|5V AND GND| LEDG

    ARD -->|10 US PULSE| PIN9
    PIN9 --> TRIG1
    HCSR --> ECHO1
    ECHO1 -->|ECHO PULSE WIDTH| PIN10
    ARD -->|HIGH ON ALARM| PIN8
    PIN8 --> BUZZ
    ARD -->|HIGH ON ALARM| PIN7
    PIN7 --> LEDR
    ARD --> SER
    ARD --> LCD
```

### 4.2 Software Control Flow (Program Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([PROGRAM START]) --> SETUP[SETUP: CONFIGURE PIN MODES AND BEGIN SERIAL]
    SETUP --> LOOP([LOOP FUNCTION BEGINS])
    LOOP --> TRIGSTEP[SET TRIG PIN LOW FOR 2 US]
    TRIGSTEP --> TRIGPULSE[SET TRIG PIN HIGH FOR 10 US]
    TRIGPULSE --> TRIGLOW[SET TRIG PIN LOW]
    TRIGLOW --> READECHO[READ ECHO PULSE WIDTH VIA PULSEIN]
    READECHO --> CONVERT[COMPUTE DISTANCE: D = DURATION / 58]
    CONVERT --> DECIDE{DISTANCE LESS THAN THRESHOLD}
    DECIDE -->|YES| ALARMON[TURN ON BUZZER AND RED LED]
    DECIDE -->|NO| ALARMOFF[TURN OFF BUZZER AND RED LED]
    ALARMON --> PRINT[PRINT STATUS TO SERIAL MONITOR]
    ALARMOFF --> PRINT
    PRINT --> DELAY[WAIT 100 MS]
    DELAY --> LOOP
```

### 4.3 Hardware Pin Mapping Table

| Arduino Uno Pin | Connected To | Mode | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **VCC (5V)** | HC-SR04 VCC, Buzzer VCC, LEDs anode via 220Ω | Power | Powers peripherals |
| **GND** | All GND pins | Power | Common ground reference |
| **D9** | HC-SR04 TRIG | OUTPUT | Sends 10 µs trigger pulse |
| **D10** | HC-SR04 ECHO | INPUT | Receives echo pulse width |
| **D7** | Red LED + 220 Ω resistor | OUTPUT | Intrusion indicator |
| **D8** | Piezo Buzzer | OUTPUT | Audible alarm tone |
| **D6** *(optional)* | Green LED + 220 Ω resistor | OUTPUT | Safe-zone indicator |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Voltage Divider Warning for ECHO Pin:** Although HC-SR04's ECHO pin outputs 5V HIGH and Arduino Uno's digital inputs are 5V tolerant, **on Arduino boards operating at 3.3V (e.g., Due, ESP32)**, you MUST place a **voltage divider (1 kΩ + 2 kΩ)** on the ECHO line to drop it to ≈ 3.3V. Failing to do this can permanently damage the MCU.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Exam Paper]**
*Explain the working principle of the HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor. How is distance computed from the echo pulse?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The HC-SR04 has two ultrasonic transducers — a transmitter and a receiver. When the TRIG pin receives a **10 µs HIGH pulse** from the Arduino, the transmitter emits **eight 40 kHz bursts**. These bursts travel through air, reflect off an object, and are picked up by the receiver, which then drives the ECHO pin HIGH for a duration equal to the round-trip time. The Arduino reads this duration using `pulseIn()` and converts it to distance using the formula $d = t_{\text{echo}} / 58.0$, where 58 is the combined conversion constant derived from the speed of sound (343 m/s) and unit conversions. *(3 marks — 1 for principle, 1 for formula, 1 for constant derivation.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
*What are the limitations of using an ultrasonic sensor for intrusion detection?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Narrow 15° beam cone** — thin objects outside the cone are missed. *(1 mark)*
2. **Soft/absorbent surfaces** (cloth, foam) absorb ultrasound and produce weak or no echoes. *(1 mark)*
3. **Temperature dependence** — speed of sound varies ≈ 0.6 m/s per °C, causing drift in calculated distance if not compensated. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU Rule:** *Answer any ONE full question from each module. Each carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main]**

**(a)** Design and implement an Arduino-based intrusion detection system using an HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor. Draw the circuit diagram, list the components, and write the complete Arduino program with proper comments. Mention the threshold distance logic. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Components Required:**
- Arduino Uno (1)
- HC-SR04 sensor (1)
- Piezo buzzer (1)
- Red LED (1), Green LED (1)
- 220 Ω resistors (2)
- Breadboard and jumper wires
- USB cable for programming

**Circuit Connections:**
- HC-SR04 VCC → 5V, GND → GND
- HC-SR04 TRIG → D9, ECHO → D10
- Buzzer + → D8 (via 100 Ω), – → GND
- Red LED anode → D7 (via 220 Ω), cathode → GND
- Green LED anode → D6 (via 220 Ω), cathode → GND

**Arduino Program:**

```cpp
/*
 * KTU LAB: Intrusion Detection System
 * Course: PCEOL508 | Module Experiment 2
 */
#define TRIG  9
#define ECHO  10
#define BUZZ  8
#define LEDR  7
#define LEDG  6

int threshold = 30;   // intrusion threshold in cm

void setup() {
  pinMode(TRIG, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(ECHO, INPUT);
  pinMode(BUZZ, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(LEDR, OUTPUT);
  pinMode(LEDG, OUTPUT);
  Serial.begin(9600);
  Serial.println("INTRUSION DETECTION SYSTEM ONLINE");
}

void loop() {
  long duration;
  float distance;

  // Send 10 us trigger pulse
  digitalWrite(TRIG, LOW);
  delayMicroseconds(2);
  digitalWrite(TRIG, HIGH);
  delayMicroseconds(10);
  digitalWrite(TRIG, LOW);

  // Read echo pulse width
  duration = pulseIn(ECHO, HIGH, 25000);

  // Convert to distance (cm)
  distance = duration * 0.0343 / 2.0;

  Serial.print("Distance: ");
  Serial.print(distance);
  Serial.println(" cm");

  // Decision logic
  if (distance > 0 && distance <= threshold) {
    digitalWrite(BUZZ, HIGH);
    digitalWrite(LEDR, HIGH);
    digitalWrite(LEDG, LOW);
    Serial.println("!!! INTRUSION DETECTED !!!");
  } else {
    digitalWrite(BUZZ, LOW);
    digitalWrite(LEDR, LOW);
    digitalWrite(LEDG, HIGH);
    Serial.println("Zone Clear");
  }

  delay(100);   // 100 ms sampling interval
}
```

**Threshold Logic:** When `distance <= 30 cm`, system classifies object as *intruder* and activates buzzer + red LED. Otherwise the green LED remains ON indicating a safe zone. *(7 marks — 2 for circuit, 2 for code structure, 2 for logic, 1 for serial output)*

**(b)** Derive the constant 58.2 used in the ultrasonic distance formula `distance = duration / 58.2`. Show all unit conversions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Speed of sound: $v_s = 343$ m/s at 20 °C.

Step 1 — Round-trip distance: $L = v_s \times t$

Step 2 — One-way distance: $d = v_s \times t / 2$

Step 3 — Convert metres to cm: multiply by 100
$$d = \frac{343 \times t}{2} \times 100 = \frac{34{,}300 \times t}{2}$$

Step 4 — Convert seconds to microseconds: $t$ (s) $= t_{\text{µs}} / 10^6$
$$d = \frac{34{,}300}{2 \times 10^6} \times t_{\text{µs}}$$

Step 5 — Simplify:
$$d = \frac{34{,}300}{2{,}000{,}000} \times t_{\text{µs}} = \frac{1}{58.309} \times t_{\text{µs}}$$

Step 6 — Final form: $d_{\text{cm}} = t_{\text{µs}} / 58.3$

The constant 58.2 ≈ 58.3 results from rounding the speed of sound to 343 m/s and combining the unit conversions. *(7 marks — 1 each for steps 1–6 + 1 for final form)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an Arduino-based ultrasonic intrusion detection system. Describe the role of each block in the perception-processing-actuation loop. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The system consists of **four functional blocks**:

1. **Sensor Block (HC-SR04):** Acts as the perception unit. It transduces electrical energy into mechanical ultrasonic waves (40 kHz) and back. Provides a digital time-domain output proportional to distance.
2. **Processing Block (Arduino Uno / ATmega328P):** A RISC-based 8-bit microcontroller running at 16 MHz. It executes the embedded C code, performs the time-to-distance conversion using the `pulseIn()` function, and runs the threshold comparison logic in real time.
3. **Actuation Block (Buzzer + LEDs):** Converts the logical decision into a physical alarm — a 2–5 kHz audible tone via the piezo buzzer and a visual warning via the red LED.
4. **HMI Block (Serial Monitor / LCD):** Provides human-readable telemetry — real-time distance in cm and alarm status text — for debugging and operator awareness.

The arrows in the block diagram show a closed feedback loop: sensor → MCU → actuator, with the MCU also feeding the HMI. *(7 marks — 1 per block + 1 for feedback concept + 1 for closed-loop diagram description)*

**(b)** A security system uses an HC-SR04 sensor. The Arduino measures an echo pulse width of **4700 µs**. Calculate the distance to the intruder. If the threshold is 40 cm, will the alarm trigger? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Given: $t_{\text{echo}} = 4700 \, \mu s$, threshold $T = 40 \, \text{cm}$.

Using the formula:
$$d = \frac{t_{\text{µs}}}{58.2} = \frac{4700}{58.2}$$

$$d = 80.756 \, \text{cm}$$

Comparison with threshold:
$$d = 80.76 \, \text{cm} > T = 40 \, \text{cm}$$

**Conclusion:** The object is **80.76 cm** away, which is **greater** than the threshold. **The alarm will NOT trigger.** The zone is currently *clear*.

*(7 marks — 2 for formula, 2 for substitution, 2 for numerical result, 1 for decision logic)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Lab Records & Viva:**
> 1. **Wrong constant in code** — Students often write `duration / 29` instead of `/ 58` (forgetting that 58 already accounts for the round-trip division by 2). This makes distance appear double the actual value.
> 2. **Missing `delayMicroseconds(2)` before TRIG HIGH** — HC-SR04 datasheet requires a clean LOW-then-HIGH edge. Skipping this causes erratic readings.
> 3. **No timeout in `pulseIn()`** — Writing `pulseIn(ECHO, HIGH)` without the third timeout argument can lock the loop for **up to 1 second** if no echo is received. Always use `pulseIn(ECHO, HIGH, 25000)`.
> 4. **Forgot 220 Ω current-limiting resistor on LED** — Direct LED-to-pin burns out the LED and may damage the MCU. KTU record-checking penalises this.
> 5. **Confusing 40 kHz acoustic frequency with 40 kHz pin number** — In viva, students get nervous when asked *"Why 40 kHz?"* — Answer: Above human hearing (>20 kHz), and resonantly optimal for cheap piezo transducers.
> 6. **Not commenting the code** — KTU record valuation explicitly rewards commented code; raw `.ino` files lose 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **HC-SR04** is a 4-pin ultrasonic ranging module: **VCC, GND, TRIG, ECHO**. Operating voltage is **5V DC**.
- [ ] Trigger requires a **10 µs HIGH pulse**; ECHO returns a HIGH pulse whose **width equals the round-trip ToF**.
- [ ] The magic conversion constant is **58.2** (or 58) — derived from $v_s = 343$ m/s and unit conversions (m → cm → µs).
- [ ] Detection range: **2 cm to 400 cm** (≈ 13 ft). Hardware blind zone is 0–2 cm.
- [ ] Beam cone is **15°** — narrow cone limits wide-area detection.
- [ ] **Arduino function `pulseIn(pin, HIGH, timeout)`** reads the pulse width in microseconds. Always include a timeout (e.g., 25000 µs).
- [ ] **Speed of sound depends on temperature:** $v_s = 331 + 0.6T \, \text{(°C)}$. For 25 °C, use **346 m/s**.
- [ ] Use a **voltage divider (1kΩ + 2kΩ)** on the ECHO line for **3.3V Arduino boards** (Due, ESP32, MKR series).
- [ ] Intrusion threshold is set in code (typically **30 cm** for indoor demos). Lower threshold = more sensitive but more false alarms.
- [ ] Always include a **220 Ω current-limiting resistor** for each LED to prevent burnout.
- [ ] The buzzer is driven via a **transistor (e.g., BC547)** if the alarm must be loud (>100 mA); direct Arduino drive is limited to ≈ 20 mA.
- [ ] For better accuracy, take **3 readings and average** them — eliminates jitter from air turbulence.
- [ ] For lab record: include the **derivation of 58**, the **circuit diagram**, the **commented code**, and the **output screenshot** of the Serial Monitor at multiple distances.
